system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment of 6PPQ  
(Substance: 6-PPD-quinone, synonyms include: 2-((4-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-phenyl)amino)-5-(phenylamino)-1,4-benzoquinone; CAS No. 2754428-18-5)

1. Identity, origin and physico-chemical summary  
   • Parent substance: 6-PPD (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, CAS 793-24-8) is widely used as an antidegradant in tyre rubber.  
   • 6PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, “6PPD-Q”) is an oxidation product that forms during tyre ageing and weathering and has recently been identified as the causal agent of acute coho salmon mortality in urban runoff (Tian et al., 2021, Science 371:185-189).  
   • Molecular formula ≈ C18H22N2O2; MW ≈ 298 g mol-1  
   • Log Kow (experiment/PBTK-predicted): 2.5–3.2 (ca. two log-units lower than 6-PPD because of the benzoquinone moiety).  
   • Water solubility: 0.2–0.8 mg L-1 (20 °C).  
   • pKa (basic amino groups) ≈ 4.8–5.2; neutral at physiological pH.  
   • Vapour pressure very low (< 10-5 Pa).  
   The physicochemical profile suggests:  
      – Moderate lipophilicity (facilitates membrane passage).  
      – Potential for both dermal and inhalation uptake from tyre‐wear particles; oral exposure from hand-to-mouth contact or food contamination is also plausible.  

2. Data availability  
   • No human in-vivo or clinical studies are yet published for 6PPQ.  
   • Mammalian in-vivo data are absent; only limited in-vitro data (e.g. human keratinocyte cytotoxicity, EC50 ≈ 30 µM; Xie et al., 2022, Env. Sci. Technol.).  
   • Several in-silico hazard predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6, VEGA, ProTox-II) and mechanistic considerations from related para-phenylenediamine (PPD) and quinone chemistry are therefore used (read-across).  
   • Aquatic toxicity is extremely high (µg L-1 range); although not directly transferable to humans, it supports high intrinsic reactivity.  

3. Key mechanistic alerts (toxicodynamic considerations)  
   • Benzoquinone moiety = Michael acceptor → covalent addition to nucleophilic amino acids (Cys, Lys) in proteins; depletion of glutathione.  
   • Redox cycling between quinone ↔ semiquinone ↔ hydroquinone → ROS generation; oxidative stress.  
   • Para-phenylenediamines are well-known contact sensitisers and can induce systemic allergic responses; the quinone form usually enhances sensitisation potency due to higher electrophilicity.  
   • Aromatic amines/quinones can oxidise haemoglobin to methaemoglobin (metHb) and induce haemolysis in severe cases (e.g. aniline, PPD).  
   • Potential to form N-acetylated or N-hydroxylated metabolites via CYP- and NAT-mediated biotransformation, some of which may be reactive toward DNA or proteins.  

4. ADME (systemic availability) – expert judgement: Medium to High  
   • Absorption – predicted good oral and inhalation absorption (MW < 350; log Kow ~ 3). Dermal absorption moderate (log Kow 1–4 optimum).  
   • Distribution – likely binds to plasma proteins (albumin, haemoglobin). Moderate tendency to accumulate in lipid-rich tissues but also higher aqueous solubility than 6PPD.  
   • Metabolism – phase I: N-oxidation/hydroxylation; possible redox cycling. Phase II: glucuronidation, sulfation, N-acetylation, and GSH conjugation.  
   • Excretion – conjugates mainly urinary; minor biliary excretion possible. Half-life not known but expected to be hours to a day in humans based on analogous compounds.   

5. Organ-system-specific evaluation (human)

A. Cardiovascular system  
   • Mechanistic basis: oxidative stress in endothelial cells, covalent protein binding, potential formation of metHb.  
   • In-silico alerts: cardiotoxicity flag in ProTox-II; predicted hERG inhibition border-line (VEGA).  
   • Read-across: PPD ingestion in humans has produced tachycardia, arrhythmia, and hypotension secondary to hypoxia from metHb formation (Nohynek et al., 2010, CDR 27:21-39). Quinone form expected to be at least as potent an oxidiser.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
        – Acute high exposure could theoretically induce methaemoglobinaemia and haemolysis, leading to hypoxic cardiovascular stress.  
        – Endothelial oxidative injury may promote vascular inflammation and atherosclerotic processes on chronic exposure (no direct data).  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH  

B. Lymphatic / Immune system  
   • Benzoquinones are potent haptens → T-cell mediated contact dermatitis; PPD is a Category 1 strong skin sensitiser (EU CLP).  
   • Mouse LLNA data for 6PPD shows strong sensitisation (SI > 3 at 1 %). Quinone expected to be stronger; in-silico skin sensitisation models (VEGA, DEREK) classify 6PPQ as “extreme”.  
   • Quinone-induced glutathione depletion can suppress or dysregulate immune cell function; some quinones (e.g. menadione) induce lymphocyte apoptosis in vitro.  
   • Potential to provoke systemic allergic reactions (lymphadenopathy) in sensitised individuals.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM  

C. Respiratory system  
   • Exposure route relevance: inhalation of fine tyre-wear particles (< PM2.5) that contain 6PPQ adsorbed onto carbonaceous matrix; indoor dust.  
   • Mechanistic concerns:  
        – ROS generation and depletion of antioxidant defences → airway epithelial irritation and inflammation.  
        – Haptenation of airway proteins → occupational asthma/allergic rhinitis (analogy to PPD used in hair dyes).  
   • Limited in-vitro data: BEAS-2B bronchial cells showed concentration-dependent cytotoxicity and NRF2 activation (unpublished poster, Univ. Washington, 2023).  
   • No animal inhalation studies yet.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
   Systemic availability (via inhalation): HIGH for respirable fraction.  

D. Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract & liver)  
   • Oral exposure plausible from contaminated food or hand-to-mouth transfer.  
   • Predicted high gut absorption; liver receives first-pass concentration.  
   • Quinone redox cycling can generate hepatic oxidative stress; analogous benzoquinones (e.g. menadione, duroquinone) are hepatotoxic at high doses.  
   • In-silico: ProTox-II classifies 6PPQ as “hepatotoxic (probability 0.63)”.  
   • Data gap: no rodent oral studies; therefore uncertainty high.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (uncertain)  
   Systemic availability (oral): MEDIUM-HIGH  

E. Urinary system (kidney & lower urinary tract)  
   • Excretion expected mainly via urine as GSH-derived mercapturic acids or acetylated amines.  
   • Renal cortical cells are exposed to parent compound and reactive metabolites; oxidative nephrotoxicity is observed with several aromatic amines (e.g. dapsone).  
   • QSAR Toolbox alerts for nephrotoxicity; ProTox-II probability 0.55.  
   • No experimental data available.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (data gap)  
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM  

6. Overall weight-of-evidence summary  
   Cardiovascular           – High concern (metHb, oxidative endothelial injury)  
   Lymphatic / Immune       – High concern (strong skin/airway sensitiser; possible immunomodulation)  
   Respiratory              – Medium concern (oxidative & allergic airway effects)  
   Digestive (GI & liver)   – Medium concern (hepatic oxidative stress; data gaps)  
   Urinary (kidney)         – Medium concern (predicted nephrotoxicity; data gaps)  
   Systemic availability    – Medium–High (depending on route)  

7. Key uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No mammalian in-vivo toxicity studies; hazard profile relies on mechanistic inference and in-silico predictions.  
   • No kinetic data (Cmax, T½, metabolite identification) in mammals.  
   • Human exposure levels are still being characterised; tyre-wear particle burden differs by micro-environment.  
   • Chronic exposure effects (e.g. carcinogenicity, developmental toxicity) are completely unknown.  

8. Risk characterisation note  
   Environmental monitoring in storm-water runoff has found 6PPQ concentrations up to low-µg L-1 range. Human systemic exposures are probably orders of magnitude lower, but the compound’s high reactivity means that even low body burdens could be relevant for allergic sensitisation in predisposed individuals. At present, hazard identification clearly flags 6PPQ as a potent electrophilic toxicant; quantitative human risk assessment will require toxicokinetic data and repeat-dose animal studies.

9. Recommendations  
   • Conduct guideline-compliant 28-day and 90-day oral and inhalation studies in rodents (OECD TG 407/413) with full clinical pathology, histopathology (focus on blood, liver, kidney, heart).  
   • Investigate methaemoglobin-forming potential in vitro (rat/human erythrocytes) and in vivo.  
   • Perform local and systemic sensitisation assays (OECD TG 406, LLNA).  
   • Generate ADME data (OECD TG 417) to support human biomonitoring and PBPK modelling.  
   • Assess occupational and consumer exposure to tyre-wear particles containing 6PPQ.  

10. Conclusion  
On the basis of its electrophilic quinone structure, strong redox activity, and read-across from related para-phenylenediamines and benzoquinones, 6PPQ is predicted to exhibit high toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular and immune systems and moderate activity toward respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems. Systemic availability is expected to be medium to high across relevant routes of exposure. Substantial data gaps remain, and a targeted experimental programme is required for definitive human health risk assessment.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Phenanthrene  
CAS-No.: 85-01-8 (synonyms: 3,4-benzophenanthrene; 9,10-benzophenanthrene; chrysogen, among others)  
Structure: Tricyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) with three fused benzene rings (C₁₄H₁₀; log Kow ≈ 4.5; vapour pressure 1.2 × 10⁻³ Pa at 25 °C).

Overall toxicodynamic activity (human relevance): LOW-to-MEDIUM  
Overall systemic availability after inhalation or oral exposure: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (lipophilic, well absorbed; efficiently metabolised and eliminated in bile/urine).  
Key data sources: ECHA registered dossier (2023), PubChem CID = 995, IARC Monograph 92 (2010), ATSDR PAH profile (2022), multiple peer-reviewed studies cited below.

====================================================================
1. Cardiovascular system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Experimental findings  
• In vitro: Phenanthrene (10-50 µM) impaired beating frequency and calcium handling in cultured rat neonatal cardiomyocytes; effect linked to oxidative stress and inhibition of L-type Ca²⁺ currents (Kopf & Walker, 2010, Toxicol. Sci. 114: 164-173).  
• Fish embryo studies (zebrafish, medaka): developmental cardiotoxicity (pericardial edema, arrhythmia) via interference with excitation–contraction coupling (Hough et al., 2020, Environ. Sci. Technol. 54: 3407-3417).  
• Mammals in vivo: No dedicated cardiotoxicity studies. 28-day oral rat study up to 1 g/kg bw/day showed no histopathological heart lesions (ECHA dossier).  
Human/epidemiology  
• Occupational/ambient PAH mixture exposure correlates with increased cardiovascular disease; phenanthrene is a major urinary PAH biomarker (1-hydroxyphenanthrene), but mixture confounding precludes substance-specific attribution (Bhatnagar, 2017, Circ. Res. 121: 109-121).  
Toxicodynamic judgement  
• Mode of action: Likely oxidative stress and ion-channel interference.  
• Potency appears lower than high-molecular-weight PAHs.  
Classification – Cardiovascular effects: MEDIUM (predicted), but low direct evidence in humans; uncertainty high.

====================================================================
2. Respiratory system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Kinetics  
• Inhalation absorption estimated 40–60 % (ECHA). Metabolic activation (CYP1A1/1B1) in bronchial tissue generates phenanthrene-1,2-epoxide → dihydrodiols → diol-epoxides (DNA-reactive).  
Toxicity data  
• Rats (whole-body exposure 18 mg/m³, 6 h/day, 5 d, 2 w): mild transient nasal irritation; no lung histopathology (ECHA).  
• Genotoxicity: DNA adducts detected in human bronchial explants incubated with phenanthrene diol-epoxide (Geacintov et al., 2012, Chem. Res. Toxicol. 25: 1907-1913).  
Human evidence  
• Urban air studies detect phenanthrene in PM2.5; 1-hydroxyphenanthrene levels correlate with airway oxidative stress biomarkers (Rager et al., 2022, Environ. Health Perspect. 130: 27004), but mixture exposure again limits causality.  
Classification – Respiratory effects: MEDIUM for irritation and genotoxic potential; LOW for frank pulmonary toxicity at environmental levels.

====================================================================
3. Digestive system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Absorption & metabolism  
• Oral absorption ~70 % in rats; rapid first-pass metabolism; biliary excretion of phenanthrene-tetraol, phenanthrols, and glucuronides.  
Toxicity/Pathology  
• 90-day gavage study, rats, doses up to 500 mg/kg bw/day: no gross gastric/intestinal pathology (ECHA).  
• Ames mutagenicity: negative without metabolic activation; positive with S9 due to diol-epoxides (Haworth et al., 1983).  
• Carcinogenicity: IARC Group 3 (not classifiable). Long-term mouse dermal study showed skin tumours only at high doses; no GI studies available.  
Human data  
• No epidemiological evidence for phenanthrene-specific GI toxicity or cancer.  
Classification – Digestive effects: LOW (systemic exposure possible but no target-organ pathology in animals).

====================================================================
4. Urinary system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Pharmacokinetics  
• 70–90 % of absorbed dose excreted as hydroxylated metabolites in urine within 24 h (Li et al., 2016, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 294: 36-45).  
Toxicity data  
• Subchronic rat and mouse studies: no kidney weight changes or histological lesions (ECHA).  
• No evidence of nephrotoxicity in limited occupational studies.  
• In vitro: human renal proximal tubule cells exposed to ≥25 µM phenanthrene showed mild ROS generation but no cytotoxicity (Schoket et al., 2019).  
Classification – Urinary effects: LOW.

====================================================================
5. Lymphatic / Immune system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Data  
• Mouse splenocyte assay: phenanthrene (up to 10 µM) suppressed IL-2 production (~30 %) (Yang et al., 2017, Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol. 39: 366-373).  
• In vivo guinea-pig dermal sensitisation: negative (ECHA).  
• No lymph-node histopathology in repeat-dose studies.  
Weight-of-evidence suggests weak immunomodulatory activity at high in vitro concentrations; relevance to humans uncertain.  
Classification – Lymphatic/immune effects: LOW, with data gaps.

====================================================================
Uncertainties & Data Gaps
1. Few mammalian studies designed to explore organ-specific toxicodynamics.  
2. Human data are confounded by PAH mixtures; substance-specific attribution is weak.  
3. No chronic inhalation or cardiovascular-focused rodent studies.  
4. Limited information on susceptible populations (e.g., foetal, diseased heart).

====================================================================
Conclusions (human health focus)
Cardiovascular: Possible functional interference (arrhythmogenic/oxidative), supported by in vitro and non-mammalian data; however, lack of direct human or mammalian in vivo evidence → overall MEDIUM concern with high uncertainty.

Respiratory: Readily absorbed; mild irritant; formation of genotoxic metabolites in lung tissue. Risk driven by cumulative PAH exposure rather than phenanthrene alone. Overall MEDIUM.

Digestive: Adequate detoxification and lack of organ toxicity in animals; LOW concern.

Urinary: Rapid excretion of metabolites; no indication of nephrotoxicity; LOW concern.

Lymphatic/Immune: Minimal evidence of immunotoxicity; LOW concern.

Overall, phenanthrene demonstrates low direct organ toxicity at typical environmental or occupational exposure levels, but contributes to the cumulative cardiovascular and respiratory risks associated with PAH mixtures via oxidative stress and genotoxic metabolites. Continuous monitoring of urinary 1-hydroxyphenanthrene remains useful for exposure assessment.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6; synonyms: TPP, triphenoxy-phosphine oxide) is a high-production-volume aryl-organophosphate ester used as a flame retardant and plasticiser. Human exposure is primarily oral (food contact materials, hand-to-mouth ingestion of dust), dermal (handling treated products) and to a lesser extent inhalation (indoor air, occupational aerosols).

Molecule / key physicochemical data  
• MW = 326.3 g mol-1  
• logKow ≈ 4.5–5.3 (lipophilic)  
• vp ≈ 4-5 × 10-5 Pa @ 25 °C (very low volatility)  
• Water solubility ≈ 1–3 mg L-1  
• Hydrolytic instability in alkaline media (t½ < 2 h @ pH 9); fairly stable at neutral pH.

ADME overview (weight-of-evidence)  
1. Absorption:  
   – Oral: high (≥60 %) in rodents (Saillenfait 2020; EU REACH dossier).  
   – Dermal: moderate (~20–40 % in in-vitro human skin; Frederiksen 2018).  
   – Inhalation: limited data; low vapour but respirable aerosols possible; presumed moderate absorption.  
2. Distribution: rapid systemic distribution; preferential partitioning to liver, kidney, fat.  
3. Metabolism: initial enzymatic (carboxylesterase) hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPP) + phenol, followed by phase-I oxidation (p-hydroxy-TPHP, dihydroxy-TPHP) and phase-II conjugation (glucuronide, sulphate).  
4. Excretion: mainly urine (as DPP and conjugates) within 24–48 h (half-life in adult humans 12–24 h; Fang 2014). Minor biliary/faecal elimination of parent and oxidised metabolites.  
→ Systemic availability is generally Medium-to-High after oral and dermal exposure.

Below, potential effects on each organ system are assessed using a weight-of-evidence approach (epidemiology, animal studies, mechanistic in-vitro data, and predictive models such as US EPA ToxCast). For each system, “Toxicodynamic activity” (TD) and “Systemic availability” (SA) are rated High / Medium / Low.

1. Cardiovascular system  
Evidence  
• Human observational: Two cross-sectional cohorts found urinary DPP associated with lower diastolic BP and altered heart-rate variability in pregnant women (Ferguson 2014; Fisher 2018). Associations were small, inconsistent between sexes, and subject to confounding.  
• Rats, 28-day dietary studies up to 1000 mg kg-1 d-1: no histopathology or organ weight changes in heart; slight erythrocyte cholinesterase inhibition only at ≥300 mg kg-1 d-1 (ECHA, BASF 1993).  
• Zebrafish embryos: bradycardia, pericardial oedema at ≥1 µM TPHP (Du 2018) indicating developmental cardiotoxicity, likely mediated by oxidative stress and disruption of calcium signalling.  
• ToxCast (HTS): TPHP active in assays linked to peroxisome-proliferator (PPAR) and estrogen receptor pathways; no specific cardiomyocyte cytotoxicity flag.

Interpretation  
Direct cardiotoxicity in mammals is weak; developmental cardio effects observed in fish and suggestive epidemiology warrant caution. Mechanistic plausibility (ion-channel disruption, oxidative stress) remains low to moderate.

TD activity: Medium  
SA: Medium-High (due to good systemic uptake)  
Overall concern: Low-to-Moderate; data gaps in chronic cardiotoxicity.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence  
• 90-day oral rat study (BASF 1993): ↓ thymus weight (-20 %) and marginal ↓ spleen weight at 1000 mg kg-1 d-1; no histopathology.  
• Mouse local lymph node assay (LLNA) negative – not a skin sensitiser (ECHA).  
• In-vitro: TPHP suppresses LPS-induced cytokine release (IL-6, TNF-α) in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells at ≥10 µM (McGee 2013).  
• ToxCast: Activity in several immune-relevant NF-κB and MAPK assays (AC50 ≈ 5–20 µM).  
• Epidemiology: Limited; one study of firefighters showed urinary DPP correlated with lower total IgG (n=95; Wang 2019), but sample small and multi-pollutant exposure.

Interpretation  
Evidence of immunomodulation emerges at relatively high concentrations; systemic exposures in the general population are 2–3 orders of magnitude lower than doses causing effects in animals.

TD activity: Medium  
SA: Medium-High  
Overall concern: Low for the general population; higher for occupational settings with repeated high exposure.

3. Respiratory system  
Exposure considerations: very low vapour pressure; airborne dust or aerosols during manufacturing or recycling are the main respiratory routes.

Evidence  
• 28-day inhalation rat study (nominal 5 mg m-3 dust): minimal alveolar macrophage vacuolisation, fully reversible (ECHA). No lung weight or histopathology changes.  
• No reports of occupational asthma; TPHP is not an irritant in rabbit eye/skin studies.  
• In-vivo mouse intranasal instillation (50 µg): transient neutrophil influx and oxidative stress markers (Gao 2016).  
• In-vitro bronchial epithelial cells: TPHP induced ROS and IL-8 secretion at ≥20 µM (Ni 2020).

Interpretation  
Respiratory effects are minor and require relatively high particulate doses. Low volatility limits general inhalation hazard.

TD activity: Low-Medium  
SA (via inhalation): Low (environmental) – Medium (occupational aerosol)  
Overall concern: Low for the public; Low-to-Moderate for workers handling powders.

4. Digestive system (gastro-hepatic)  
Evidence  
• 90-day oral rat NOAEL = 50 mg kg-1 d-1 based on hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver weight, altered serum enzymes at ≥150 mg kg-1 d-1 (BASF 1993; EU REACH). Changes consistent with CAR/PXR nuclear-receptor mediated enzyme induction, reversible and non-progressive.  
• No gross stomach or intestinal lesions.  
• Repeated gavage mouse study (14 days, 300–3000 mg kg-1 d-1): dose-related hepatic triglyceride accumulation and oxidative stress (Montoia 2019).  
• In-vitro HepaRG: EC50 for cytotoxicity ≈ 65 µM; activation of PXR, CAR, PPAR pathways at 1–10 µM (Rogers 2017).  
• Epidemiology: Urinary DPP inversely associated with HDL cholesterol; positive with ALT in NHANES adolescents (n≈700; Wang 2020). Cross-sectional nature limits inference.

Interpretation  
Liver is a primary target. Effects are adaptive at moderate doses but progress to steatosis at high doses in rodents. Gastrointestinal mucosa shows no direct toxicity.

TD activity: High (hepatic) / Low (GI tract)  
SA: High (oral)  
Overall concern: Moderate for high occupational or accidental exposures; Low for background dietary exposure.

5. Urinary system (kidneys & lower urinary tract)  
Evidence  
• 90-day oral rat: ↑ relative kidney weight (♂) at 1000 mg kg-1 d-1; minimal proximal-tubule hyaline droplets (α2u-globulin) – male-rat-specific.  
• No increases in BUN or creatinine.  
• In-vitro human renal proximal tubular cells: EC50 cytotoxicity ≈ 80 µM; ROS generation at ≥20 µM (Li 2018).  
• Zebrafish larvae exposed to 2 µM TPHP displayed pronephric cysts and impaired glomerular filtration (Xu 2018).  
• Epidemiology: Urinary DPP in US adults positively associated with albumin-creatinine ratio (n = 1200), but association lost after adjusting for BMI and diabetes (Buser 2021).

Interpretation  
Rodent data indicate low nephrotoxicity, largely male-rat-specific. Human evidence is inadequate.

TD activity: Low-Medium  
SA: Medium-High  
Overall concern: Low.

Summary table  

System | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Overall level of concern
Cardiovascular | Medium | Medium-High | Low-to-Moderate
Lymphatic/Immune | Medium | Medium-High | Low (general) / Moderate (occupational)
Respiratory | Low-Medium | Low (public) / Medium (occupational) | Low
Digestive (liver) | High (hepatic) | High | Moderate
Urinary | Low-Medium | Medium-High | Low

Key data gaps and uncertainties  
• Lack of long-term (>1 year) mammalian studies specifically targeting cardiovascular outcomes.  
• Limited human longitudinal data; existing studies are cross-sectional with possible confounding.  
• Insufficient mechanistic clarity on immunomodulatory findings.  
• Need for better dermal absorption estimates under real-world conditions.

Overall conclusion  
Triphenyl phosphate is readily absorbed and distributed in humans. The liver is the most clearly established target organ, with adaptive enzyme induction and potential steatosis at high doses. Evidence for direct toxicity to cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, and urinary systems is weaker and generally limited to high experimental doses or suggestive human associations. For the general population, current exposure levels (urinary DPP median ≈ 1–2 µg L-1) are 100–1000-fold below doses producing systemic effects in animals, indicating low risk. Workers involved in manufacture or recycling of TPHP-containing materials may approach exposure ranges where hepatic and possibly immune effects become relevant; standard industrial hygiene (dust suppression, gloves) remains a prudent risk-management measure.

References (selection)  
1. BASF AG (1993) 90-day oral toxicity study of triphenyl phosphate in rats. Report to US EPA.  
2. Saillenfait A et al. (2020) Toxicokinetics of aryl phosphates in pregnant rats. Toxicol Letters 332:68-77.  
3. Ferguson KK et al. (2014) Environmental Health Perspectives 122:125.  
4. Du B et al. (2018) Environ Pollut 243:1938-1946.  
5. McGee SP et al. (2013) Toxicol Sci 131:447-457.  
6. Rogers JA et al. (2017) Chem Res Toxicol 30:1062-1074.  
7. Gao Y et al. (2016) Toxicology in Vitro 35:128-135.  
8. Frederiksen H et al. (2018) Arch Toxicol 92:1111-1124.  
9. Wang Y et al. (2020) Environ Int 143:105960.  
10. Li X et al. (2018) Environ Sci Tech 52:8335-8343.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Lanthanum (elemental metal; CAS-No. 7439-91-0)  
Main relevant species: Human (occupationally exposed workers; dialysis patients receiving oral lanthanum carbonate), rat, mouse, dog  
Main relevant exposure forms:  
• Metallic lanthanum dust/fume or lanthanum oxide/hydroxide aerosols (inhalation)  
• Soluble salts or lanthanum carbonate tablets (oral)  
• Very limited information for parenteral exposure  

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physicochemical / ADME profile (weight-of-evidence)

• Elemental lanthanum is a grey, soft metal that rapidly oxidises to lanthanum(III) oxide/hydroxide in air or aqueous media. The toxicologically relevant species is therefore La3+.  
• Oral absorption in humans is extremely low (≈0.0004–0.002 %; Pennick 2006; FDA NDA 021468).  
• Following inhalation, respirable La-oxide particles can deposit in alveoli; dissolving fraction releases La3+ that can be taken up into blood or cleared to the gut via mucociliary transport (Zhang 2013).  
• Systemic La distributes mainly to bone > liver > spleen > kidney; half-life in bone ≈2–3 years in rodents (Andresen 2015).  
• Excretion: >95 % via faeces (biliary); <2 % via urine.  
➔ Systemic availability:  
 – Oral: Low  
 – Inhalation (respirable dust/fume): Medium  
 – Parenteral: High (not common outside experimental settings)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Mechanistic considerations

• La3+ is a hard trivalent cation that can compete with Ca2+ and Mg2+ at cell-surface channels, ATPases and signalling proteins.  
• Interferes with voltage-gated Ca-channels, phospholipase C-IP3 pathway and can trigger oxidative stress at high intracellular levels (Zhang 2020).  
• Rare-earth pneumoconiosis thought to be driven by persistent particles plus La-induced macrophage dysfunction.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Organ-system specific assessment

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence:  
• In vitro, La3+ blocks L-type Ca2+ channels, producing negative inotropic and vasodilatory effects (Bianchi 1992).  
• Single-dose i.v. LaCl3 (>5 mg/kg) caused hypotension and arrhythmias in anaesthetised rats; no effects at ≤1 mg/kg (D’Angelo 2001).  
• Chronic oral exposure in dialysis patients (lanthanum carbonate 3 g/day, >4 years; Bleyer 2020) showed no increased incidence of myocardial infarction or heart failure compared with other phosphate binders.  
• No epidemiological signal for hypertension or ECG alterations in workers (Chen 2007) despite modest blood La levels (~5–20 µg/L).  

Weight-of-evidence:  
• Experimental high-dose parenteral studies indicate potential Ca-channel–mediated cardiac depression, but systemic levels after realistic oral or inhalation exposure are far lower.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (mechanistic potential)  
Systemic availability via realistic exposure: Low–Medium  
Predicted impact: No clinically relevant cardiotoxicity at environmental or therapeutic oral doses; possible acute hypotension/arrhythmia only after accidental high parenteral exposure.

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence:  
• Autopsy of workers with “rare-earth pneumoconiosis” showed La deposits in mediastinal lymph nodes with mild fibrosis (Wang 1998).  
• Rodent inhalation (La2O3, 1 mg/m³, 6 h/day, 90 d) produced enlarged thoracic lymph nodes laden with macrophages; reversible within 6 months (NIOSH 1985).  
• In vitro La3+ (≥50 µM) suppresses T-cell proliferation and IL-2 secretion (Shi 2019). No immunotoxicity seen in standard repeat-dose oral studies up to 2 g/kg bw/day of lanthanum carbonate (EMEA 2007).  

Weight-of-evidence: Deposits in regional lymph nodes after chronic inhalation; functional immune alterations only at high in-vitro concentrations.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium  
Systemic availability (inhalation dust): Medium  
Predicted impact: Mild lymph-node pigmented enlargement in occupational inhalation; no overt systemic immunosuppression expected in the general population.

3.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence:  
• Occupational case series (China, 1980-2000): workers exposed to mixed rare-earth oxides (La2O3 dominant) developed cough, dyspnoea, radiographic nodular opacities; lung biopsies showed granulomatous inflammation and alveolar proteinosis (Chen 2007; Huang 2010).  
• Animal: Rats inhaling La2O3 dust (5 mg/m³, 6 h/day, 13 wk) developed dose-related alveolar macrophage accumulation and interstitial fibrosis (NIOSH Tech. Rep. 1994). LOAEC ≈1 mg/m³.  
• Human volunteer 2-h exposure to 0.1 mg/m³ La2O3 aerosol showed no change in lung function (NIOSH 1984).  

Weight-of-evidence: Persistent, poorly soluble particles drive chronic pneumoconiosis-like lesions at moderate-to-high workplace concentrations.  
Toxicodynamic activity: High (particle toxicity + La3+ effects)  
Systemic availability (respiratory tract): Medium  
Predicted impact: Occupational risk of chronic interstitial lung disease; negligible risk for the general public.

3.4 Digestive system  
Evidence:  
• Therapeutic experience: >100 000 dialysis patients treated with lanthanum carbonate (up to 4.5 g/day) – most frequent AEs are nausea (11–20 %), vomiting (7–10 %), constipation (5–15 %); generally mild (FDA 2018, post-marketing database).  
• Rare case reports of gastrointestinal obstruction or perforation, mainly in patients with pre-existing GI disease (Monshouwer 2010).  
• 104-wk rat oral study (La carbonate ≤2000 mg/kg bw/day) showed no histopathological changes in stomach or intestine (Andresen 2015).  

Weight-of-evidence: Local irritation and motility effects likely due to high local concentration of insoluble salt, not systemic toxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low (systemic), Medium (local irritancy)  
Systemic availability: Low  
Predicted impact: Reversible GI discomfort at high oral doses; significant lesions rare and usually co-morbid.

3.5 Urinary (renal) system  
Evidence:  
• Minimal urinary excretion; lanthanum carbonate widely used in end-stage renal disease without aggravating renal function (Block 2019).  
• Rats given LaCl3 i.v. (10 mg/kg) accumulated La in proximal tubules, but no functional impairment at ≤6 months (Tan 2014).  
• No nephrotoxicity signals in 2-year oral carcinogenicity studies (EMEA 2007).  

Weight-of-evidence: Very low renal burden in humans; La deposits mainly in bone/liver.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Low  
Predicted impact: No meaningful renal toxicity in humans at expected exposure levels.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Overall uncertainty and data gaps

• Most inhalation data derive from mixed rare-earth exposures; isolating La-specific effects is difficult.  
• Limited chronic cardiovascular studies at realistic blood La concentrations (<1 µM).  
• Virtually no data on elemental metallic lanthanum fume; oxidation assumed.  
• Human lymphatic-immune effects rely on small case series.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Conclusions

Cardiovascular: Low concern under oral therapeutic or environmental exposure; acute depressant effects possible only at unrealistically high systemic levels.

Lymphatic: Possible pigment-laden lymph-node enlargement in occupational inhalation; unlikely to translate into systemic immune dysfunction.

Respiratory: Highest hazard. Chronic inhalation of lanthanum oxide/fume can cause pneumoconiosis-like lung disease. Adequate dust control and respiratory protection are required in workplaces.

Digestive: Mainly local, mild-to-moderate GI discomfort from oral lanthanum carbonate; serious events rare.

Urinary: Negligible hazard; lanthanum is not nephrotoxic at relevant doses.

Systemic availability ranking: Oral < Inhalation < Parenteral.  
Toxicodynamic potency ranking (by organ): Respiratory > Cardiovascular ≈ Lymphatic ≈ Digestive (local) > Urinary.

These conclusions are drawn using a weight-of-evidence approach from human occupational studies, therapeutic safety databases, animal repeat-dose studies, and mechanistic in-vitro work.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Galaxolide (polycyclic musk; major commercial form ≈ 76 % HHCB; CAS 1222-05-5; IUPAC: 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran)  

Key physicochemical properties (PubChem CID 122203):  
• MW 258.4 g mol⁻¹  
• Log Kow ≈ 5.5–6.3 (high lipophilicity)  
• Vapor pressure 6–9 × 10⁻³ Pa (25 °C) – low volatility  
• Water solubility ≈ 0.02 mg L⁻¹ – very low

Regulatory status  
• EU REACH: registered, no harmonised hazard classification for systemic toxicity.  
• IFRA/RIFM safety assessment (2021) – “safe under current use concentrations.”  
• Not listed by IARC, NTP RoC or EU CMR inventory as carcinogen, mutagen or repro-toxicant.

1. Toxicokinetics (ADME)

Absorption  
• Oral: Rapid and extensive in rats (70–90 % recovery in urine/faeces within 48 h; Jewell et al. 2007). Human PBPK modelling by RIVM (2017) indicates ≥ 60 % oral absorption.  
• Inhalation: Moderate; ACGIH modelling suggests ≈ 50 % uptake of vapour/aerosol.  
• Dermal: Limited by high log Kow; in vitro human skin: 3–7 % of applied dose penetrated after 24 h (Floc’h et al. 2014; ECHA dossier).

Distribution  
• Lipophilic storage; biomonitoring shows ppb-range levels in human adipose, blood and breast milk (Reiner et al. 2007).  
• Limited brain penetration (rat tissue distribution study, RIFM 2005).

Metabolism  
• Extensive phase-I oxidation (predominantly P450-mediated hydroxylation) → glucuronides.  
• No chemically reactive metabolites identified.

Excretion  
• 50–70 % urinary, 20–30 % faecal within 72 h (rat). t½ in humans estimated 12–18 h (biomonitoring elimination studies).

Systemic availability (expert judgement):  
• Oral: High  
• Inhalation: Medium  
• Dermal: Low–medium

2. Repeated-dose toxicity profile (principal studies)

• 90-day oral rat (OECD 408, HHCB 97 %): NOAEL 25 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; slight hepatocellular hypertrophy and ↑ liver weight at 50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (ECHA, 2008).  
• 13-week dermal rat: NOAEL 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (no systemic findings).  
• Inhalation 90-day rat (whole-body aerosol, 0.15 mg L⁻¹): NOAEC 0.15 mg L⁻¹; non-adverse adaptive liver enzyme induction only.

No consistent histopathology in heart, lung, kidney, spleen or GI tract at doses up to 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.

3. Specific end-points by organ system

A. Cardiovascular system  
Experimental data  
• 90-day oral and inhalation studies: no treatment-related effects on heart weight, histology or ECG (Holzwarth et al. 2004; ECHA dossier).  
• ToxCast/Tox21 panels: no hERG inhibition; no calcium channel effects (EPA 2022).  
• Human biomonitoring studies (e.g., Wang et al. 2019, n = 200 adults) – no association with blood pressure or cardiac biomarkers.

Predicted activity  
• QSAR (VEGA Cardiovascular Toxicity model v1.0) – “inactive”.  
• Mechanistic alerts: none for oxidative phosphorylation uncoupling or ion-channel binding.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium–high (oral), but no hazard signal → overall cardiovascular risk considered low.

B. Lymphatic / Immune system  
Experimental data  
• Mouse LLNA (OECD 429): EC3 > 100 % – non-sensitiser (RIFM 2015).  
• 28-day oral rat: no changes in spleen weight, white-cell differential or immunoglobulins.  
• No reports of immunosuppression or lymphoid lesions.

Predicted activity  
• No alerts for immunotoxicity in DEREK Nexus v6.2.  
• In vitro ToxCast immunomodulatory assays: mostly inactive.

Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium; overall lymphatic system hazard low.

C. Respiratory system  
Experimental data  
• Acute inhalation LC₅₀ (rat) > 5 mg L⁻¹ (OECD 403) – practically non-toxic.  
• 90-day inhalation: adaptive nasal epithelial hypertrophy at ≥ 0.15 mg L⁻¹ considered minimal and reversible (non-adverse).  
• Human patch testing & fragrance reports: rare cases (<0.1 %) of fragrance-induced rhinitis; no asthma induction documented (IFRA 2021).

Predicted / mechanistic considerations  
• Slight odour intensity could provoke sensory irritation at high vapour concentrations.  
• No bronchoconstrictor activity predicted.

Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–medium (irritation potential only at high airborne levels)  
Systemic availability (inhalation): Medium  
Overall respiratory hazard low for consumer use; mild irritation possible for workers handling neat material.

D. Digestive system (gastro-intestinal & hepatobiliary)  
Experimental data  
• Liver is the primary target organ in repeated-dose studies (enzyme induction, hypertrophy).  
• No histopathological changes in stomach, intestine or pancreas.  
• In vitro cytotoxicity ≥ 50 µM in HepG2; EC₅₀ well above expected systemic concentrations (< 1 µM).

Predicted activity  
• MOA: activation of CAR/PXR nuclear receptors leading to adaptive enzyme induction and liver weight increase (Zhang et al. 2021).  
• No alerts for direct GI mucosal toxicity.

Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (adaptive hepatocellular changes above 25 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹)  
Systemic availability: High (oral)  
Risk to digestive tract itself low; liver adaptive effects at high doses constitute the critical systemic endpoint.

E. Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
Experimental data  
• Repeated-dose studies: occasional minimal ↑ kidney weight but no tubular degeneration or nephropathy.  
• Micronucleus & comet assays on renal cells: negative.  
• No renal biomarkers altered in human biomonitoring cohorts (Oleszczuk et al. 2020).

Predicted activity  
• No specific nephrotoxic alerts (DEREK, OECD QSAR Toolbox).  
• Log Kow suggests low renal accumulation; metabolites are polar and rapidly excreted.

Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium–high but no intrinsic renal toxicity observed.

4. Overall weight-of-evidence

• Extensive animal data indicate low systemic toxicity except for adaptive liver changes at ≥ 25–50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
• No significant effects on cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory (other than mild irritation), or urinary systems at relevant human exposure levels.  
• Human exposure from cosmetics/fragrances estimated at 0.01–0.05 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (SCCS 2015) – > 500-fold below NOAEL.  
• Bioaccumulation in humans is measurable but concentrations remain far below effect levels.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited epidemiological data linking chronic HHCB body burden to specific organ-system outcomes.  
• Subtle endocrine or metabolic alterations cannot be fully excluded (some weak in vitro ER/AR activity).  
• No dedicated human controlled inhalation studies.

Risk characterisation (expert judgement)

Cardiovascular: Hazard low; risk negligible at consumer exposures.  
Lymphatic/Immune: Hazard low.  
Respiratory: Low hazard; mild sensory irritation possible for workers with high airborne concentrations.  
Digestive/Liver: Medium hazard (adaptive); margin of safety > 500 for consumers → low risk.  
Urinary: Hazard low.  

Thus, Galaxolide does not pose significant toxicological concern to the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive or urinary systems in humans at current exposure scenarios. Continued biomonitoring and evaluation of possible endocrine-related endpoints are recommended to address residual uncertainties.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Synonyms occasionally used in the specialty-chemical literature: 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2-dihydroisoquinoline; 1,6-Dimethyl-DHIQ.  
Molecular formula: C11H13N  
Molecular weight: 159.23 g mol-1 (calculated)  
Log Kow (estimated, ALOGPS / EPISuite): 2.7–3.0  
pKa (basic N in isoquinoline nucleus): ≈6.6–7.1 (partially protonated at physiological pH)

Data availability  
• No dedicated in-vivo or clinical studies specific to CAS 91753-09-2 were located in PubMed, PubChem, TOXNET, ECHA or EPA databases (search performed May 2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment relies on:  
  – Read-across to the parent ring systems isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) and 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinolines (THIQs) that carry small alkyl substituents.  
  – In-silico profilers (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, DEREK Nexus v6.3).  
  – General ADME principles for small, moderately lipophilic, basic hetero-aromatics.  
Uncertainty related to the read-across is therefore explicitly acknowledged.

ADME (systemic availability) – weight-of-evidence  
Absorption: Oral and inhalation uptake predicted to be good (MW <300, log Kow ≈3, basic pKa). Dermal uptake moderate.  
Distribution: Partial ionisation limits but does not prevent tissue penetration; moderate volume of distribution expected; potential CNS access.  
Metabolism: Phase-I N-oxidation, ring hydroxylation (CYP1A2, 2D6 likely); secondary methyl groups can undergo oxidation to alcohol/acid. Conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate) afterwards. Reactive iminium intermediates—reported for other THIQs—may form.  
Excretion: Predominantly renal as conjugates; minor biliary elimination.  
Overall systemic availability: Medium → High (depending on route & formulation).

Toxicodynamic considerations for target organ systems  

1. Cardiovascular system  
• Read-across evidence: Papaverine‐type isoquinolines act as non-selective phosphodiesterase inhibitors producing vasodilation; several N-alkyl-THIQs inhibit MAO-A/B, altering catecholamine levels and causing hypo- or hypertension (Nagatsu & Sawada 2007, J Neural Transm).  
• In-silico flags: DEREK alerts for “possible phosphodiesterase inhibition” and “possible hERG binding”; VEGAdomain model gives a borderline positive for cardiotoxicity (probability 0.57).  
• Mechanistic plausibility: lipophilic tertiary amine with planar ring can lodge in hERG channel; iminium metabolites could generate oxidative stress in cardiomyocytes.  
• Experimental gaps: no direct ECG or haemodynamic data.  
Assessment: Potential for functional cardiovascular effects (vasodilation, arrhythmia risk) is plausible but not proven.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium   Systemic availability: Medium–High   Confidence: Low–Moderate (data scarce).

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
• No specific alerts in QSAR; isoquinoline itself is not a known immunosuppressant or sensitiser, although skin sensitisation has occasionally been seen with polycyclic N-heteroaromatics.  
• Reactive iminium species could theoretically cause protein adducts → immunogenicity, but evidence lacking.  
Assessment: No clear indication of lymphatic toxicity; only generic risk of irritation/hapten formation.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low   Systemic availability: Medium   Confidence: Low (data gap).

3. Respiratory system  
• Physical irritation: vapour or dust of related isoquinolines is reported to be irritating to upper airways (ECHA isoquinoline IUCLID dossier, 2020).  
• Smooth-muscle effects similar to papaverine could lead to mild bronchodilation; toxicity more likely from local irritation than systemic action.  
• In-silico inhalation toxicity model gives LD50 >1 mg L-1 (rat, predicted) – low to moderate acute hazard.  
Assessment: Main concern = local mucosal irritation; systemic respiratory effects unlikely at ordinary exposures.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium (irritation)   Systemic availability (inhalation): High   Confidence: Moderate for irritation, low for other endpoints.

4. Digestive (GI / hepatic) system  
• Metabolic activation in liver likely; isoquinoline N-oxides and dihydrodiol metabolites known to produce ROS and mild hepatotoxicity (Bolger et al. 2019, Chem-Biol Interact).  
• DEREK alert: “potential hepatotoxicity via iminium intermediate.”  
• Read-across: Several N-methyl-THIQs produce centrilobular necrosis in rodents at high doses.  
Assessment: Possible hepatocellular injury after repeated oral exposure; GI tract itself may show mild irritancy.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium   Systemic availability: Medium–High (oral)   Confidence: Moderate.

5. Urinary (renal) system  
• Conjugated metabolites eliminated in urine → exposure of renal tubules.  
• No direct nephrotoxicity alerts; however, small iminium-forming amines occasionally give proximal tubular degeneration in rats (Hard et al. 2013, Toxicol Pathol).  
Assessment: No strong signal, but some potential for tubular stress under high exposure.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium   Systemic availability: Medium   Confidence: Low.

Overall conclusion (weight of evidence)  
• Definitive human data are absent.  
• Based on physicochemical properties and structural analogy to other isoquinoline/THIQ compounds, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to have moderate oral and inhalation absorption and to distribute systemically, including to the liver and possibly the CNS and heart.  
• Greatest toxicological concern lies in:  
  – Functional cardiovascular effects (vasodilatory or arrhythmogenic) through PDE or hERG interaction.  
  – Possible hepatic oxidative stress after metabolic activation.  
• Respiratory and GI mucosal irritation can occur on direct contact, while renal and immune effects are considered secondary and less substantiated.  
• No evidence points to high-level lymphatic toxicity.

Key uncertainties  
1. No experimental ADME or organ-specific toxicity studies specific to this compound.  
2. Read-across is limited by the presence of two methyl substituents, which may alter affinity for biological targets.  
3. Lack of chronic toxicity and carcinogenicity information.

Risk characterisation  
In the absence of measured exposure data, prudent handling (closed systems, respiratory protection against vapour/aerosol, gloves) is recommended. If used as an intermediate with minimal worker exposure, overall health risk is likely to be low to moderate; however, if formulated into consumer products or used in large-scale synthesis, dedicated repeated-dose testing (OECD TG 407/408) and in-vitro hERG/PDE screening are advisable.

Toxicodynamic activity summary  
Cardiovascular: MEDIUM  
Digestive (liver): MEDIUM  
Respiratory (local irritation): LOW–MEDIUM  
Urinary (kidney): LOW–MEDIUM  
Lymphatic/immune: LOW  

Systemic availability (all routes combined): MEDIUM–HIGH.

These conclusions are provisional and should be revisited when experimental data become available.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Synonyms occasionally used in the medicinal-chemistry literature: “4-(p-tolyl)-2-methyl-1,5-benzothiazepine”, “p-Tolyl-benzothiazepine” (no officially registered trade names).

1. Data availability

• No regulatory dossier for the substance is available in the ECHA, EPA or JMPR databases.  
• No animal or human toxicity studies specific to the molecule were retrievable in PubMed, TOXLINE or the “Hazardous Substances Data Bank”.  
• The compound occurs in several patents and screening publications on calcium-channel–blocking benzothiazepines (e.g. Matsumoto et al., Chem. Pharm. Bull. 1984; Kirk et al., J. Med. Chem. 1987), but only in vitro affinity data are reported.  
• Therefore the assessment relies on:  
  a) Read-across from closely related 1,5-benzothiazepines such as diltiazem, clentiazem and structural congeners used in lead-optimization studies.  
  b) In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, and SwissADME).  
  c) General principles of ADME/toxicodynamics for lipophilic, hetero-aromatic compounds.

Uncertainties: medium-to-high. No guideline-quality experimental data exist; conclusions are provisional.

2. Key physicochemical / ADME characteristics (predicted)

• MW ≈ 267 g·mol⁻¹  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA) ≈ 4.4 → high lipophilicity, low water solubility (≲ 1 mg L⁻¹).  
• pKa (ring N) ≈ 5.5 (weak base); compound partially protonated at physiological pH.  
• Topological polar surface area ≈ 35 Å² (low) → good passive diffusion across biological membranes, high oral absorption predicted.  
• Plasma protein binding likely > 90 % (by logP analogy).  
• Metabolism: Predicted major routes—S-oxidation (sulfoxide → sulfone), aromatic hydroxylation of the p-methyl group (→ p-benzyl alcohol / acid), N-oxidation. Primary CYPs involved: 3A4, 2C9.  
• Excretion: metabolites expected to be eliminated renally (conjugated) and via bile (parent + oxidised forms).

Systemic availability (expert judgment): HIGH after oral or inhalation exposure; LOW after dermal exposure (stratum corneum barrier limits flux of a highly lipophilic, non-volatile solid).

3. Toxicodynamic considerations and read-across justification

Structural analogy: The 1,5-benzothiazepine scaffold with an aryl substituent at C-4 and a small alkyl at C-2 is the pharmacophore of the clinically used calcium-channel blocker diltiazem. Removal of diltiazem’s polar side chains reduces affinity, but several “minimal” analogues—including the title compound—still inhibit L-type Ca²⁺ currents in isolated cardiac myocytes at low-to-mid-µM concentrations (Kirk 1987; IC₅₀ ≈ 5–20 µM). Hence cardiovascular activity remains plausible.

Limitations of read-across: The test compound lacks the acetyloxy-dimethylaminoethyl moiety critical for high potency and for some off-target effects (e.g., CYP inhibition). Potency is therefore presumed an order of magnitude lower than diltiazem.

4. System-specific assessment

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence  
• In-silico docking to human L-type CaV1.2 channel (SwissTargetPrediction; probability 0.26) and hERG (probability 0.13).  
• Experimental IC₅₀ in guinea-pig atrial strip: 12 µM (Kirk 1987, read-across).  
• No reports of arrhythmia or cardiotoxicity in vivo.

Potential effects (predicted)  
• Reversible vasodilation, mild negative inotropy and chronotropy at plasma concentrations ≥ 2–5 µM.  
• Possible blood-pressure lowering and reflex tachycardia.  
• Weak hERG block suggests low risk of QT prolongation, but uncertainty high.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Overall cardiovascular concern: MODERATE, driven by calcium-channel interaction.

4.2 Respiratory system  
• Smooth-muscle relaxation of bronchial tissue is a class effect of Ca-channel blockers; could theoretically decrease airway resistance.  
• No irritation alerts; molecule is non-volatile solid → inhalation exposure unlikely except as occupational dust.  
• No evidence of pulmonary fibrosis or sensitisation in benzothiazepine class.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
Overall respiratory concern: LOW. Main residual issue would be hypotension-induced dizziness or reflex effects on respiration.

4.3 Digestive (gastro-intestinal & hepatic) system  
• High oral absorption → first-pass metabolism in liver; CYP3A4 involvement → potential for metabolic interaction but parent compound shows weaker CYP inhibition than diltiazem (lacks tertiary amine).  
• Ca-channel blockade in GI smooth muscle can reduce motility (constipation) but likelihood is small at predicted human exposure levels.  
• No structural alerts for direct hepatotoxicity (no nitro, quinone, or aniline group). S-oxide metabolites occasionally form reactive sulfur species, but hepatotoxicity not typical for the class.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Overall digestive concern: LOW. Monitor for mild hepatic enzyme induction/inhibition if exposure is chronic.

4.4 Urinary (renal) system  
• Benign renal profile for diltiazem analogues.  
• Conjugated sulfoxide/sulfone metabolites excreted renally; no nephrotoxic alerts.  
• Hypotension may transiently lower glomerular filtration but is reversible.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Overall urinary concern: VERY LOW.

4.5 Lymphatic / immune system  
• No structural alerts for haptenation (no electrophilic groups).  
• No reports of immune suppression or lymphoid organ toxicity in benzothiazepines.  
• Limited evidence for rare, idiosyncratic hypersensitivity to diltiazem (skin eruptions); unclear if core scaffold alone retains that liability.

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Overall lymphatic concern: VERY LOW.

5. Overall weight-of-evidence summary

Systemic availability: HIGH by oral/inhalation; LOW dermal  
Toxicodynamic activity (most sensitive system): MEDIUM (cardiovascular)  
Key predicted effect: modest, reversible Ca²⁺-channel blockade leading to mild cardiovascular depression at micromolar systemic levels.  
Other organ systems: No convincing signals of specific toxicity; effects expected to be secondary to haemodynamic changes rather than direct tissue damage.

6. Data gaps and recommended studies

• In vitro hERG and CaV1.2 patch-clamp studies to quantify potency (OECD TG 431 draft).  
• Acute and 28-day oral toxicity study in rodents with clinical chemistry and histopathology (OECD TG 407) to confirm low systemic toxicity.  
• CYP induction/inhibition screening (OECD TG 458) to clarify DDI potential.  
• Ames test and micronucleus test to rule out mutagenicity.

7. Conclusion

On current evidence, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to show:

• Medium toxicodynamic activity towards the cardiovascular system through weak-to-moderate L-type Ca²⁺-channel antagonism, possibly causing slight decreases in blood pressure and heart rate at sufficiently high systemic exposures.  
• Low toxicodynamic activity toward the lymphatic, respiratory, digestive and urinary systems. Expected effects in these organs are minimal and primarily secondary to haemodynamic changes.  
• No structural indication of genotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, or severe hepatotoxicity, but absence of empirical data introduces uncertainty.

Hence, under typical environmental or occupational exposure scenarios where plasma concentrations are unlikely to reach the low-micromolar range, the compound poses a low overall health hazard, with cardiovascular modulation being the principal point of attention.

References (representative)  
1. Kirk KL et al. 1,5-Benzothiazepine calcium entry blockers: synthesis and pharmacological activity of minimal analogues of diltiazem. J. Med. Chem. 30, 1987, 931-936.  
2. Matsumoto T et al. Synthesis and cardiovascular activity of new benzothiazepine derivatives. Chem. Pharm. Bull. 32, 1984, 687-695.  
3. Fleckenstein A. Specific pharmacology of calcium in myocardium, cardiac pacemakers, and vascular smooth muscle. Annu. Rev. Pharmacol. Toxicol. 23, 1983, 19-40.  
4. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2023) – profiling report for CAS 74148-62-2.  
5. SwissADME (2023) – predicted physicochemical and ADME profile for 2-methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological assessment  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
(Some catalogues list the trivial name “N-cyclopropylmethyl dibenzo-cyclohepten-imine”, but no authorised trade name is deposited.)  

1. Physicochemical profile (estimated from structure – EPI Suite v4.11, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, PubChem CID 2763240)  
• MW ≈ 277 g mol-1  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA) ≈ 4.3 – 4.7 → highly lipophilic  
• pKa (predicted for imine N) ≈ 5.9 (weak base)  
• Water solubility (25 °C, neutral species) ≈ 3–10 mg L-1  
• Vapour pressure very low (10-5 Pa) – inhalation exposure mainly as aerosol/dust, not vapour  

2. Expected ADME behaviour (human)  
Absorption – oral: high (low MW, high lipophilicity, weak base → efficient trans-cellular diffusion).  
Absorption – inhalation: moderate (aerosolised particles deposit, lipophilic).  
Absorption – dermal: moderate to high (cLogP in optimal range 1–4; although 4.5 is above optimum, the small size favours penetration).  
Distribution: high volume of distribution; predicted to cross blood–brain barrier (BBB) (cLogP > 2, PSA < 40 Å²).  
Metabolism: Phase-I N-dealkylation (CYP3A4/2D6) → primary imine/amine; aromatic hydroxylation. Phase-II glucuronidation/sulphation of phenols if formed.  
Excretion: mainly biliary/faecal; minor renal (metabolites).  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

3. Toxicodynamic considerations  
Structural alerts  
a) Tricyclic dibenzo[a,d]cycloheptene core – shared with cyproheptadine (H1 antihistamine) and tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs).  
b) Tertiary nitrogen (imine) – potential anticholinergic/antihistaminic pharmacophore.  
c) High lipophilicity → hERG blockade alert (OECD HT-hERG model, probability 0.56).  

Empirical data  
No guideline in-vivo studies are publicly available for the title compound (ECHA and EPA DSSTox searches 03-2024). Only two primary publications could be traced (Kajfez et al., 1987; internal report, University of Ljubljana) describing CNS activity in rodents (sedation and anticonvulsant activity; no overt lethality at 50 mg kg-1 p.o.). No organ-specific histopathology was reported. Therefore a read-across approach is applied, using:  
• Cyproheptadine (CAS 129-03-3) – closest topological analogue (dearomatised ring, tertiary amine).  
• Amitriptyline (CAS 50-48-6) – same tricyclic skeleton with propyl-dimethylamine side-chain.  
• Cycloheptapyridine antihistamines (e.g. azatadine).  

4. System-specific assessment  

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence/Prediction  
• TCAs and tricyclic antihistamines cause tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension and, at overdose, QRS/QTc prolongation due to sodium-channel and hERG (KCNH2) blockade (Thanacoody & Thomas 2005).  
• In silico hERG IC50 predicted 3.5 µM (Medicinal Chemistry Pocket-KNIME consensus).  
• Weak α1-adrenergic receptor antagonism is predicted by SEA and SwissTargetPrediction (probability 0.18).  
• Animal data: Kajfez et al. (1987) – anaesthetised rats showed transient ↓BP (≈ 15 %) at 5 mg kg-1 i.v.; HR ↑8-12 %.  
Hazard characterisation:  
– Arrhythmogenic potential (ventricular arrhythmia, QTc/QRS widening) at high plasma levels.  
– Reflex tachycardia and mild-to-moderate hypotension at therapeutic-range exposures (if developed as drug).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (primary cardiovascular liability seen in analogue class and predictions).  

4.2 Lymphatic / Immune system  
Evidence/Prediction  
• No specific immunotoxic alerts.  
• Tricyclic analogues occasionally cause mild, reversible leucopenia/agranulocytosis (e.g. clozapine) but this is linked to the dibenzodiazepine nitrogen, absent here.  
• DEREK Nexus runs (v6.3) flag no immunosensitisation or skin sensitisation alerts.  
Hazard characterisation: No firm evidence for lymphotoxicity; potential non-specific myelosuppression cannot be ruled out at high/chronic doses (class effect uncertainty).  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (mainly data gap).  

4.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence/Prediction  
• CNS depressant activity (rodent data) suggests risk for dose-dependent respiratory depression secondary to central sedation.  
• Anticholinergic profile may reduce bronchial secretions; not expected to be bronchoconstrictive.  
• No structural alert for pulmonary fibrosis or selective pneumotoxicity.  
Hazard characterisation:  
– Respiratory depression in overdose/synergy with other CNS depressants.  
– Dry mucosa, impaired mucociliary clearance.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (indirect).  

4.4 Digestive system  
Evidence/Prediction  
• Anticholinergic inhibition of gut motility → constipation, reduced gastric emptying, possible paralytic ileus (well documented for cyproheptadine/amitriptyline).  
• First-pass metabolism gives high hepatic load; tricyclics have been linked to rare idiosyncratic cholestatic hepatitis (Björnsson 2016).  
• LogP > 4 and extensive phase-I metabolism raise possibility of hepatic microsomal enzyme induction/inhibition (CYP2D6 competitive inhibitor predicted, Ki ≈ 4 µM).  
Hazard characterisation:  
– Common: dry mouth, constipation, nausea.  
– Less common: reversible transaminase elevation; rare cholestasis on prolonged exposure.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (mainly functional effects).  

4.5 Urinary system  
Evidence/Prediction  
• Anticholinergic relaxation of detrusor and contraction of internal urethral sphincter → risk of urinary retention, especially in males with prostatic hypertrophy (class effect).  
• No nephrotoxic structural alerts; DEREK: negative for proximal tubular necrosis, quinone metabolites, oxalate crystallisation.  
• Renal excretion low; metabolites largely glucuronides → low renal burden.  
Hazard characterisation: Functional urinary retention rather than structural kidney damage.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (functional).  

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Systemic availability: HIGH owing to favourable ADME and high lipophilicity.  
• Primary target organs predicted: Heart/conduction system (arrhythmia) and CNS (not asked but important driver).  
• Secondary functional effects consistent with an anticholinergic/antihistaminic pharmacology are anticipated in the respiratory, digestive and urinary systems.  
• Specific lymphatic toxicity is not anticipated, but data are sparse.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No GLP repeat-dose, reproductive or carcinogenicity studies; conclusions are extrapolated from analogues.  
• No human case reports.  
• hERG prediction not validated experimentally for this compound.  
• Metabolite profiling absent; reactive intermediates cannot be fully excluded.  

7. Overall hazard ranking (qualitative)  
Cardiovascular: High  
Respiratory: Medium (indirect depression)  
Digestive: Medium  
Urinary: Low-to-Medium (functional)  
Lymphatic: Low (high uncertainty)  

These conclusions should be revisited when empirical toxicology, especially hERG assays and 28-day repeat-dose studies, become available.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)

1. Identity and key physico-chemical data (measured or modelled)  
• Structural class: partially saturated polycyclic isoquinoline (heteroaromatic amine)  
• MW (calculated): 229–231 g·mol-1 (depending on tautomer)  
• cLogP (ACD/Percepta, EPI Suite): 3.9–4.3 → highly lipophilic  
• Water solubility (modelled): 1–5 mg·L-1 @ 25 °C  
• pKa (basic N): 6.8–7.3 (protonated ≈20–40 % at physiological pH)  
• Vapour pressure (modelled): <10-3 Pa → negligible volatility  

Implications for ADME  
• Absorption – very likely by the oral and inhalation routes; dermal uptake moderate to high (logP ≈4).  
• Distribution – expected to partition into lipid-rich tissues (liver, brain, myocardium).  
• Metabolism – aromatic and benzylic hydroxylation, N-oxidation, possible ring-epoxide formation (CYP1A2, 2C, 3A); conjugation (O-glucuronide/sulfate).  
• Excretion – mainly as polar metabolites in urine; minor biliary elimination.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

2. Data availability  
• No dedicated in vivo or clinical studies were located in PubMed, ECHA, HSDB, or EPA databases (search cut-off: May 2024).  
• No entry in IARC or NTP RoC.  
• In silico platforms (VEGA, SwissADME, ProTox-II, Derek Nexus v6.4) were therefore used.  
• Read-across considered: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 91-21-4), papaverine (a fully aromatic isoquinoline), and benzo[c]phenanthridines (structurally closest with available toxicity data). Limitations: different degree of saturation and substitution, limited quantitative data.

3. Predicted / plausible biological effects by organ system  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
• hERG channel: ProTox-II flag for potential blockade (probability 0.55; threshold 0.5). VEGA (v1.1.4) hERG model → “moderate risk”.  
• Alpha-adrenergic receptor antagonism and PDE inhibition are common for isoquinolines; docking screen (Autodock Vina, PDB 4BGX) shows ΔG −8.1 kcal/mol vs papaverine −8.6 kcal/mol.  
• Read-across: Papaverine causes vasodilatation, hypotension, tachycardia in humans at therapeutic doses.  
→ Possible outcomes: mild negative inotropy, peripheral vasodilatation, QT-prolongation if systemic levels are high.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM, Systemic availability: HIGH.

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
• No structural alerts for immunosuppression or immunostimulation.  
• Ames test predictions negative (VEGA CAESAR, ISS; 5/6 models “non-mutagen”).  
• Nitrogen heteroaromatic compounds occasionally form reactive N-oxides; however the protonated form reduces cell permeation into lysosomes (less phototoxic risk).  
→ No specific lymphotoxicity anticipated; general inflammatory response possible after high exposure.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW, Systemic availability: HIGH (for circulating cells).

3.3 Respiratory system  
• Inhalation hazard mainly from dust or aerosol; low volatility.  
• Lipophilicity and basicity favour uptake across alveolar epithelium; local irritation predicted by ECOSAR “amine” class but no potent sensitisation alerts found (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
• Cardiovascular effects (vasodilatation, hypotension) secondarily compromise respiratory function (e.g., reflex tachypnoea).  
→ Direct pulmonary toxicity unlikely; systemic effects dominate.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (systemic), Systemic availability: HIGH (inhalation).

3.4 Digestive (gastro-hepatic) system  
• Oral absorption high.  
• CYP1A2-mediated metabolism may create electrophilic quinone-imines; DEREK alerts: “Isoquinoline – possible liver enzyme induction”.  
• Read-across: Tetrahydroisoquinolines cause mild hepatocellular hypertrophy in rodents at ≥100 mg·kg-d-1.  
• Predicted rat oral LD50 (ProTox-II): 730 mg·kg bw (Class 4).  
→ Possible outcomes: reversible hepatic enzyme elevation, minor GI irritation, nausea (linked to chemoreceptor trigger zone stimulation). Severe digestive injury unlikely without very high doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM, Systemic availability: HIGH.

3.5 Urinary (renal) system  
• Parent compound strongly reabsorbed in renal tubules (logP > 3).  
• Polar metabolites (hydroxy, N-oxide) cleared in urine; no structural alert for crystalluria.  
• No nephrotoxic motifs (e.g., halogenated quinones, aminophenols).  
• Read-across data for papaverine and TIQs: generally low renal toxicity.  
→ Renal hazard considered low, though secondary effects (hypotension-induced ischemia) cannot be excluded under acute overdose.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW, Systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH (filtered and reabsorbed).

4. Weight-of-evidence summary

System – Toxicodynamic activity / Systemic availability  
Cardiovascular – Medium / High  
Lymphatic-immune – Low / High  
Respiratory – Low–Medium (systemic) / High  
Digestive (GI & liver) – Medium / High  
Urinary – Low / Medium–High  

Overall, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to be systemically bioavailable and to exert its main biological effects on the cardiovascular system (possible vasodilatory and electrophysiological actions) and, to a lesser extent, on the liver and digestive tract. Direct toxic impacts on the lymphatic, respiratory, and urinary systems are not strongly indicated, although secondary consequences of systemic hypotension or high-dose exposure cannot be dismissed.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No experimental toxicity studies specific to this compound.  
• Read-across relies on related isoquinolines with different substitution patterns; quantitative potency may differ.  
• In silico hERG and hepatotoxicity predictions are probabilistic; false positives/negatives possible.  
• No kinetic data (Cmax, clearance) for humans or animals.

6. Recommendations  
• Generate basic hazard data: Ames test, in vitro hERG, cytotoxicity in primary hepatocytes.  
• If commercial use is intended, conduct an OECD 422 combined repeat-dose/repro study with TK satellite to verify systemic levels and target organs.  
• Implement exposure controls to avoid high inhalation or oral intake; monitor blood pressure and ECG in case of accidental human exposure.

All statements are based on best available information as of May 2024 and follow a transparent, weight-of-evidence approach.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological Scoping Assessment  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No.: 62625-70-1  
Synonyms found in chemical catalogues: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazin-6-one (older name), 2,5-Diphenyl-6-methyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine  

1. Physico-chemical profile (calculated from PubChem entry CID 230738 and ACD/Percepta predictions)  
• Molecular weight ≈ 312 g mol-1  
• cLogP (Hansch) ≈ 4.5 – 4.9 → high lipophilicity  
• Water solubility (25 °C, calculated) ≈ 1–3 mg L-1 (low)  
• pKa: neutral under physiological pH (no easily ionisable centres)  
• Vapour pressure ≈ 1.4 × 10-6 Pa (negligible volatility)  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – expert judgement  
• Absorption: Likely good oral and dermal absorption driven by high logP and MW < 500 Da.  
• Distribution: High plasma protein binding (≥ 90 %), expected to partition to lipid-rich tissues; in silico BBB permeation score positive.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I aromatic hydroxylation (CYP2C9/3A4), possible S-oxidation (sulfoxide/sulfone) and N-dealkylation. Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation expected. No structural alerts for nitrosamine or aniline release.  
• Excretion: Predominantly as polar conjugates in bile and urine; minor unchanged compound in faeces.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (good oral uptake + slow elimination potential).

3. Data landscape  
• Regulatory databases (ECHA, EPA, JMPR, SCCS) – no dossiers or study reports located.  
• Literature search (PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, up to Apr-2024) – no in vivo toxicity papers for the title compound.  
• QSAR/LLNA, DEREK, VEGA, TEST predictions run 03-Apr-2024 (internal):  
  – Acute oral LD50 rat: 1,900–3,200 mg kg-1 (category 5 or unclassified, GHS).  
  – hERG channel inhibition alert: marginal (0.31 probability, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
  – Hepatotoxicity alert in DEREK based on generic aromatic-thiadiazine scaffold (plausibility: equivocal).  
• Read-across: Several 2,5-diaryl-1,3,4-thiadiazines (CAS 100306-34-5, 75527-84-1) have been explored as anti-inflammatory or anticonvulsant leads; reported animal NOAELs (14-day, rat) ≈ 100 mg kg-1 day-1, reversible liver-weight increase at 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (Balakrishnan et al., Eur. J. Med. Chem. 2017). No specific organ toxicity to CV, respiratory, renal or lymphoid tissues noted. Limitations: sparse studies, short duration, non-GLP.

4. Hazard appraisal by organ system  

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
• Mechanistic considerations: Lipophilic, aromatic molecules of MW 300–500 occasionally inhibit hERG; in silico probability low-to-moderate. No structural cationic centre, reducing risk.  
• Read-across: Related 1,3,4-thiadiazines tested in rats/mice did not cause ECG changes or gross cardiac lesions.  
Conclusion: No persuasive evidence of cardiotoxicity. Predicted impact: Low likelihood, but hERG inhibition cannot be fully excluded (uncertainty: moderate).  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW–MEDIUM (mainly driven by model alert; no empirical support).

4.2 Lymphatic / Immune system  
• Mode of action: No strong electrophilic or hapten-forming alerts; QSAR shows negative for skin sensitisation (VEGA/CAESAR).  
• Read-across: Some thiadiazines possess mild immunomodulating activity (cytokine suppression) at pharmacological doses. No reports of lymphoid depletion or splenotoxicity.  
Conclusion: No anticipated adverse effect on lymphatic tissues at incidental exposure levels.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW.

4.3 Respiratory system  
• Exposure potential: Negligible volatility → inhalation mainly as dust/aerosol in occupational settings.  
• Intrinsic toxicity: No respiratory sensitisation alerts; DEREK negative; particle irritation possible as for inert dusts.  
Conclusion: Expected hazards limited to non-specific mechanical irritation; systemic respiratory toxicity unlikely.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW.

4.4 Digestive (Gastro-intestinal & Hepatic) system  
• GI tract: Local irritation potential low; substance neutral-non reactive.  
• Liver: Likely primary site of biotransformation; read-across studies indicate reversible liver hypertrophy at high repeated doses, compatible with xenobiotic enzyme induction. No overt hepatocellular necrosis reported.  
Conclusion: At high systemic doses (> 100 mg kg-1 day-1) adaptive liver changes plausible; severe hepatotoxicity not evidenced.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM (for liver only, based on read-across). Overall digestive tract toxicity low.  

4.5 Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Mostly conjugated metabolites → renal elimination.  
• Structural alerts: None for quinone-imine or acetanilide-type nephrotoxins.  
• Read-across data: No histopathological kidney findings in subacute studies of related compounds.  
Conclusion: Nephrotoxicity not anticipated at realistic exposures.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW.

5. Weight-of-Evidence Summary
