• Direct experimental data on CAS 62625-70-1 are lacking; assessment relies on (i) calculated ADME properties, (ii) in-silico toxicity models, and (iii) read-across from close 1,3,4-thiadiazine analogues with limited animal data.  
• High systemic availability is predicted, so any intrinsic hazard would likely manifest if exposure is significant.  
• No compelling signals of specific toxicity to cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive (except adaptive liver changes at high doses), or urinary systems.  
• Principal uncertainty: absence of guideline-compliant repeat-dose and functional organ toxicity studies; moderate probability of undiscovered idiosyncratic effects (e.g., hERG).  

6. Overall conclusion  
Under a conservative screening scenario, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine presents:  
• High systemic availability.  
• Low to medium toxicodynamic activity, with the liver being the most probable target organ at elevated exposures.  
• No clear evidence or strong prediction of harmful effects on cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory or urinary systems at anticipated exposure levels.  

Key data gaps:  
• GLP 28- and 90-day repeat-dose studies with clinical chemistry and histopathology.  
• In vitro hERG assay and cardiovascular safety pharmacology.  
• Human-relevant metabolism profiling to confirm absence of reactive metabolites.  

Given the current evidence base, the overall human health risk to the specified organ systems is expected to be low, provided exposure is controlled. However, definitive hazard classification remains uncertain until targeted experimental studies are conducted.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid; CAS 50-78-2)  
Focus end points: Reproductive (fertility + developmental) and immune systems

1. Substance identity & basic ADME
• MW 180.16 g mol-1, log Kow ≈ 1.2, pKa 3.5 (carboxylic acid).  
• Very well absorbed orally (> 70 % within 1 h); extensive first-pass esterase hydrolysis to salicylic acid, but both parent and metabolite circulate systemically.  
• t½ (salicylate) 2–4 h (low dose) up to 15 h (high dose, saturated metabolism).  
• Crosses placenta and is present in amniotic fluid, fetal plasma and breast milk.  
Systemic availability: HIGH

2. Reproductive end points

2.1 Fertility (male & female)

Human data  
• Epidemiology does not show consistent impairment of male or female fertility in therapeutic users (PubMed ID 26657724, 32267914).  
• Chronic high-dose (>3 g day-1) salicylate therapy was not associated with altered semen parameters in small cohorts (n ≤ 50), but datasets are limited.  
• ECHA registration dossier contains no animal 2-generation study; however, several rodent studies (summarised by EMA 2018) showed no effect on mating performance or litter size up to 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (rat, oral).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: LOW toxicodynamic activity for fertility impairment; uncertainty moderate owing to paucity of modern guideline studies.

2.2 Developmental toxicity / fetal effects

Human epidemiology  
• First and second trimester: meta-analyses (Nakhai-Pour 2011; Li 2019) do not show a general increase in major structural malformations at analgesic doses (<325 mg day-1).  
• Cohort and case-control studies show a modestly elevated risk for early pregnancy loss (RR≈1.6) when >325 mg day-1 is taken peri-implantation (Li 2018).  
• Third trimester: well-documented risks shared with other NSAIDs  
  – Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus, pulmonary hypertension of the newborn  
  – Oligohydramnios and delayed/prolonged labour (FDA Drug Label, 2022)  
• Perinatal/neonatal bleeding (especially intracranial) is increased when full-dose aspirin is taken close to delivery.  
• Low-dose aspirin (75–150 mg day-1) is therapeutically used to prevent pre-eclampsia and recurrent miscarriage; large RCTs (e.g. ASPRE trial, N = 1 776) did not show teratogenicity but slight increase in postpartum haemorrhage.

Animal data  
• Rat, OECD TG 414: maternal NOAEL 50 mg kg-1 day-1; foetal effects (resorptions, skeletal variations) ≥250 mg kg-1 day-1 (Hazleton 1988, cited in ECHA).  
• Mice: cleft palate and exencephaly reported at 150–300 mg kg-1 day-1 (Wilson 1977).  
• Rabbits: embryo-lethality and cardiovascular malformations at 125 mg kg-1 day-1 (Collins 2010).

Regulatory status  
• FDA Pregnancy Category D (benefit–risk dependent); EMA: “contra-indicated from week 28 onwards except under specialist advice”.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
– Early pregnancy (≤ week 20) – MEDIUM developmental hazard at doses >325 mg day-1; LOW at <150 mg day-1.  
– Late pregnancy (≥ week 28) – HIGH developmental/reproductive hazard.

3. Immune system end points

3.1 Immune modulation / suppression  
Mechanism: irreversible inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2, leading to ↓PGE2 and TXA2 and altered cytokine milieu.  

• In vitro: 0.5–5 mM aspirin suppresses NF-κB activation and IL-1β, TNF-α production in human monocytes (Kopp & Ghosh 1994, Nature 368: 191-193).  
• Clinical: therapeutic plasma salicylate concentrations (100–300 µg mL-1 ≈ 0.7–2 mM) reduce fever and inflammatory pain but do not cause clinically significant lymphopenia or immunosuppression.  
Toxicodynamic activity for general immunosuppression – LOW (clinical doses), MEDIUM at high/overdose exposures.

3.2 Hypersensitivity / idiosyncratic responses  
• Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD/N-ERD): prevalence 0.3–0.9 % in general population; mechanism related to shunting of arachidonic acid metabolism toward cysteinyl-leukotrienes.  
• Urticaria/angio-oedema and anaphylaxis: incidence ~1–2 per 10 000 users (summarised in WHO-UMC 2020).  
• These reactions are immune-related but non-IgE-mediated pseudohypersensitivity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM for susceptible sub-population; LOW for general population.

3.3 Reye’s syndrome  
• Idiosyncratic, potentially immune-mediated mitochondrial hepatopathy in children with viral infection; epidemiological link with aspirin use established (FDA boxed warning 1986).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – immune system:  
Overall LOW to MEDIUM hazard. Main concern is hypersensitivity in predisposed individuals rather than broad immunotoxicity.

4. Overall assessment

Toxicodynamic activity (endpoint specific):  
• Fertility: Low  
• Developmental (early): Medium; (late gestation): High  
• Immune function (general): Low; Hypersensitivity: Medium (population-specific)

Systemic availability: High (good absorption; placental transfer).

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No modern two-generation or extended one-generation reproductive toxicity study.  
• Limited human male fertility data; potential confounding by underlying disease.  
• Immune end points largely inferred from pharmacologic mechanism; few dedicated immunotoxicity studies.

6. Risk context & mitigating factors  
• Low-dose aspirin offers clear clinical benefits (anti-platelet, prevention of pre-eclampsia) – careful medical supervision required, especially beyond week 28 of gestation.  
• Alternative antipyretics (paracetamol) recommended for children to avoid Reye’s syndrome.  
• Individuals with asthma, nasal polyps, or chronic urticaria should be screened for AERD before aspirin therapy.

References (representative)  
– ECHA REACH registration dossier for acetylsalicylic acid, 2023 update  
– FDA Drug Label “Aspirin” (DailyMed, 2022)  
– Nakhai-Pour HR et al. Epidemiology 2011;22:290-296.  
– Li J et al. Hum Reprod 2018;33:1770-1780.  
– Kopp E, Ghosh S. Nature 1994;368:191-193.  
– ASPRE Trial (Rolnik DL et al. N Engl J Med 2017;377:613-622).  
– Wilson JG. Teratology Principles 1977.  
– WHO Uppsala Monitoring Centre, Vigibase 2020.

The conclusions above are drawn using a weight-of-evidence approach integrating human epidemiology, animal studies, mechanistic data, and regulatory assessments.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Common names: Paracetamol, Acetaminophen, 4-acetamidophenol  
• CAS No.: 103-90-2  
• Molecular formula: C8H9NO2; MW = 151.16 g mol-1  
• Key physicochemical data (25 °C): log Kow ≈ 0.5; water solubility ≈ 14 g L-1; pKa 9.5  

Systemic availability (expert judgement): HIGH  
• Oral bioavailability in humans ≈ 70–90 % (Forrest 1982; ECHA 2023).  
• Rapid absorption (tmax 0.5–1 h) and extensive distribution to most tissues, including placenta and fetal compartments.  
• Hepatic first-pass metabolism (mainly glucuronidation/sulfation; <10 % CYP-mediated to the reactive N-acetyl-p-benzo-quinone-imine, NAPQI).  
• Elimination t½ ≈ 1.5–2.5 h (longer in neonates).  

Toxicodynamic activity (expert judgement): MEDIUM for reproductive end-points, LOW–MEDIUM for immune end-points.  
Basis: reversible COX-2/PGH2 synthase inhibition, possible endocrine-disrupting potential via reduced prostaglandin and steroidogenesis; glutathione depletion and mild immunomodulation at therapeutic doses.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Reproductive toxicity – fertility
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.1 Human epidemiology  
• Therapeutic use in adults (>100 million patient-years) has not revealed clear fertility impairment. Large prescription databases (e.g., UK Clinical Practice Research Datalink, n > 45 000 couples) did not show reduced conception rates in women exposed to paracetamol monotherapy (Andersen 2022).  
• In subfertile men attending fertility clinics, self-reported frequent paracetamol use showed no consistent alteration in semen quality after adjustment for confounders (Jensen 2016). Limitations: cross-sectional design, recall bias.

1.2 Experimental animals  
• Male rats: 30–100 mg kg-1 day-1 for 28–90 d ↓ testicular weight, intratesticular testosterone, and sperm count; LOAEL ~50 mg kg-1 day-1 (Patil 2021).  
• Female mice: 150 mg kg-1 day-1 GD7-13 → reduced primordial follicle pool in offspring (Holm 2016).  
Mechanism: suppression of fetal testicular/ovarian prostaglandin synthesis; reduced steroidogenic gene expression.

Weight of evidence  
• Evidence in rodents indicates anti-androgenic/anti-estrogenic effects at doses close to, or modestly above, human therapeutic plasma Cmax.  
• Human fertility data are sparse and largely negative.  
Conclusion: Possible but unconfirmed fertility hazard. Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM; uncertainty high.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Developmental toxicity (pregnancy & offspring)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 Placental transfer  
• Cord:maternal plasma ratio ≈ 1 (Ito 1993) → fetus exposed to pharmacologically active levels.

2.2 Human cohort studies  
• Cryptorchidism: two Danish birth cohorts (Andersen 2011, n = 47 000; Snijder 2012) reported OR 1.3–1.5 for maternal use >2 wks in 1st/2nd trimester.  
• Anogenital distance (AGD): shortened AGD in male infants with 2nd-trimester exposure >4 wks (Jensen 2016, N = 273).  
• Neurodevelopment: multiple prospective cohorts (ALSPAC, DNBC, MoBa) link prenatal use (especially >4 wks, third trimester) with higher risk of ADHD-like behaviour and ASD traits (Liew 2014; Ji 2020). Confounding by indication (fever/pain) remains an issue.  
• Fetal growth & malformations: meta-analyses (Epidemiology 2020) show no significant association with major congenital anomalies at therapeutic use.

2.3 Experimental animals  
• Rats: 350 mg kg-1 day-1 GD13–21 → reduced masculinisation index & AGD in male pups (Kristensen 2016).  
• Mice: 150 mg kg-1 day-1 GD7–13 ↓ fetal testosterone, delayed testicular descent (Holm 2016).  
• NOAEL (developmental) ≈ 70 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA REACH dossier).

Mechanistic considerations  
• Paracetamol inhibits COX-derived prostaglandins critical for sexual differentiation, brain development, and immune maturation.  
• Disruption of Leydig cell PGE2 → ↓ testosterone production during masculinisation window.  
• Oxidative stress & glutathione depletion in fetal brain may influence neurodevelopment.

Weight of evidence  
• Consistent experimental findings + biologically plausible mechanisms + epidemiological signals → Suspicion of developmental hazard, especially with prolonged/high use in critical windows.  
Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM to HIGH (developmental). Uncertainty moderate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Immunotoxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1 Human data  
• Early-life exposure (in utero or first year) associated with increased childhood asthma/wheezing (meta-analysis: OR ≈ 1.2–1.3; Garcia-Marcos 2021). Causality debated; confounding by infections/indication likely.  
• Adult therapeutic dosing does not meaningfully alter lymphocyte counts or antibody titres.  
• Overdose/acute liver failure: profound cytokine activation and systemic inflammation, but this is secondary to hepatotoxicity, not direct immune suppression.

3.2 Experimental studies  
• Mice: chronic exposure 100 mg kg-1 day-1 28 d → mild suppression of T-cell proliferation and NK activity (Yoshida 2010).  
• Neonatal mice: single therapeutic-equivalent dose reduced lung glutathione, enhanced Th2 cytokines after allergen challenge (Thorburn 2017).  
• In vitro: NAPQI at ≥10 µM depletes GSH in macrophages, transiently modulating NF-κB signalling; effect reversed by N-acetylcysteine.

Weight of evidence  
• Data suggest modest immunomodulation linked to oxidative stress and prostaglandin blockade, but no clear immunosuppression at therapeutic levels in adults.  
Toxicodynamic activity = LOW–MEDIUM; uncertainty high.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Overall hazard characterisation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Systemic exposure after standard dosing is sufficient to engage COX and cross the placenta.  
• Developmental end-points (male reproductive tract, neurodevelopment) show the strongest evidence base, though still classified as “suspected” rather than proven human hazard (ECHA: Repr. 2; GHS H361d – “Suspected of damaging the unborn child”).  
• Fertility impairment in adults remains unsubstantiated.  
• Immunotoxic potential appears limited and secondary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Data gaps & uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Reliance on self-reported medication use; fever/pain as confounders.  
• Limited information on paternal exposure and offspring outcomes.  
• Lack of chronic immunotoxicity studies in humans.  
• Sparse dose–response data at sub-therapeutic vs therapeutic levels in pregnancy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Risk considerations & mitigating factors
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Therapeutic doses (≤4 g day-1 in adults, ≤60 mg kg-1 day-1 in children) remain the analgesic/antipyretic standard with favourable benefit-risk balance.  
• Clinical guidelines advise using the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration during pregnancy, avoiding chronic (>7 days) use unless medically indicated.  
• Alternative non-pharmacological pain/fever management may further minimise developmental risk.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion (Weight-of-Evidence)  
Paracetamol is highly systemically available and crosses the placenta.  
1. Fertility: Evidence for adult fertility impairment is weak (human) but supported by rodent findings; classified as LOW–MEDIUM concern.  
2. Fetal development: Converging animal and epidemiological data support a MEDIUM–HIGH concern, particularly for prolonged use during the sex-hormone-sensitive “masculinisation window” (weeks 8–14 gestation) and for certain neurodevelopmental endpoints.  
3. Immune function: Only modest, reversible effects anticipated at therapeutic doses; overall LOW–MEDIUM concern.

Additional targeted studies (prospective exposure quantification, mechanistic biomarkers) are needed to refine causal inference and dose–response relationships.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Metformin ‒ CAS No. 657-24-9  
Synonyms: 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide, Metformin hydrochloride (for the marketed salt), Dimethylguanylguanidine  

Scope of the request: Potential biological effects on the human reproductive and immune systems, with emphasis on fertility, fetal development and immunotoxicity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physico-chemical and basic TK (ADME) profile

• Small, highly polar base (log Kow ≈ –1.9; pKa ≈ 12.4) → very low lipophilicity, negligible bio-accumulation.  
• Oral absolute bioavailability ≈ 50–60 % (immediate-release); Cmax 1–3 h post dose.  
• Distribution: Vd 3–5 L/kg, limited plasma-protein binding (<5 %). Crosses the placenta by organic cation transporters (OCTs); cord blood concentrations approach maternal levels (Charles et al., 2006).  
• Metabolism: essentially none; excreted unchanged in urine via OCT2/MATE1 transport.  
Systemic availability: Medium (sufficient to reach systemic circulation and fetal compartment, but rapid renal elimination and no accumulation).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reproductive toxicity

2.1 Experimental animal data  

a) Fertility/early embryonic development  
• Rat: 1-month oral gavage, 600 mg/kg bw/day (≈5× human AUC) induced no adverse effects on male or female fertility (FDA NDA 21-202; summarized in US label).  
• Mouse: Chronic dietary study up to 2 % in feed (~2500 mg/kg bw/day) reported marginal reduction in male testis weight but no impairment of fertility or mating (ECHA dossier, 2019).  
Toxicodynamic activity for fertility (animals): Low.

b) Teratogenicity / prenatal development  
• Rat and rabbit developmental studies at 600 mg/kg bw/day (≈2–6× human exposure) showed no teratogenic malformations. Slight increase in post-implantation loss and reduced fetal weight in rats at maternally toxic doses (>900 mg/kg bw/day) (FDA label; REPROTOX, 2021).  
• No evidence of skeletal or visceral malformations in rabbits.  
Toxicodynamic activity for teratogenicity: Low.

c) Post-natal development  
• Rat peri-/post-natal study (0, 150, 450, 600 mg/kg bw/day) revealed decreased offspring weight at the highest dose, correlating with decreased maternal food intake (FDA dossier). No neuro-behavioral or reproductive sequelae observed.

Uncertainties: Only standard guideline studies; no multi-generation study. Highest doses exceed GI tolerability limits; systemic margins therefore adequate.

2.2 Human data  

a) Female fertility  
• Widely prescribed for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) to restore ovulation. Randomized trials and meta-analyses (e.g., Moll et al., Hum. Reprod. Update 2023) show improved ovulation rate and live birth compared with placebo. No signal of infertility or ovarian toxicity.  
• Mechanism: indirect endocrine modulation (decreased insulin and androgen levels) rather than gonadal cytotoxicity.

b) Male fertility  
• Limited clinical information.  
• Cross-sectional study in type-2 diabetic men (N ≈ 100) found slightly reduced total testosterone and sperm concentration compared with diabetic men on other antidiabetics (Yang et al., Andrology 2021), but confounding by diabetes cannot be excluded.  
• Rodent studies at supra-therapeutic doses occasionally report decreased spermatid counts (Giménez et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2012). The findings were reversible and accompanied by marked systemic stress.  
Weight-of-evidence: No consistent evidence that metformin impairs male fertility at therapeutic exposures; data set, however, is smaller than for females → Uncertainty: moderate.

c) Pregnancy outcome / fetal development  
• >15 controlled cohort studies and three meta-analyses comparing metformin vs. insulin in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) or continued use in women with type-2 diabetes. Key findings (e.g., Butalia et al., Diabet. Med. 2017; Zhao et al., BMC Med. 2022):  
  – No increase in major congenital malformations, spontaneous abortion or perinatal mortality.  
  – Slight reduction in maternal weight gain and neonatal hypoglycaemia vs. insulin.  
  – Some studies show marginally lower birth weight and increased risk of small-for-gestational-age, possibly related to reduced maternal hyperglycaemia.  
• Long-term follow-up of offspring (MiG-TOFU study; Rowan et al., Diabetes Care 2018, age 9 years) did not detect differences in metabolic or neuro-developmental indices.  
• Registry data (Finnish, Danish, Canadian) encompassing >10 000 first-trimester exposures do not indicate teratogenic signals (Wensink et al., BJOG 2019).  
Overall reproductive hazard classification (human): Low. Metformin is generally considered safe in pregnancy by major guidelines (ADA, NICE), albeit with advisory to monitor fetal growth.  

Data gaps:  
• Placental transporter polymorphisms may modulate fetal exposure; limited data.  
• Lack of data in very early embryonic exposure prior to pregnancy recognition.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Immunotoxicity / immune modulation

3.1 Mechanistic considerations  

Metformin activates AMPK and secondarily inhibits mTORC1 and NF-κB pathways, which can dampen inflammatory responses and modulate innate/adaptive immunity.

3.2 In vitro / in silico  

• Human PBMCs: 0.1–1 mM metformin reduces IL-1β, IL-6 and TNF-α after LPS stimulation (Moiseeva et al., Aging Cell 2013).  
• QSAR and ToxCast assays: No structural alerts for immunosuppression or cytokine storm; high-throughput data suggest anti-proliferative effects in activated T-cells only at millimolar concentrations (EPA ToxCast phase III).  

3.3 Animal models  

• Mouse models of autoimmune encephalomyelitis, lupus and rheumatoid arthritis: oral 200 mg/kg attenuated disease scores, decreased Th17/Treg ratio (Lee et al., J. Immunol. 2017). No lymphoid organ atrophy or cytopenias.  
• Sub-chronic oral studies in rats (90 days up to 900 mg/kg) showed no changes in spleen weight, hematology or lymphocyte subsets (ECHA dossier, key study).  
Interpretation: Metformin exhibits pharmaco-logical immuno-modulation but not overt immunotoxicity.

3.4 Human clinical data  

• Large pharmaco-epidemiological studies (>100 000 T2DM patients) show reduced incidence of infections such as pneumonia and tuberculosis compared with sulfonylureas (Singh et al., Diabetes Care 2020). Confounding by glycaemic control cannot be fully ruled out.  
• Randomized controlled trials in COVID-19 (TOGETHER, 2022) indicate modest reduction in progression/severity, supporting anti-inflammatory action; no evidence of immunosuppression.  
• No signals of leukopenia, hypogammaglobulinaemia or opportunistic infections in >60 years of pharmacovigilance.  
Immunotoxic risk classification: Low; immunomodulatory benefits predominate.

Uncertainties:  
• Long-term immune effects in non-diabetic populations under investigation for anti-aging indications.  
• Limited data on vaccine response, although preliminary studies show no impairment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Weight-of-evidence summary and expert judgement

Toxicodynamic activity  
• Reproductive toxicity: Low (beneficial on female fertility; no teratogenic signal; equivocal/weak findings on male fertility at high doses).  
• Immunotoxicity: Low (pharmacological anti-inflammatory activity without evidence of clinically relevant immunosuppression).  

Systemic availability  
• Medium (adequate systemic and placental passage but short half-life, no accumulation).

Overall hazard characterization  
1. Fertility: No adverse effects at therapeutic exposure; possible benefit in females with PCOS; uncertain mild male effects at supra-therapeutic doses – monitor if high-dose off-label use in males is anticipated.  
2. Fetal development: Not teratogenic; may slightly reduce fetal growth; benefits vs. maternal hyperglycaemia generally outweigh risks.  
3. Immune system: Exhibits mild anti-inflammatory/immunomodulatory properties; no evidence of immunotoxicity.

Risk characterization  
For intended human exposures (≤2 g/day orally), margin of safety versus animal NOAELs (450–600 mg/kg bw/day) ≥20-fold. Given the long clinical history and extensive pregnancy data, overall reproductive and immune risk is rated low.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Key references

• FDA. Metformin hydrochloride product label, NDA 21-202, 2022 revision.  
• ECHA. Dossier for CAS 657-24-9, accessed March 2024.  
• Moll E. et al., Hum. Reprod. Update 2023; 29: 36-54.  
• Butalia S. et al., Diabet. Med. 2017; 34: 27-36.  
• Charles B. et al., BJOG 2006; 113: 753-758.  
• Giménez B. et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2012; 265: 94-103.  
• Lee M. et al., J. Immunol. 2017; 198: 1935-1945.  
• Singh S. et al., Diabetes Care 2020; 43: 2349-2358.  
• Rowan J. et al., Diabetes Care 2018; 41: 1907-1913.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Remaining data gaps and recommendations

• Conduct dedicated male fertility studies in healthy volunteers, including semen parameters and hormonal profiling.  
• Further research on transporter polymorphisms affecting fetal/metformin exposure.  
• Long-term follow-up of offspring into adolescence to detect subtle metabolic or immune alterations.  
• Immunotoxicity ancillary endpoints (T-dependent antibody response) in any future long-term toxicology studies intended for new indications.

End of assessment

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5)  
Focus end points: reproduction (fertility + developmental toxicity) and immune function

1. Identity & basic physicochemical/ADME profile  
• Synonyms: Atorvastatin; Atorvastatin calcium; CI-981; Lipitor®  
• MW (free acid): 558.6 g/mol; log Kow ≈ 4.0 (moderately lipophilic)  
• Oral absorption: rapid; absolute bioavailability ~14 % because of extensive first-pass extraction to liver (primary site of action).  
• Protein binding > 98 %.  
• Metabolism: mainly CYP3A4 oxidation → active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites (+ lactone inter-conversion).  
• Elimination t½ parent ≈ 14 h; active metabolites 20–30 h.  
• Placental passage and distribution into milk demonstrated in animals; limited, indirect evidence in humans (label).  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM (efficient absorption but strong hepatic first pass and high protein binding restrict systemic free concentration).

2. Mode of action relevant for the assessed organ systems  
Primary pharmacodynamics: competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA-reductase → ↓ mevalonate pathway → ↓ cholesterol and downstream isoprenoids (farnesyl-PP, geranylgeranyl-PP).  
Toxicodynamic considerations for reproduction and immunity:  
• Cholesterol is essential for steroidogenesis, cell-membrane integrity, embryonic development (sonic hedgehog signalling).  
• Isoprenoid depletion modulates small G-proteins → pleiotropic immunomodulatory effects.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity for the endpoints of interest: HIGH.

3. Reproductive toxicity

3.1 Fertility  
Animal data  
• Rats: Oral dosing 10–300 mg/kg d (≈ 10–100× human AUC). M/F fertility not overtly impaired at ≤100 mg/kg, but males showed reduced testicular/epididymal weights and sperm motility at ≥100 mg/kg (Pfizer pre-clinical dossier, FDA review 1996).  
• Rabbits: decreases in testicular weight at 100 mg/kg.  
• Mechanistic studies in rodents: ↓ intratesticular testosterone and down-regulated StAR/3β-HSD (Karli 2021, Soltani 2013).

Human data  
• Limited and heterogeneous. Most clinical trials exclude women of child-bearing potential and do not collect semen data.  
• Small observational studies (n < 100) report minor, reversible reductions of total testosterone and sperm count; others show no change or even slight improvement related to metabolic health (Mareev 2020).  
Weight-of-evidence: Possible mild anti-fertility effect at high exposures in animals; no consistent signal in humans at therapeutic doses. Classification: LOW–MEDIUM hazard for fertility.

3.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity  
Animal data (FDA label, ECHA dossier):  
• Rats: 20, 100, 225 mg/kg d GD 6–17 → ↓ fetal body weight, delayed ossification, post-implantation loss at ≥100 mg/kg. Placental/fetal drug levels up to 30 % of maternal.  
• Rabbits: ↑ fetal resorptions and skeletal anomalies at 30 mg/kg d (approx. 10× human AUC).  
Human data  
• Contra-indicated in pregnancy (former FDA Cat. X; EMA “must not be used”).  
• Observational cohorts:  
  – Ofori 2007 (n +477 exposed, Quebec): adjusted OR for major malformations 1.34 (95 % CI 0.76–2.36) – not significant but study underpowered.  
  – Bateman 2015, NEJM (n 886 first-trimester statin exposure, ≈ one-third on atorvastatin): risk ratio for malformations 1.04 (CI 0.82–1.32) after controlling for diabetes, hypertension, obesity.  
  – Later meta-analysis (Chen 2023, 8 cohort studies): pooled OR 1.09 (0.85–1.39) – no clear signal; however, designs prone to confounding by indication.  
Mechanistic plausibility (cholesterol dependence of the embryo) + positive animal findings → regulators retain the contraindication despite equivocal human epidemiology.

Weight-of-evidence: Developmental hazard judged HIGH (precautionary approach).

4. Immune system effects

4.1 Immunomodulation (beneficial / pharmacological)  
• Decreased CRP and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) in multiple RCTs.  
• In vitro: atorvastatin (0.1–10 µM) reduces MHC-II expression, co-stimulatory molecules (Marracci 2004) and promotes Treg differentiation via FOXP3 up-regulation (Rosenson 2004).  
These effects are generally considered protective in cardiovascular disease and some autoimmune settings.

4.2 Immune-related adverse effects  
• Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM): rare incidence ~2–3/100,000 patient-years; strongly associated with anti-HMG-CoA-reductase auto-antibodies (Needham 2014). Often requires immunosuppression.  
• Hypersensitivity: isolated reports of anaphylaxis, angio-oedema, lupus-like syndrome (FDA ADR database).  
• Infection risk: No consistent elevation in opportunistic infections in large cardiovascular trials (e.g., TNT, SPARCL).  

Weight-of-evidence: Overall immunomodulatory activity is MEDIUM (mostly anti-inflammatory; rare serious autoimmune events).

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Lack of controlled human fertility studies – available data are small and possibly subject to confounding.  
• Pregnancy data rely on observational cohorts; impossible to exclude moderate risk increases (<2-fold).  
• Long-term immunological surveillance data limited beyond CV trial durations.  
• Placental transfer kinetics in humans not quantified.

6. Overall conclusions  

Fertility: Low–medium hazard. High-dose animal studies show testicular effects; human therapeutic exposures have not demonstrated consistent impairment.

Developmental toxicity: High hazard. Animal malformations/resorptions, mechanistic plausibility, and regulatory consensus justify contraindication during pregnancy and lactation.

Immune system: Medium hazard/benefit profile. Atorvastatin exerts anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects that are largely beneficial but carries a low incidence of serious autoimmune myopathy and hypersensitivity.

Systemic availability: Medium (high absorption but hepatic sequestration/protein binding reduce systemic free drug).

Risk management recommendations  
• Contraindicated in pregnancy and during breastfeeding unless alternative lipid-lowering strategies inadequate and benefit outweighs risk.  
• Advise effective contraception for women of reproductive potential; discontinue therapy ≥1 month before planned conception.  
• Monitor CK in patients reporting muscle symptoms; if markedly elevated with weakness consider anti-HMGCR antibody testing.  
• No routine fertility or immune monitoring required in otherwise healthy adults, but clinicians should be alert to unexplained myalgia, rash, or constitutional symptoms suggestive of autoimmune disease.

Cited key sources  
1. US FDA. Atorvastatin Label (revised 2021).  
2. European Chemicals Agency (ECHA). Atorvastatin calcium dossier, accessed 2024.  
3. Bateman BT et al. Statins and congenital malformations. N Engl J Med 2015;373:2088–90.  
4. Ofori B et al. Statins and congenital anomalies. Birth Defects Res A 2007;79:353–8.  
5. Karli R et al. Atorvastatin impairs steroidogenesis and sperm parameters in rats. Andrologia 2021;53:e13950.  
6. Needham M et al. Statin‐associated immune-mediated myopathy. Curr Opin Rheumatol 2014;26:734–40.  
7. Rosenson RS. Statins in inflammatory disorders. Lancet 2004;363:468–70.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Prednisone (synonyms: Δ¹-dehydrocortisone, Sterapred; CAS No. 53-03-2)

1. Substance identity and key properties  
   • Therapeutic pro-drug rapidly converted in vivo to the active glucocorticoid prednisolone.  
   • Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 1.5–2.3), moderate plasma-protein binding (≈ 70–90 %).  
   • Systemic bioavailability: high after oral dosing (≥ 70 %), t½ ≈ 2–4 h (prednisone) and 2–3 h (prednisolone); elimination mainly hepatic metabolism followed by renal excretion of conjugates.  
   • Widely used clinically at 2–60 mg day⁻¹ (short-term) and 5–15 mg day⁻¹ (long-term).

2. Systemic availability (ADME-based score): HIGH  
   – Rapid and extensive absorption, active metabolite in systemic circulation, distribution into most tissues (including placenta; breast milk).

3. Mechanistic background (toxicodynamic activity)  
   – Classic glucocorticoid receptor (GR) agonist → genomic and non-genomic regulation of > 1 000 genes.  
   – Down-regulates pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), inhibits NF-κB; induces lymphocyte apoptosis; interferes with hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis feedback.  
   – Influences fetal organogenesis by altering growth factors, extracellular matrix enzymes, and endocrine signalling.

Overall toxicodynamic activity score: HIGH (well-established, therapeutically exploited).

4. Immune system effects

4.1 Human data (clinical & epidemiology)  
   • Controlled trials and decades of pharmacovigilance unequivocally show dose-dependent immunosuppression (Coutinho & Chapman 2011; Schäcke et al. 2022).  
   • Effects include:  
     – Lymphopenia (T > B cells) within hours after dosing.  
     – Neutrophilia (demargination) but impaired neutrophil chemotaxis and oxidative burst.  
     – Suppression of antibody responses; reduced vaccine efficacy (CDC vaccination guidelines 2023).  
     – Diminished cell-mediated immunity, leading to increased risk of infections (e.g., tuberculosis re-activation, varicella).  
   • Long-term use (> 7.5 mg prednisolone-equivalent ≥ 3 months) is associated with:  
     – Opportunistic infections (risk ratio 2–7 depending on pathogen; Dixon et al. 2018).  
     – Reactivation of latent viral hepatitis (Yoo et al. 2019).  

4.2 Animal & in-vitro supporting data  
   • Mice: 1–10 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ for 14 d decreased splenic weight, thymic cellularity, T-cell proliferation (Kim et al. 2015).  
   • In vitro: IC₅₀ for inhibition of human peripheral blood mononuclear cell proliferation ≈ 10⁻⁷ M (Zhang et al. 2013).

Conclusion – Immune system: Clear, well-documented immunosuppressive agent. Hazard classification: HIGH.

5. Reproductive system and developmental toxicity

5.1 Fertility (male & female)  
   Human information is limited and confounded by underlying diseases.  
   • No consistent epidemiological signal for impaired fertility at therapeutic doses (ECHA registration dossier 2021; FDA label).  
   • Case reports of reversible oligospermia and menstrual disturbances, usually at doses > 20 mg day⁻¹ for ≥ 2 months. Mechanism likely HPA axis cross-talk with hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis.  
   Animal data  
   • Rats & rabbits: No effect on mating performance or litter size up to 50 µg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (low NOAEL); higher doses (≥ 1 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) produced decreased testosterone and ovarian weight but still no fertility impairment (Abbott 1977; EPA RED).  
   Weight-of-evidence: Fertility hazard LOW to MEDIUM (only at supratherapeutic or chronic high-dose exposure).

5.2 Developmental (embryo-fetal) toxicity  
   Human observational studies  
   • Meta-analysis of 14 617 pregnancies exposed to systemic corticosteroids during organogenesis found a small but statistically significant increase in oral clefts (OR 1.45, 95 % CI 1.11–1.89) (Park-Wyllie et al. 2011). Absolute risk remains low (≈ 0.3 % vs 0.2 %).  
   • High-dose, prolonged therapy in 2nd/3rd trimester associated with intrauterine growth restriction, pre-term delivery, and transient neonatal adrenal suppression (Murphy & Gibson 2011; ACOG 2020).  
   • No consistent evidence for neurodevelopmental deficits.  
   Animal studies  
   • Cleft palate, skeletal malformations, and fetal loss in mice, rats, rabbits, hamsters at 0.1–1 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ during gastrulation (Kang et al. 2017). LOAEL often overlaps with human therapeutic range after allometric scaling.  
   Mechanistic considerations  
   • Glucocorticoid-induced apoptosis in palatal mesenchyme; disruption of Shh and TGF-β signalling.  
   Weight-of-evidence: Developmental hazard MEDIUM. Risk in clinical use mitigated by careful dose-timing management.

5.3 Lactation  
   • Prednisone is excreted in breast milk at low levels (infant dose ≈ 0.1 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹). Breast-feeding is generally considered compatible when maternal dose ≤ 20 mg day⁻¹ or after waiting 4 h post-dose; higher doses may transiently suppress infant adrenal function (WHO 2002 guideline).

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • Limited controlled fertility studies in humans; reliance on case reports.  
   • Confounding by underlying diseases requiring glucocorticoids (autoimmune disorders).  
   • Sparse long-term developmental follow-up beyond early childhood.  
   • No modern OECD guideline-compliant multigeneration studies.

7. Overall toxicological conclusions

Immune system  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Systemic availability: HIGH  
   Level of concern: Well-established immunosuppressant; risk proportional to dose–duration.

Reproductive system  
   • Fertility: LOW–MEDIUM hazard; clinically relevant effects are uncommon and mostly reversible.  
   • Developmental toxicity: MEDIUM hazard; minor but measurable increase in cleft palate and growth effects at therapeutic doses, pronounced malformations in animals; risk management feasible with lowest-effective dose, alternate-day or post-organogenesis use.  

Risk management and regulatory status  
   • Pregnancy category (US FDA prior system): Category C / D depending on indication.  
   • EMA & ACOG recommend individualized risk-benefit assessment; use is acceptable when maternal benefit outweighs potential fetal harm.  
   • Vaccination guidelines: Avoid live vaccines during systemic prednisone ≥ 20 mg day⁻¹ for > 14 days.

8. References (key)  
   – Park-Wyllie L et al. Corticosteroid use during pregnancy and risk of orofacial clefts. CMAJ 2011; 183:E1-E5.  
   – Coutinho AE, Chapman KE. The anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects of glucocorticoids. Clin Sci 2011;121:111-121.  
   – Dixon WG et al. Glucocorticoid therapy and risk of serious infection. Rheumatology 2018;57:1800-1809.  
   – Kang HJ et al. Mechanisms of glucocorticoid-induced cleft palate. Birth Defects Res 2017;109:905-917.  
   – Murphy VE, Gibson PG. Corticosteroid use in pregnancy: Asthma and beyond. Clin Exp Allergy 2011;41:295-302.  
   – CDC. General Best Practice Guidelines for Immunization, 2023.  
   – ECHA-REACH registration dossier: Prednisone, 2021.  
   – FDA. Prescribing Information – Prednisone tablets (2022).  
   – Schäcke H, Döcke WD, Asadullah K. Mechanisms involved in the side effects of glucocorticoids. Pharmacol Ther 2022.

Prepared with a weight-of-evidence approach; uncertainties acknowledged where data are limited.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)
CAS No. 1746-01-6

1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical properties
• Planar, highly lipophilic chlorinated aromatic (log Kow ≈ 7.0)
• Practically insoluble in water; vapor pressure extremely low
• Very resistant to biotic and abiotic degradation
Implication: once absorbed it partitions into lipid-rich tissues (adipose, liver, breast milk) and persists for years (human t½ ≈ 7–11 years).

2. Systemic availability (ADME profile) – HIGH
Absorption: 40–90 % after oral intake in animals; limited experimental human data but absorption from contaminated food is assumed to be similarly high. Dermal uptake slower but significant with prolonged contact.
Distribution: Strong binding to plasma lipoproteins; Vd ≈ 6 L kg-1; crosses the placenta and enters breast milk.
Metabolism / Excretion: Slow CYP1A2-mediated oxidation followed by conjugation; fecal elimination is rate-limiting. Biomagnifies along food chains.
Overall systemic availability rated HIGH with accumulation during chronic exposure, including fetal exposure in utero and during lactation.

3. Toxicodynamic profile
Primary molecular initiating event: high-affinity agonism of the aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR). AhR activation elicits:
• Altered transcription of xenobiotic-metabolizing enzymes (CYP1A, CYP1B)  
• Cross-talk with estrogen, androgen, thyroid, glucocorticoid, and immune-related signalling.
Adverse outcome pathways for TCDD cover developmental toxicity, immunotoxicity, carcinogenicity, and endocrine disruption.

Toxicodynamic activity for the immune and reproductive systems is classified as HIGH based on potencies in vitro (pM-nM range), sensitive LOAELs in vivo (≤ 0.1 µg kg-1 bw d-1 for some outcomes) and concordant human epidemiology.

4. Reproductive and developmental effects

4.1 Human data
• Occupational cohorts (e.g. chemical production workers, herbicide applicators) and Seveso residents show dose-related alterations in semen quality: ↓ sperm concentration and motility, ↑ abnormal morphology at serum TCDD ≥ 50 pg g-1 lipid (Mocarelli et al., 2008; 2011; EPA 2023 IRIS).
• Altered sex ratio at birth (fewer males) in highly exposed fathers (Mocarelli et al., 2000; Ryan et al., 2002).
• Epidemiological signals for prolonged time-to-pregnancy, dysmenorrhoea and earlier menopause, but results are heterogeneous (Eskenazi et al., 2018 review).
• Developmental outcomes in Seveso and Agent Orange offspring: slight, exposure-related decreases in birth weight/length and later pubertal timing; congenital malformations not consistently elevated after adjustment for co-exposures. Confidence: moderate because of exposure-assessment uncertainties.
• Breast-fed infants from contaminated areas receive an estimated 1–10 ng kg-1 bw day-1 during early lactation; transient elevations in liver enzymes and thyroid hormones reported, though long-term consequences remain unclear (Korrick et al., 2014).

4.2 Animal data (supportive, higher certainty)
• Rat, mouse, hamster, guinea pig, monkey studies show decreased fertility, extended estrous cycles, pre- and post-implantation loss, and reduced litters at maternal doses as low as 0.03 µg kg-1 bw day-1 (Tyl et al., 1998).
• Classic terata: cleft palate, kidney defects, hydronephrosis, digit/musculoskeletal malformations, primarily in mice (Khera & Ruddick 1973).
• Profound postnatal effects: reduced anogenital distance, feminisation of male pups, delayed sexual maturation, permanent reduction in sperm counts.
• Fetal & neonatal LOAELs frequently at 0.064–0.16 µg kg-1 bw (single gavage on gestation day 15), highlighting extreme potency.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion for reproduction/development
• Human evidence: LIMITED to SUFFICIENT for altered male fertility parameters and sex-ratio changes; LIMITED for direct effects on female fertility and developmental endpoints.  
• Animal evidence: SUFFICIENT and highly concordant.  
Overall hazard classification: TCDD is a potent reproductive and developmental toxicant.

5. Immunological effects

5.1 Human observations
• Seveso adults with serum TCDD > 100 pg g-1 lipid exhibited lowered peripheral CD4+ counts and altered Th1/Th2 cytokine profiles decades post-exposure (Baccarelli et al., 2002).
• Children highly exposed in utero or during lactation showed reduced antibody responses to diphtheria and tetanus vaccination at 18 months (Tobias et al., 2019; Grandjean et al., 2012).
• Increased incidence of respiratory infections and otitis media reported in Vietnamese children (Phuong et al., 2016) living in dioxin hotspots.
• Occupational studies reveal elevated markers of chronic inflammation (CRP, IL-6) and autoimmune phenomena (e.g., altered ANA titres); causal inference complicated by co-exposures.

5.2 Animal data
• TCDD is a reference immunosuppressant in rodents: thymic atrophy, ↓ T-dependent antibody response (TDAR), ↓ CTL activity, impaired NK function at doses​ ≤ 0.1 µg kg-1 bw (Kerkvliet 2009).
• Increased susceptibility to viral (influenza), bacterial (Listeria), and parasitic infections documented.
• Enhanced autoimmune disease in genetically prone strains, indicating both immunosuppression and dysregulation.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion for immunotoxicity
• Human data: SUFFICIENT for modulation of immune function (vaccine response) and suggestive for increased infections.  
• Animal data: SUFFICIENT, mechanistically coherent (AhR-dependent).  
Overall, TCDD is a potent immunotoxicant.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Epidemiologic studies often rely on historical serum reconstruction; residual confounding by other persistent organochlorines cannot be fully ruled out.
• Human developmental toxicity data are limited by small cohort sizes and mixed exposures.
• Genetic and nutritional modifiers of AhR sensitivity in humans remain under-studied.

7. Overall assessment (weight of evidence)
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for both reproductive and immune systems.
Systemic availability: HIGH due to efficient absorption, strong bioaccumulation, and very long biological half-life.
Risk context: At background population TEQ body burdens (≈ 5–10 pg g-1 lipid) margin-of-exposure for sensitive endpoints (vaccine antibody suppression, sperm quality) is narrow (≈ 10–30), justifying current international efforts to further lower dietary intake (WHO TDI 2 pg TEQ kg-1 bw week-1; EFSA 2018).

8. Key references
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012) – 2,3,7,8-TCDD.  
• U.S. EPA IRIS Reassessment (2023).  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Chlorinated Dibenzo-p-dioxins (2022).  
• Eskenazi et al. Environ. Health (2018) 17:43 – Seveso cohort review.  
• Mocarelli et al. Lancet (2008, 2011) – Sperm alterations/semen quality.  
• Kerkvliet, Toxicol. Sci. (2009) – Mechanistic immunotoxicity review.  
• Grandjean et al. Environ. Health Perspect. (2012) – Vaccine antibody suppression.  
• EFSA CONTAM Panel (2018) – Risk assessment of dioxins and DL-PCBs in food.  

Confidence statement
The conclusions are supported by a robust body of experimental animal work, mechanistic insight (AhR pathway), and multiple human cohorts with quantified serum TCDD. Remaining uncertainties relate mainly to quantification of effect magnitude at current background exposures and inter-individual susceptibility.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Benzene (synonyms: cyclohexatriene, benzol, phenyl hydride)  
CAS No.: 71-43-2

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

Absorption  
• Inhalation: 50–80 % of inspired benzene is absorbed at rest; uptake increases with exercise (ATSDR, 2007).  
• Dermal: Rapid dermal penetration (Kp ≈ 0.12–0.18 cm h-1); occlusion markedly increases uptake (Johanson & Dynesius-Trentham, 1988).  
• Oral: Nearly complete (>90 %) absorption in rats and mice (Sabourin et al., 1987).

Distribution  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow 2.13) ⇒ rapid distribution to fat, liver, bone marrow, reproductive organs.  
• Crosses the placenta (Ghantous & Danielsson, 1986).

Metabolism  
• Predominantly hepatic CYP2E1 → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, benzene oxide, muconic acid; bone-marrow–specific secondary metabolism (myeloperoxidase, prostaglandin-H synthase) generates reactive quinones.  
• Polymorphisms in CYP2E1, NQO1, GSTT1 modulate internal dose and susceptibility.

Excretion  
• Unchanged benzene in exhaled air; metabolites in urine (t½ for t,t-muconic acid ≈ 6 h).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid multi-route uptake, distribution to target tissues, formation of reactive metabolites).

2. Reproductive toxicity

Human data  
• Occupational studies (petrochemical, shoe manufacturing):  
  – Carcinogenicity cohorts occasionally report increased spontaneous abortion or reduced birth weight at mean airborne levels of 3–30 ppm, but exposure misclassification and co-exposures are common (Chen et al., 2000; Taskinen et al., 1994).  
  – Cross-sectional study of 522 female workers (median 4.2 ppm) showed longer menstrual cycles and lower progesterone (Wu et al., 2010).  
  – A study of 180 male workers (median 2.8 ppm) found lower sperm concentration and motility; association stronger in GSTT1-null individuals (Xing et al., 2010).  
• Epidemiological evidence judged “limited” by IARC (2018) and ECHA (dossier update 2021); not sufficient for CLP Repr.1 classification.

Animal data  
• Fertility:  
  – Mice inhalation 0, 100, 300 ppm (6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 8 wk): males showed ↓ testis weight, ↓ sperm count, increased abnormal sperm; mating success reduced at 300 ppm (Olsen et al., 1984).  
  – Female rats inhalation 0, 1, 10, 100 ppm (6 h/d GD 6–15): no effect on implantation or litter size; maternal toxicity at 100 ppm (Murray et al., 1979).

• Development:  
  – In mice, 500 ppm (6 h/d, GD 6–15) caused ↓ fetal weight, ↑ skeletal variations; effects occurred only with maternal toxicity.  
  – Neurobehavioral alterations (↓ startle response) reported in offspring of CD-1 mice exposed to 300 ppm during gestation (Gill et al., 1989).

Mechanistic considerations  
• Benzene metabolites produce oxidative DNA damage and protein adducts in germ cells.  
• Aromatic benzene metabolites may disrupt endocrine signaling (in vitro anti-estrogenic effects at 10-5 M; Zhang et al., 2012).  
• Epigenetic changes (DNA hypomethylation in imprinted genes) observed in cord blood of mothers occupationally exposed (Bollati et al., 2010).

Weight-of-evidence judgement (fertility / development)  
• Consistent animal evidence at relatively high doses; human data suggest possible effects but are inconsistent and often confounded.  
Toxicodynamic activity on reproduction: MEDIUM.  
Critical data gaps: well-designed epidemiological studies below 1 ppm; mechanistic confirmation of endocrine disruption.

3. Immunotoxicity / hematotoxicity

Human data  
• Benzene is a well-established hematopoietic toxicant at low levels (≥1 ppm).  
• Occupational cohorts show dose-related decreases in total WBC, neutrophils, B-cells, CD4+ and CD8+ T-cells at mean exposures 1.6 ppm (Lan et al., 2004, China).  
• Increased serum IgA and IgG class-switch defects reported in refinery workers (Qu et al., 2002).  
• Elevated infections and delayed wound healing observed among shoe-factory workers (median 7 ppm) (Rothman et al., 1997).  
• Epidemiological association with aplastic anemia and non-Hodgkin lymphoma further confirms immune system involvement.

Animal data  
• Mice inhalation 10–100 ppm (6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 4-13 wk):  
   – ↓ bone-marrow cellularity, ↓ colony-forming units, lymphopenia.  
   – Suppressed IgM plaque-forming response to sheep RBC (Rozen et al., 1984).  
• NK-cell activity and cytotoxic T-cell responses depressed in mice after 50 ppm for 6 wk (Wang et al., 2015).  
• NOAEL for hematotoxicity in rats ~10 ppm; LOAEL ~30 ppm.

Mechanistic considerations  
• Reactive quinones form protein adducts in hematopoietic stem cells, cause oxidative stress and topoisomerase II inhibition → apoptosis / clastogenicity.  
• Altered cytokine milieu (↑ IL-6, TNF-α) and deregulated hematopoietic transcription factors (RUNX1, GATA2).  
• Benzene-induced immune suppression secondary to bone-marrow damage; direct effects on peripheral lymphocytes also demonstrated in vitro (EC50 ≈ 50 µM for proliferation inhibition).

Weight-of-evidence judgement (immune system)  
• Robust human and animal data, mechanistic plausibility, dose-response demonstrated below 1 ppm.  
Toxicodynamic activity on immune system: HIGH.

4. Overall conclusions

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption by all routes, distribution to bone marrow, gonads, placenta; formation of electrophilic metabolites).  

Reproductive effects: Animal studies show fertility and developmental toxicity at ≥100 ppm (inhalation) concurrent with maternal toxicity; human data suggest possible menstrual disturbances, decreased sperm quality, and increased risk of spontaneous abortion at ≥3 ppm but are inconsistent. The current weight of evidence supports MEDIUM concern, warranting exposure minimisation, especially for pregnant workers.

Immunotoxic effects: Strong, reproducible bone-marrow and immune suppression in humans and animals at low occupational levels (≈1 ppm). Classified as HIGH concern.

Uncertainties & research needs  
• Better exposure characterisation in epidemiological studies below 1 ppm.  
• Clarification of endocrine-disrupting potential and epigenetic effects in germ cells.  
• Long-term immune consequences (e.g., infection susceptibility, autoimmunity) at environmental exposure levels.

Regulatory context  
• IARC: Group 1 carcinogen (sufficient evidence in humans).  
• EU CLP: Carc.1A (H350), Muta.1B (H340); no harmonised reproductive hazard classification, but several Member State dossiers propose Repr.2 (H361d).  
• OSHA PEL 1 ppm (8 h TWA); ACGIH TLV 0.5 ppm. Maintaining exposures well below 1 ppm is advisable to protect both immune and potential reproductive endpoints.

References (selected)  
• Lan et al. 2004. Blood 104: 4063-4069.  
• IARC Monograph 120. 2018.  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2007 (update draft 2022).  
• Murray et al. 1979. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 50: 189-196.  
• Olsen et al. 1984. Environ Res 32: 270-278.  
• Rothman et al. 1997. Environ Health Perspect 105(Suppl 6): 1369-1372.  
• Wu et al. 2010. Occup Environ Med 67: 467-470.  
(Additional citations within text)

This assessment follows a weight-of-evidence approach integrating human, animal, and mechanistic data.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III); CAS 15502-74-6)  
Synonyms frequently used in the literature: arsenious acid, arsenite ion, arsenic(III), sodium arsenite* (*most in-vivo data are generated with sodium arsenite; toxicodynamic behavior is driven by the As(III) moiety, therefore the information is applicable to arsenite ion per se).

1. Systemic Availability (ADME) – HIGH  
• Absorption: ∼80–95 % of ingested As(III) is rapidly absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (ATSDR 2007; NRC 2001). Dermal uptake is lower but still measurable; inhalation absorption is also high (≥70 %).  
• Distribution: After absorption, As(III) is distributed via blood, crosses the placenta, and is found in fetal tissues and breast milk (Concha 1998; Hall 2007).  
• Metabolism/Excretion: As(III) undergoes methylation to MMA(V) and DMA(V) in liver and other tissues; metabolites and part of the parent compound are eliminated mainly in urine (t½ ≈ 10–30 h for the first phase, but bio-retention in keratinous tissues is weeks–months).  
→ Conclusion: High bioavailability and efficient systemic distribution, including trans-placental transfer, support a HIGH rating for systemic availability.

2. Toxicodynamic Activity – HIGH  
• Mechanistic hallmarks: oxidative stress, interaction with zinc-finger proteins, inhibition of DNA repair enzymes, epigenetic alterations, endocrine-like effects (particularly estrogen and glucocorticoid receptor interference), and immunomodulation through NF-κB, MAPK and Nrf2 pathways.  
• These mechanisms are relevant to both reproductive and immune endpoints.

3. Reproductive System Effects  

3.1 Fertility (male & female)  
Human evidence  
• Epidemiology from Bangladesh, Taiwan and Mexico links drinking-water As(III) (≥50 µg/L) with increased risk of spontaneous abortion, stillbirth, and reduced birth rate (Milton 2005; Ahmed 2011).  
• Cross-sectional biomonitoring of Bangladeshi men showed inverse associations between urinary arsenic and sperm motility/concentration (Hossain 2010).  
• Occupational studies of smelter workers exposed to airborne arsenic (predominantly As(III)) reported decreased testosterone and increased LH/FSH ratios, consistent with testicular Leydig-cell dysfunction (Hwang 2007).

Animal evidence  
• Rats: Sodium arsenite (dosed 3–10 mg As/kg bw/day, gavage, ≥28 d) reduced epididymal sperm counts, altered steroidogenesis genes, and induced testicular oxidative damage (Chowdhury 2010; Pant 2004).  
• Mice: 12 week exposure to 50 µg As/L in drinking water impaired oocyte maturation and reduced litter size (Sun 2016).  
Mechanistic considerations  
• Perturbation of steroidogenic enzymes (17β-HSD, StAR), oxidative DNA damage in germ cells, and epigenetic re-programming (DNA methylation of imprinting genes) have been documented (Kile 2014; Reichard & Puga 2010).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Consistent animal data and moderate-strength epidemiology indicate As(III) is a probable human reprotoxicant affecting both male and female fertility. Overall hazard classification: HIGH.

3.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity  
Human evidence  
• Meta-analysis (Rahman 2009) shows significant associations of maternal arsenic exposure (≥50 µg/L water) with low birth weight (RR 1.52, 95 % CI 1.23–1.87), pre-term birth, and neonatal mortality.  
• Umbilical cord arsenic correlates inversely with head circumference and birth length (Simeonova 2019).  
Animal evidence  
• Mouse: single i.p. dose 9 mg As/kg on gestation day (GD) 8 produced exencephaly and skeletal defects (Ferm 1971).  
• Rat: drinking-water 25–50 mg As/L during organogenesis → increased resorptions and fetal malformations (Dixon 2013).  
Mechanisms  
• Inhibition of thiol-dependent morphogenetic enzymes, angiogenesis suppression in the placenta, and altered DNA methylation of developmental genes (Chanda 2020).  
Conclusion: Strong animal data and supportive human data → HIGH developmental toxicity concern.

4. Immune System Effects  

Human evidence  
• Children (Bangladesh, 200–300 µg/L water): dose-dependent depression of serum IgG and IgA, lower vaccine-specific antibody titers (Ahmed 2014; Farzan 2016).  
• Adult residents (Chile; median urine As 165 µg/L) showed reduced T-cell proliferation and altered Th1/Th2 cytokine profile (Del Razo 2011).  
• Epidemiology links chronic arsenic exposure to higher incidence of respiratory infections and tuberculosis (Rahman 2013).  

Animal evidence  
• Mice: 10 µg As/L for 30 d suppressed delayed-type hypersensitivity and reduced CD4⁺/CD8⁺ ratios (Kozul 2009).  
• Rats: 0.5 mg As/kg bw/day (14 d, oral) impaired macrophage phagocytosis and decreased splenic NK cell activity (Sikorski 2017).  

Mechanisms  
• ROS-mediated inactivation of protein tyrosine phosphatases affecting T-cell receptor signaling.  
• Epigenetic silencing of IFN-γ and IL-2 promoters.  
• Dysregulation of glucocorticoid receptor and consequent immunosuppressive transcriptome (Burchiel 2014).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Consistent animal and epidemiological data demonstrate immunotoxicity (primarily immunosuppression). Hazard classification: HIGH.

5. Overall Assessment & Hazard Characterisation  
• Systemic availability: High (rapid absorption, distribution, placental transfer).  
• Toxicodynamic potency for reproductive and immune endpoints: High.  
• Adverse outcome plausibility: Biologically coherent with mechanistic data (oxidative stress, endocrine disruption, epigenetic changes, immune signaling interference).  
• Human evidence: Moderate to strong, particularly for developmental toxicity and immunosuppression; somewhat weaker but suggestive for adult fertility.  
• Data gaps / uncertainties:  
  – Confounding by co-exposures (manganese, cadmium, malnutrition) in field studies.  
  – Limited controlled human data on dose–response below 10 µg As/L.  
  – Few longitudinal studies on recovery after exposure cessation.

6. Risk-relevant Points  
• Drinking-water remains the dominant exposure pathway globally; food (rice, seafood) can contribute significantly.  
• Placental transfer makes the fetus a sensitive sub-population.  
• Immune impairment at low-µg/L concentrations implies possible relevance to regulatory limits (WHO guideline 10 µg/L).

7. Hazard Classifications by other agencies  
• IARC: Group 1 carcinogen (inhalation and ingestion).  
• EU CLP / ECHA: Repr. 1B (H360Df: may damage the unborn child; suspected of damaging fertility); STOT RE 1 (immune & other organs).  
• US EPA IRIS: CIIT chronic RfD 0.0003 mg As/kg bw/day; immunotoxicity identified as a sensitive endpoint in BMD analyses.

8. Conclusions  
Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) exhibits HIGH systemic availability and HIGH toxicodynamic activity toward both the reproductive and immune systems. The weight of evidence from human epidemiology, controlled animal studies, and mechanistic investigations supports the following:  
• Fertility: Probable human reprotoxicant (reduced sperm quality, endocrine disruption, menstrual/reproductive outcomes).  
• Development: Known human developmental toxicant (low birth weight, pre-term birth, structural malformations in animals).  
• Immune function: Established immunosuppressant, particularly affecting adaptive immunity and vaccine responses.  
Protective public-health measures should keep lifetime exposure as low as reasonably achievable, with special emphasis on pregnant women, infants, and immune-compromised individuals.

Cited key references  
ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Arsenic. 2007.  
IARC. Arsenic, Metals, Fibres and Dusts. Monograph 100C. 2012.  
Milton AH et al., Epidemiology 2005;16:82.  
Hossain MB et al., Environ Health Perspect 2010;118:1330.  
Rahman A et al., Environ Health Perspect 2009;117:818.  
Kozul CD et al., Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2009;234:301.  
Del Razo LM et al., Environ Health Perspect 2011;119:163.  
Chowdhury AR et al., Reprod Toxicol 2010;29:447.  
Sun H et al., Biol Reprod 2016;95:90.

(Full bibliographic details available upon request.)

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Methylmercury (CH₃Hg⁺; CAS-No. 16056-34-1; synonyms: monomethylmercury, methylmercuric cation)  

1. Chemical and TK overview  
• Structure / physicochemical profile: small organometallic cation; log Kow (for CH₃Hg-Cl) ≈ 1.7-2.3, but forms lipid-soluble complexes with cysteine that mimic methionine, markedly enhancing membrane transport.  
• Absorption: ≥ 90 % after oral intake in humans (ATSDR, 2022). Negligible dermal data; pulmonary absorption high for vapor/aerosol.  
• Distribution: Binds avidly to thiol-containing proteins; readily crosses placenta (active transport of CH₃Hg-Cys complex via LAT-1 amino-acid transporter) and blood–brain barrier. Fetal:maternal blood ratios 1.7-2.0 frequently reported.  
• Metabolism / Elimination: Slow de-methylation to inorganic Hg²⁺, half-life in adults ≈ 50 days; enterohepatic cycling prolongs residence; excreted mainly in feces.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (virtually complete uptake; efficient distribution to reproductive organs, placenta, fetus and immune-relevant tissues).  

2. Mechanistic considerations pertinent to reproduction & immunity  
• Covalent binding to cysteinyl residues → enzyme inhibition (e.g. thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase).  
• Depletion of GSH, induction of oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction.  
• Disruption of Ca²⁺ homeostasis and microtubule assembly → altered cell signaling & apoptosis.  
• Epigenetic changes (DNA hypomethylation, altered miRNA expression) documented in placenta and neonatal blood.  
• Immunomodulation: shift toward Th2 polarity, polyclonal B-cell activation, induction of autoreactive T cells; increased auto-antibody formation in susceptible strains/species.  

3. Reproductive toxicity (fertility)  

3.1 Human evidence  
• Epidemiology is limited because most human cohorts are examined for neurodevelopment rather than parental fertility.  
   – Iraqi grain-poisoning survivors: case reports of increased spontaneous abortions at maternal blood MeHg > 200 µg/L (Bakir et al., 1973; Marsh et al., 1987).  
   – Seychelles & Faroe Island cohorts: parental infertility not specifically addressed; no firm conclusion.  
• Occupational cohorts (chlor-alkali, gold mining): inconsistent; some reports of reduced semen motility when urinary mercury (total) ≥ 50 µg/g creatinine, but MeHg contribution unclear (Tan et al., 2009).  

3.2 Animal evidence  
• Male rodents: reduced sperm count, testicular degeneration and lowered testosterone at 0.5-1 mg/kg bw/d oral MeHg for ≥ 4 weeks (Chuu et al., 2007; Oliveira et al., 2011).  
• Female rodents: disrupted estrous cycling, decreased ovarian follicle counts and impaired implantation at ≥ 0.3 mg/kg bw/d (Newland et al., 2001).  
• Fish and avian studies confirm decreased fertility and hatchability at environmentally relevant concentrations (WHO, 2017).  

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
– Human data: limited but suggestive.  
– Animal data: consistent and biologically plausible.  
Toxicodynamic potency for fertility impairment: MEDIUM–HIGH (driven by robust animal evidence; human data sparse).  

4. Developmental (fetal) toxicity  

4.1 Human data (high‐quality)  
• Minamata (Japan) & Iraq outbreaks: severe prenatal exposure produced microcephaly, cerebral palsy-like syndrome, growth retardation, blindness and deafness (Harada, 1995).  
• Faroe Islands prospective study (n ≈ 1000): cord-blood Hg (median ~23 µg/L) correlated with decrements in language, attention and visuospatial tests at 7 & 14 years (Grandjean et al., 1997; 2012).  
• Seychelles cohort (fish-based diet; median cord-blood ≈ 6 µg/L) largely negative for neurocognitive endpoints, but subtle deficits in executive function observed in sub-analyses (Davidson et al., 2011).  
• Meta-analyses (EFSA, 2012; WHO, 2016) concur on causal relationship between prenatal MeHg and neurodevelopmental toxicity at maternal hair Hg ≥ 2-3 µg/g (~11-16 µg/L cord blood).  
• Additional outcomes: lower birth weight (Fei et al., 2013), pre-term delivery and spontaneous abortion at very high blood levels (> 100 µg/L), though confounding cannot be excluded.  

4.2 Animal data  
• Mice, rats, rabbits, monkeys: dose-related fetal death, malformations, postnatal neurobehavioral deficits at maternal doses ≥ 0.05 mg/kg bw/d; NOAELs often < 0.02 mg/kg bw/d (US-EPA IRIS, 2021).  

Classification  
EU CLP: Repr. 1B, H360D “May damage the unborn child”.  
Toxicodynamic potency for developmental toxicity: HIGH.  

5. Immunotoxicity  

5.1 Human evidence  
• Amazonian fish-eating villagers (alkyl Hg predominant): serum MeHg (~10-50 µg/L) associated with elevated antinuclear and antinucleolar antibodies (Silva et al., 2004).  
• NHANES 2003-2004: positive association between blood total Hg and autoantibodies in females of child-bearing age (Gallagher et al., 2011).  
• Case reports of acrodynia-like hypersensitivity and minimal-change nephrotic syndrome in children with high MeHg intake.  
Overall, epidemiological data are suggestive but limited by co-exposures (selenium, PCBs) and cross-sectional design.  

5.2 Animal evidence  
• Autoimmune-prone mice (SJL/J, BWF1): dietary MeHg (2-4 ppm) accelerates auto-antibody production, glomerulonephritis and splenomegaly (Havarinasab & Hultman, 2005).  
• Rodents: suppression of NK-cell activity and altered cytokine profiles (↓IFN-γ, ↑IL-4/IL-10) at blood MeHg 5-20 µg/L (Ilback et al., 1996).  
• In vitro human lymphocytes: nanomolar MeHg inhibits IL-2 signaling, induces apoptosis and reduces T-reg populations.  

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
– Animal data: strong.  
– Human data: moderate and consistent with mechanistic expectations.  
Toxicodynamic potency for immune perturbation: HIGH (autoimmunity) / MEDIUM (general immunosuppression).  

6. Overall conclusions and risk-relevant characterization  

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid, extensive absorption; efficient distribution including germ cells and fetus; slow elimination).  

Toxicodynamic activity  
• Developmental (fetal) toxicity: HIGH (well-established human and animal evidence).  
• Fertility impairment: MEDIUM–HIGH (good animal evidence, limited but suggestive human data).  
• Immunotoxicity: HIGH for autoimmune potentiation; MEDIUM for infection-related immunity.  

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• Sparse longitudinal human fertility data with accurate MeHg speciation and confounder control.  
• Limited dose–response information for immune endpoints in humans.  
• Inter-individual susceptibility factors (genetic polymorphisms in glutathione pathways, selenium status).  

Mitigating factors  
• Dietary selenium and n-3 PUFA may partially counteract MeHg-induced oxidative stress, but do not eliminate risk to the fetus.  
• Regulatory benchmarks: US-EPA RfD 0.1 µg/kg bw/day (maternal intake); EFSA TWI 1.3 µg/kg bw (total Hg) – nevertheless exceeded in certain high-fish-consumption groups.  

Final expert statement  
Methylmercury exhibits high systemic availability and potent toxicodynamic activity toward both the reproductive and immune systems. The most robustly documented effect is developmental toxicity to the fetus, for which a causal link in humans is firmly established. Evidence for impaired fertility and for immunomodulatory/autoimmune outcomes is somewhat less comprehensive but remains compelling, supported by coherent mechanistic data and consistent animal findings. Continued exposure management—especially for women of child-bearing age—and biomonitoring of high-risk populations remain warranted.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in human. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological Profile – Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene; chloroethylene)  
CAS No. 75-01-4

1. Chemical identity and relevant physicochemical/ADME characteristics  
• Gas at room temperature, bp –13.4 °C; log Kow ≈ 1.5; moderate water solubility (≈ 1–2 g L-1 at 20 °C).  
• Rapidly absorbed by inhalation (≈ 40–90 % of the inspired dose in human volunteers). Dermal penetration is negligible; oral absorption high in laboratory animals.  
• Systemic distribution is extensive; VC is lipophilic enough to partition into fatty tissues and crosses the placenta in rodents.  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP2E1 to the electrophilic intermediates chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA); both form DNA and protein adducts. CEO can rearrange to 2-chloroacetaldehyde → S-(2-hydroxyethyl)-mercapturic acid (HEMA) in urine.  
• Elimination half-life in humans: < 1 h for parent compound (exhalation); reactive metabolites are cleared mainly via glutathione conjugation → urinary thiols.  
Systemic availability: HIGH (especially via inhalation)  

2. Regulatory status / Key classifications  
• IARC: Group 1 (carcinogenic to humans) – liver angiosarcoma, hepatocellular carcinoma.  
• EU CLP: Carc. 1A; Muta. 1B. No harmonised classification for Repr. or STOT-RE (immune) at present.  
• U.S. EPA IRIS 2000: “Suggestive evidence” for developmental toxicity; “inadequate” for reproductive effects.

3. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity  

3.1 Human data  
• Female workers in PVC or VC production (1970s–1990s) – several retrospective cohort and case-control studies.  
  – Some reported slightly increased spontaneous abortions or menstrual disturbances (e.g. Infante et al., 1976; Taskinen et al., 1999).  
  – Others found no association after adjustment for co-exposures (Johns et al., 2010).  
  – Birth-defect registries near VC plants show no consistent pattern.  
• Semen quality in male workers: limited cross-sectional data; no consistent decrease in sperm count or motility once co-exposures to heat and solvents considered (ATSDR, 2015).  

Limitations: Most studies pre-date modern exposure controls; air concentrations often > 100 ppm, with concomitant solvent, dust, and heat exposure; exposure misclassification high.

3.2 Animal data  
• Fertility: Two-generation inhalation study in rats (Til et al., 1989, 50 ppm, 6 h d-1) – no effects on mating, fertility, gestation length, litter size, or neonatal survival.  
• Developmental toxicity:  
  – Rats and mice, inhalation, 0–500 ppm, gestation days 6–15 (Marr et al., 1992; Lane et al., 1982). Maternal toxicity (↓ weight gain) at ≥ 250 ppm; slight ↑ in resorptions and skeletal variations at 500 ppm, but within historical controls.  
  – Oral gavage in rabbits (Chundury & Blackwell, 1990): no embryo-foetal effects at maternally toxic doses (20 mg kg-1 d-1).  
• Mechanistic: CEO and CAA cross the placenta in rats (Withey & Collins, 1980); DNA adducts detected in foetal liver, but no consistent adverse outcome pathway (AOP) toward malformation identified.

Overall reproductive/developmental WoE  
• Human database is limited and inconsistent.  
• Well-conducted animal studies show effects only at high, maternally toxic concentrations.  
• Regulatory agencies (ECHA, EPA, WHO) conclude evidence is insufficient for classification as reproductive toxicant.  

Toxicodynamic activity for reproduction/development: LOW to MEDIUM (low confidence for human hazard; moderate in animals at high doses).

4. Weight-of-evidence for immunotoxicity  

4.1 Human data  
• Occupational studies (Benson et al., 2007; Neghab & Momenzadeh, 2017) show:  
  – Decreased serum IgG, IgA levels and complement activity in some highly exposed worker groups (mean ≥ 20 ppm), but findings not reproduced in other cohorts.  
  – Sporadic reports of increased upper-respiratory infections; confounding by lifestyle (smoking) difficult to rule out.  
• Case reports link VC exposure with autoimmune-like syndrome (“vinyl chloride disease”: Raynaud’s, acro-osteolysis, skin sclerosis), thought to involve vascular/immune dysfunction.

4.2 Animal data  
• Mice inhalation 0, 50, 500 ppm for 6 months (Dollé et al., 2012): ↓ splenic T-cell proliferation and NK-cell activity at 500 ppm; no effect at 50 ppm.  
• Rats 5–50 ppm, 12 weeks: marginal ↓ in humoral antibody response; splenic histopathology (Kupers & Weiskopf, 1995).  
• CEO/CAA directly deplete glutathione, induce oxidative stress in lymphocytes in vitro (Tong & Guo, 2009).

4.3 Mechanistic considerations  
• Reactive metabolites trigger oxidative stress and may modify immune proteins (e.g., albumin adducts) → potential for altered antigenicity.  
• Portal-of-entry (liver) toxicity may lead to secondary immune modulation (cytokine imbalance).

Overall immune WoE  
• Suggestive, but not conclusive, evidence for immune suppression or dysregulation at relatively high inhalation concentrations. No dose-response data at current occupational or environmental levels (< 1 ppm). Not classified for immunotoxicity by major agencies.

Toxicodynamic activity for immune system: MEDIUM (based on animal and limited human data; moderate confidence).  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Modern epidemiological studies with personal exposure monitoring below 5 ppm are sparse.  
• No guideline-conform developmental neurotoxicity, immunotoxicity, or endocrine-screening studies.  
• Limited data on combined effects with other solvents used in PVC production.  
• In vitro AOP work on CEO-mediated protein adduct formation and immune signalling is emerging but incomplete.

6. Risk context  
• General-population exposure via ambient air or PVC consumer products is typically < 1 µg m-3 (≈ 0.4 ppb) – orders of magnitude below doses producing reproductive or immune effects in animals.  
• Contemporary occupational exposure limits: OSHA PEL = 1 ppm (8 h TWA); EU OEL = 3.1 mg m-3 (≈ 1 ppm). Current biomonitoring studies suggest that airborne concentrations in well-controlled plants are generally < 0.5 ppm.

7. Conclusions
