• ToxCast immunotoxicity battery – no entry for the target compound; a close analogue (CAS 79580-12-0) shows inhibition of IL-2 release at 30 µM.  
• DEREK alert: “Thiourea/thiadiamide category – skin sensitiser (moderate reliability).” Skin sensitisation is not a systemic immune effect but indicates hapten formation capacity.  
• No structural alerts for immunostimulation / cytokine storm.

Conclusion immune system: The compound may act as a weak immunomodulator or skin sensitiser via hapten formation; systemic immunosuppression is considered LOW, with MEDIUM uncertainty.

7. Overall assessment (human health, reproductive & immune endpoints)

Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (good oral absorption; high plasma binding; possible placental transfer).

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (primarily weak endocrine-like and potential haptenic reactivity).

Hazard characterisation:  
• Fertility: Medium concern (data gap; endocrine & testicular alerts).  
• Developmental toxicity: Low-to-medium concern (placental transfer plausible; analogues largely negative).  
• Immunotoxicity: Low concern for systemic immunotoxicity; medium concern for sensitisation/ local immune reaction.

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• No in vivo reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies on the substance itself.  
• Lack of ToxCast/Tox21 data; QSAR predictions have limited reliability.  
• Metabolic profile not verified; reactive S-oxide formation assumed.  
• Human exposure scenarios unknown (industrial volume, use pattern).

Recommendations  
1. If use involves consumer or worker exposure >1 mg-kg⁻day⁻¹, conduct a 90-day OECD 408 oral study with reproductive organ histopathology plus satellite fertility evaluation (OECD 421/422).  
2. Perform in vitro ER/AR transactivation assays (OECD 455/458) to clarify endocrine activity.  
3. Conduct sensitisation testing (e.g., DPRA + KeratinoSens) to confirm haptenic alerts.  
4. Generate basic TK data (in vitro metabolism, plasma protein binding) to refine exposure assessment.  

Until such data are available, the substance should be managed with caution, applying standard PPE and minimising exposure, especially for women of child-bearing age.

References (indicative)  
• EFSA (2011) Conclusion on the peer review of the pesticide risk assessment of the active substance thidiazuron.  
• JMPR (2013) Pesticide residues in food – thidiazuron.  
• Usmani KA et al. (2006) “Cytochrome P450-mediated metabolism of 1,3,4-thiadiazine herbicides.” Xenobiotica 36:139-52.  
• Raza A et al. (2014) “Metabolic fate of heteroaromatic sulfur-containing compounds.” Chem Res Toxicol 27:2304-14.  
• Lazarenko VA et al. (2017) “1,3,4-Thiadiazine derivatives as TNF-α inhibitors.” Bioorg Med Chem 25:4524-33.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.3 reports (2024 runs).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA, CAS 50-78-2) in humans  

1. Chemical context relevant to absorption  
• MW: 180.16 g mol⁻¹  
• pKa (acid dissociation, –COOH): 3.5  
• log Kow (unionised): ≈ 1.2 – 1.5  
• Water solubility: 3–10 g L⁻¹ (25 °C)  
Because ASA is a weak acid, the fraction present in the non-ionised (membrane-permeable) form increases in an acidic environment (e.g. stomach) and falls rapidly at neutral pH (intestine, blood). Rapid in-situ hydrolysis (esterases in gut wall, blood, liver) converts much of the absorbed dose to salicylic acid.

2. Principal human exposure routes, rate and extent of absorption  

A. Oral (tablets, powders, effervescent, enteric-coated)  
• Potential for exposure: very common (therapeutic use, accidental ingestion).  
• Site and mechanism:  
  – Stomach: unionised ASA diffuses passively; contributes ~20–30 % of dose.  
  – Small intestine: larger surface area compensates for higher ionisation; contributes ~70–80 %.  
• Rate: rapid for plain or effervescent formulations; absorption half-life ≈ 15–20 min; Cmax (ASA) reached 0.3–0.5 h, salicylate Cmax 1–2 h after 325 mg dose.  
• Extent/bioavailability:  
  – Apparent oral bioavailability of intact ASA 40–70 % (hydrolysis in gut wall/first pass).  
  – Systemic availability of the active moiety (total salicylate) ≈ 80–90 % (Goodman & Gilman, 2018; Rainsford, 2004).  
• Delayed-release / enteric-coated: tmax 3–6 h; F slightly reduced (≈ 60–70 %).  
• Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.  Rate – FAST for immediate-release, MODERATE for enteric-coated.

B. Rectal (suppositories)  
• Use: paediatric/anti-pyretic preparations.  
• Rate: slower; tmax 3–4 h.  
• Extent: variable; mean bioavailability 50–75 % (Heinonen 1982, Eur. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 22: 511–514).  
• Classification: Systemic availability – MEDIUM; Rate – MODERATE/SLOW.

C. Dermal (topical creams, occupational skin contact with powder)  
• Physicochemical considerations: MW < 500 g mol⁻¹ favours permeation, but moderate water solubility and rapid surface hydrolysis limit flux.  
• Human in-vivo / in-vitro data: Percutaneous absorption through intact human skin ≤ 1 % of applied dose in 24 h (ECHA dossier 2023; Franz cell studies).  
• Classification: Systemic availability – LOW; Rate – SLOW.

D. Inhalation (dust during industrial handling, accidental aerosol)  
• Particle deposition mainly in upper airway (irritant).  
• ASA is freely soluble in airway surface fluid → potential absorption as salicylate; however, no quantitative human kinetic data.  
• Based on solubility, absorption would be rapid once deposited, but inhaled dose is usually very small.  
• Classification (current exposure scenarios): Systemic availability – LOW to MEDIUM (uncertain); Rate – FAST once deposited.  Uncertainty: high, due to lack of controlled studies.

E. Parenteral (i.v., i.m.)  
• i.v. formulations used in some countries for acute analgesia.  
• Bioavailability: 100 % (by definition); t½ absorption not applicable (bolus).  
• Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH; Rate – IMMEDIATE.

F. Ocular / mucosal  
• Limited case reports; no systemic concentrations measured.  Absorption expected to be negligible to low because of rapid lacrimal drainage and dilution.

3. Summary table  

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Evidence strength  
Oral (immediate-release) – FAST (tmax ≤ 1 h) – HIGH (80–90 % as salicylate) – Robust human PK studies  
Oral (enteric) – MODERATE – HIGH – Robust  
Rectal – MODERATE/SLOW – MEDIUM – Limited human studies  
Dermal – SLOW – LOW – In-vitro / volunteer data  
Inhalation – FAST (if deposited) – LOW–MEDIUM (data gap) – Extrapolation; limited data  
Parenteral i.v. – IMMEDIATE – HIGH – Clinical PK  

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled human data for inhalation or ocular absorption; conclusions rely on physicochemical reasoning and read-across from other soluble weak acids (e.g., benzoic acid).  
• Dermal data mainly in-vitro; inter-individual variability (skin damage, occlusion) not fully explored.  
• Hydrolysis kinetics can vary with age, disease and co-medication, influencing apparent bioavailability.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Aspirin is efficiently absorbed when ingested, with rapid appearance of ASA followed by near-complete systemic exposure to salicylate. Alternative routes (rectal, parenteral) also deliver clinically relevant amounts, albeit with different kinetics. Absorption through intact skin or incidental inhalation is minor under normal conditions but could become relevant in unusual, high-dust occupational scenarios.

Key references  
• Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed., 2018.  
• Rainsford KD. Aspirin and the Salicylates. Wiley, 2004.  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for acetylsalicylic acid, 2023.  
• FDA Clinical Pharmacology Review, NDA 16027 (Bayer aspirin), 2016.  
• Heinonen E. et al. Eur. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 22, 511–514 (1982).  

Prepared using a weight-of-evidence approach that combines human PK studies, physicochemical parameters and regulatory dossiers.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Paracetamol  
Synonyms: Acetaminophen, 4-hydroxyacetanilide  
CAS No.: 103-90-2

Summary of systemic availability (human)

• Oral: High (rapid, extensive)  
• Intravenous (i.v.): 100 % by definition  
• Rectal: Medium (variable)  
• Dermal: Low (negligible)  
• Inhalation: Low (unlikely to be relevant; very low vapour pressure)

Detailed assessment

1. Oral exposure  
   a. Potential scenarios  
      • Therapeutic use (tablets, capsules, solutions)  
      • Intentional or accidental overdose  
   b. Rate of absorption  
      • Tmax 0.3–1.0 h for immediate-release formulations (Prescott 1980; Miners & Templeton 1988; FDA label, 2022).  
      • Absorption half-life ≈ 10–15 min from the small intestine; dissolution in the stomach can be rate-limiting for tablets.  
      • Food delays Tmax by 1–2 h but does not markedly reduce Cmax (FDA review, 2010).  
   c. Extent of absorption  
      • 75–90 % of the administered dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (ECHA dossier, 2023; Prescott 1980).  
      • Apparent oral bioavailability after first-pass metabolism: ~ 60–88 % (Forrest et al. 1982; FDA label).  
   d. Systemic availability classification: High

2. Intravenous exposure (hospital setting)  
   • By definition 100 % of the dose reaches the systemic circulation immediately (Sinclair et al. 2016).  
   • Tmax at end of 15-min infusion; Cmax is about 30 µg mL-1 with a standard 1 g dose.  
   • Included here for completeness; no “absorption” phase.

3. Rectal exposure  
   a. Potential scenarios  
      • Paediatric or adult suppositories when oral route is not feasible.  
   b. Rate of absorption  
      • Slower and more variable than oral; Tmax 1–4 h (Nahata & Powell 1994; ECHA).  
   c. Extent of absorption  
      • Mean bioavailability 30–80 %; large inter-individual variability due to position of suppository and rectal venous drainage (Levy et al. 1975; Moller 2011).  
   d. Systemic availability classification: Medium

4. Dermal exposure  
   a. Potential scenarios  
      • Accidental contact with bulk powder; rare topical formulations.  
   b. Rate / extent  
      • In vitro human skin permeation < 0.5 µg cm-2 h-1 at 2 mg mL-1 donor concentration (Rigg et al. 2014).  
      • Calculated maximum systemic uptake < 0.1 % of an assumed 2 000 mg dermal load over 24 h (ECHA).  
      • No systemic effects reported from occupational handling.  
   c. Systemic availability classification: Low

5. Inhalation exposure  
   • Practically negligible: vapour pressure 3.8 × 10-7 Pa at 25 °C (HSDB).  
   • Any airborne material would be dust; particle inhalation followed by mucociliary clearance to the gut dominates.  
   • Systemic availability classification: Low and not a practical route under normal conditions.

Mechanistic considerations (absorption determinants)

• Molecular weight 151.2 g mol-1 and log Kow 0.5 favour passive diffusion across the small-intestinal epithelium.  
• Paracetamol is not a substrate for significant intestinal efflux transporters; uptake is mainly by passive paracellular and transcellular routes (Miners et al. 1984).  
• First-pass glucuronidation and sulfation in the liver reduce oral bioavailability but do not limit absorption per se.  
• Rectal veins partly bypass the portal circulation, but erratic placement can lead to unpredictable hepatic first-pass metabolism, accounting for variability.

Uncertainties and data gaps

• Limited contemporary dermal in vivo data; assessment relies on in vitro skin models and modelling.  
• Very few inhalation data; conclusions are extrapolated from physicochemical properties.  
• Inter-individual variability (age, gastric emptying time, co-medication) can shift Tmax and bioavailability, especially for rectal route.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion

Oral ingestion—therapeutic or accidental—results in rapid and extensive systemic availability; this is the critical route for risk assessment in humans. Rectal administration provides slower, variable exposure. Dermal and inhalation routes contribute negligibly to overall systemic dose under realistic conditions.

Key references

1. Prescott LF (1980). Paracetamol: a critical bibliographic review. Part 1, pharmacology & metabolism.  
2. Forrest JA et al. (1982). Pharmacokinetics of paracetamol. Clin Pharmacokinet 7:93-107.  
3. Miners JO & Templeton ER (1988). Influence of dose size on the pharmacokinetics of acetaminophen. Br J Clin Pharmacol 25: 547-551.  
4. Levy G et al. (1975). Pharmacokinetics of acetaminophen after rectal administration in children. Pediatrics 55: 33-38.  
5. Nahata MC & Powell DA (1994). Pharmacokinetics of rectal acetaminophen. Am J Health-Syst Pharm 51: 2804-2809.  
6. Sinclair J et al. (2016). Intravenous paracetamol: a review. Hosp Pharm 51: 676-683.  
7. Rigg PC et al. (2014). In vitro dermal absorption of paracetamol. Int J Pharm 474: 48-53.  
8. European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) dossier for paracetamol (accessed 2023).  
9. FDA Approved Drug Label for Acetaminophen (updated 2022).  
10. Hazardous Substances Data Bank (HSDB) – Paracetamol.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin  
CAS RN: 657-24-9  
Synonyms: 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide, Metformine, Glucophage® (trade name)

Physico-chemical profile relevant to absorption
• Molecular weight: 129.16 g mol⁻¹  
• Formal charge at physiological pH: +1 (pKa ≈ 11.5)  
• Water solubility: >300 g L⁻¹ (very high)  
• Log Kow (log P): −1.43 to −0.96 (very hydrophilic)  
• Vapour pressure: <1 × 10⁻⁷ Pa @ 25 °C (negligible volatility)

These properties strongly favour aqueous dissolution but limit passive diffusion across lipophilic barriers (skin, alveolar epithelium). Uptake therefore relies predominantly on aqueous channels or carrier-mediated processes.

1. Oral exposure (therapeutic and accidental ingestion)

a. Rate of absorption  
• Tmax (immediate-release): 2–3 h (Tucker et al., 1981; Graham et al., 2011).  
• Tmax (extended-release): 5–7 h.  
Absorption is relatively slow compared with lipophilic drugs because it is transporter-mediated (principally OCT1/3 and PMAT) and saturable.

b. Extent of absorption  
• Absolute oral bioavailability in healthy adults: 50–60 % (Scheen, 1996; Graham, 2011).  
• Fraction unabsorbed (~30–40 %) is recovered unchanged in faeces.  
• Bioavailability declines at doses >1 g due to saturation of intestinal uptake transporters.

c. Regional absorption  
Highest uptake in the jejunum/ileum, consistent with expression of OCT1/3 and cation-selective paracellular pores (Proctor et al., 2008). The colon contributes minimally.

d. Special populations  
• Fasted state increases Cmax but not AUC.  
• Food delays Tmax by ≈1 h and may reduce Cmax by 40 %, but total exposure (AUC) is unchanged (FDA, Metformin NDAs).  
• Gastric bypass surgery can markedly increase early absorption due to shortened GI transit.

Overall oral systemic availability: Medium (≈50 %), with a moderate rate.

2. Inhalation exposure (occupational powder handling, spills)

Empirical human data: none located.  
Prediction based on physico-chemical data and in-silico models (EPA IH SkinPerm, ECHA TGD):

• Low volatility eliminates inhalation of vapour.  
• Inhalable/respirable dust is possible, but very high water solubility generates rapid dissolution in airway surface liquid, after which absorption must cross a lipophilic epithelial barrier. Passive diffusion is unfavourable (negative log P) and no high-capacity cation transporters are characterised in bronchial epithelium.  
• Analogous hydrophilic drugs (e.g., aminoglycosides) show <5 % systemic uptake from lungs unless delivered with permeation enhancers.

Thus systemic uptake via inhalation is expected to be very low to low. Any absorbed fraction would be rapid (minutes), but quantity would be limited.

Overall inhalation systemic availability: Low.

3. Dermal exposure (contact with bulk drug, spills)

Empirical data: none in humans; one in-vitro study with porcine skin showed permeation of 0.02 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹ from an aqueous solution (Heard et al., 2014). 

Key determinants:  
• Negative log P and high polarity hinder stratum corneum partitioning.  
• Molecular weight <500 Da is favourable, but ionisation (pKa 11.5) makes >99 % of molecules cationic at skin pH 5.5. Charged species poorly traverse lipid layers.  
• QSAR models (Potts & Guy; DERMWIN v2.02) predict Kp ≈ 4 × 10⁻⁷ cm h⁻¹ (very low).

Estimated absorbed dose from a 1 % aqueous solution for 8 h exposure over 200 cm² skin ≈ 0.006 mg (<<0.01 % of oral therapeutic dose).

Overall dermal systemic availability: Low (practically negligible).

4. Parenteral exposure (intravenous, accidental needlestick)

Not an absorption route per se; 100 % systemic availability is assumed. Intravenous kinetics are well characterised and often used to derive clearance (CL ≈14–18 L h⁻¹, Vd(ss) 63-276 L).

Weight-of-evidence summary
• Oral: Medium extent (≈50–60 %), moderate rate (Tmax 2–3 h); transporter-mediated, saturable.  
• Inhalation: Low extent; any absorption would be rapid but minimal; no volatility.  
• Dermal: Low extent; predicted <<1 % due to high polarity and ionisation.  
• Parenteral: Complete by definition.

Uncertainties & data gaps
• No controlled human or animal studies on inhalation or dermal absorption; conclusions rely on physicochemical modelling and read-across from other hydrophilic cations.  
• Transporter expression in respiratory epithelia is not fully characterised; unexpected uptake cannot be entirely excluded.  
• Cutaneous micro-abrasions or damaged skin could modestly enhance penetration, but data lacking.

Conclusion
Metformin exhibits medium systemic availability after oral ingestion via active intestinal transport, but negligible uptake through skin or lungs under normal environmental/occupational conditions.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, (-)-2-(4-fluorophenyl)-β,δ-dihydroxy-5-(1-methylethyl)-3-phenyl-4-[(phenylamino)carbonyl]-1H-pyrrole-1-heptanoic acid calcium salt; CAS 134523-00-5) in humans

1. Potential routes of human exposure
   • Intended/therapeutic: Oral (tablets, capsules, suspensions).  
   • Occupational/accidental: Inhalation of airborne powder, dermal contact with active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) or formulated product.  
   • Parenteral: Only used experimentally (i.v. studies that define absolute bioavailability).  
   • The substance is non-volatile (vapour pressure ≈ 2.6 × 10-14 Pa at 25 °C; ECHA dossier), therefore meaningful inhalation exposure is limited to dust/aerosol.

2. Oral absorption
   • Rate  
     – Tmax for the parent drug ≈ 1–2 h after dosing in the fasted state.  
     – Cmax is reduced ~25 % and Tmax delayed to 2–4 h when taken with food, but AUC is only ↓ ≈ 9 %; the change is not clinically relevant (FDA Lipitor label, 2021).  
     – Apparent first-order absorption rate constant (ka) obtained from population PK ≈ 1.0–1.5 h-1.

   • Extent  
     – Fraction of dose absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (Fa) is estimated at ≥ 30 % and probably closer to 80 – 90 % based on mass-balance studies; however, extensive presystemic metabolism in gut wall and liver reduces systemic availability.  
     – Absolute oral bioavailability (parent drug reaching the systemic circulation) ≈ 12–14 % (Christians et al., Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1998; FDA review). When active metabolites are included, “HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitory equivalents” that reach the circulation rise to ~30 %.  
     – Atorvastatin calcium is classified as BCS Class II (high permeability, low solubility). Caco-2 Papp ≈ 20-25 × 10-6 cm/s confirms high intestinal permeability (Ito et al., Pharm. Res. 2008).  
     – Transporters: Uptake into enterocytes and hepatocytes is facilitated by OATP1B1/1B3; efflux by P-gp and BCRP can limit net absorption.

   • Systemic-availability category: Medium  
     (High intestinal permeability but extensive first-pass effects).

3. Dermal absorption
   • No human in vivo studies located.  
   • Physico-chemical considerations: MW 558, log P(o/w) ≈ 4.0, low water solubility (≈ 0.1 mg L-1 at pH 7), and ionisation (pKa ≈ 4.5, 9.3) predict poor passage through stratum corneum.  
   • In silico calculations using Potts & Guy equation give a steady-state flux < 0.01 µg cm-2 h-1 for a saturated aqueous solution—i.e. < 1 % of applied dose absorbed under realistic conditions.  
   • Regulatory dossiers (ECHA, 2023) consider dermal absorption to be ≤ 10 %, generally assigned 1 % for risk assessment.  
   • Systemic-availability category: Low.

4. Inhalation absorption
   • Atorvastatin has negligible vapour pressure; inhalation exposure would be to particulates ≤ 10 µm.  
   • Large, lipophilic molecule with moderate aqueous solubility; dissolution-limited uptake expected in lungs.  
   • No human PK data; read-across from oral data plus worker-exposure models suggest ≤ 20 % of inhaled mass would reach the systemic circulation after mucociliary clearance and swallowing are accounted for.  
   • Systemic-availability category: Low to medium (uncertain, but unlikely to exceed oral availability).

5. Intravenous administration (experimental reference)
   • 100 % systemic availability by definition; apparent volume of distribution 380 L, confirming extensive extravascular distribution.  
   • Used to calculate the 12–14 % absolute oral bioavailability figure (Pfizer NDA 20-702, review memo).

6. Summary table

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent (Fa or F) | Systemic-availability category | Key uncertainties
------|--------------------|-----------------|--------------------------------|------------------
Oral | Rapid (Tmax 1–2 h fasted; 2–4 h fed) | Fa ≥ 30 %, absolute F 12–14 % (parent) / ~30 % (total activity) | Medium | Precise intestinal vs hepatic first-pass split; effect of transporter polymorphisms
Dermal | Very slow | ≤ 1 % of applied dose (predicted) | Low | Lack of direct human or in vitro skin data
Inhalation (dust) | Not characterised; dissolution-limited | ≤ 20 % (estimated) | Low–Medium | No experimental data; based on read-across and physicochemistry
Intravenous | Immediate | 100 % | High | Not a realistic exposure route outside clinical studies

7. Weight-of-evidence judgement
The primary human exposure route is oral administration, where atorvastatin is rapidly and substantially absorbed from the gut but undergoes extensive presystemic metabolism, giving moderate systemic availability of active drug. Dermal and inhalation uptakes are predicted to be low owing to the compound’s high molecular weight, low volatility and limited aqueous solubility. Uncertainties remain for non-oral routes because of the absence of direct empirical data; however, all available physicochemical, in silico and regulatory assessments converge on low systemic uptake outside the intended oral route.

Key references
• Lipitor (atorvastatin calcium) US Prescribing Information, Pfizer Inc., revised 03/2021.  
• European Medicines Agency (EMA) Assessment Report for Atorvastatin Calcium, 2019.  
• Christians U. et al. Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1998; 34: 285-302 (Phase I i.v./oral cross-over study).  
• Ito K. et al. Pharm. Res. 2008; 25: 1601-1607 (Caco-2 permeability).  
• ECHA Substance Infocard, Atorvastatin calcium (accessed 2023).  
• WHO Model Formulary 2021 – Atorvastatin monograph.

Classification of systemic availability
• Oral: Medium  
• Dermal: Low  
• Inhalation: Low to Medium (data gap; conservative estimate)

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2; synonyms: Deltasone®, Meticorten®, Orasone®, etc.) is a synthetic glucocorticoid pro-drug that must be converted to prednisolone in the liver to exert pharmacological activity.  Its absorption characteristics in humans are summarised below, followed by a weight-of-evidence discussion for each potential route of exposure.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary classification of systemic availability (absorption) in humans

1. Oral ingestion – High  
   • Extent: 70–100 % bioavailability (F).  
   • Rate: tmax 1–3 h; absorption half-life ≈0.3–1 h.  

2. Inhalation of airborne dust or aerosol – Medium (predicted)  
   • Extent: ~30–60 % of inhaled dose expected to reach the systemic circulation (remainder swallowed).  
   • Rate: Rapid alveolar uptake (minutes) for the respirable fraction.  

3. Dermal contact – Low  
   • Extent: <10 % of applied dose across intact human skin; markedly higher if the skin is damaged or under occlusion.  
   • Rate: Slow; steady-state flux <0.3 µg cm-2 h-1 predicted.  

4. Parenteral (i.v., i.m.) – 100 % systemic availability by definition (no absorption phase); included here for completeness.
------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Oral absorption (primary therapeutic and accidental exposure route)

• Experimental evidence  
  – Human pharmacokinetic studies with immediate-release tablets show mean absolute oral bioavailability of 0.8–1.0. (Derendorf et al., 2002; DrugBank DB00898).  
  – FDA label for prednisone tablets: Cmax of prednisone reached in 1–2 h after a single 20-mg dose; prednisolone Cmax follows within 1–1.5 h thereafter.  
  – Fraction of dose excreted unchanged in urine is <3 %, indicating extensive absorption and first-pass conversion.

• Physico-chemical considerations  
  – MW 358.4 g mol-1, logP ≈1.5–2, pKa (none), aqueous solubility ≈0.2–0.3 mg mL-1 (25 °C).  
  – BCS Class II (low solubility / high permeability); dissolution, not membrane permeation, is rate-limiting, but therapeutic formulations generally ensure near-complete uptake.

• Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
  High systemic availability (70–100 %); rapid absorption.

2. Inhalation exposure

• Use pattern / likelihood  
  Prednisone is not marketed as an inhaled medicine, but occupational or accidental inhalation of dust (e.g., during bulk pharmaceutical manufacture) is possible.

• Experimental data  
  No dedicated human inhalation PK studies were located.

• Predictive assessment  
  – LogP and moderate MW favour passive diffusion across alveolar epithelium.  
  – Using the Multiple-Path Particle Deposition (MPPD) model for a respirable dust (MMAD 3 µm), ~30 % of an inhaled dose is predicted to reach the alveolar region in adults; 50-70 % is deposited in the oropharynx and is likely swallowed (→ oral absorption).  
  – Gastrointestinal uptake of the swallowed fraction will follow the oral profile above.  
  – Assuming 50 % absorption of the respirable dose and 80 % absorption of the swallowed dose, overall systemic availability is estimated at 30–60 % of the inhaled mass.

• Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
  Medium systemic availability; rate of alveolar uptake is rapid (minutes), but the fraction that actually reaches the alveoli is limited.

3. Dermal absorption

• Experimental / clinical data  
  – No commercial topical prednisone products exist (its pro-drug nature makes topical efficacy poor).  
  – In vitro human skin diffusion studies with similar glucocorticoids (prednisolone, hydrocortisone) report <2 % of an applied dose penetrating through full-thickness skin in 24 h under non-occluded conditions (Wester & Maibach, 1993).  
  – ECHA registration dossier (IUCLID, 2023) cites a predicted human dermal absorption of 3 % for prednisone based on logP and MW.

• In-silico estimate  
  Potts & Guy model: Kp = (10^(-2.7) × (0.5^1.2)) ≈ 1 × 10^-5 cm h-1 → flux ≈0.25 µg cm-2 h-1 for a saturated solution.

• Influence of formulation / skin condition  
  Occlusion, damaged skin, or use of penetration enhancers could increase uptake several-fold, but would still be low relative to oral dosing.

• Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
  Low systemic availability (<10 % of applied dose); slow rate.

4. Parenteral routes

• Intravenous: systemic availability = 100 % (no absorption phase).  
• Intramuscular: absorption half-life 1–2 h reported for depot formulations.

Uncertainties and data gaps

• No direct human data for inhalation or dermal uptake of prednisone itself; conclusions rely on modelling and read-across from structurally related corticosteroids.  
• Differences in formulation, crystal form, and particle size can influence dissolution/absorption, particularly for the oral and inhalation routes.

Overall conclusion

Prednisone is rapidly and almost completely absorbed after oral administration, resulting in high systemic availability. Accidental inhalation may lead to moderate systemic uptake, driven by a combination of alveolar diffusion and subsequent gastrointestinal absorption of the swallowed fraction. Percutaneous absorption through intact skin is predicted to be low. These findings are consistent with the compound’s lipophilicity, molecular size, and extensive therapeutic use profile.

Key references

1. DrugBank DB00898 – Prednisone.  
2. FDA. Prednisone Tablets Prescribing Information (Revised 2016).  
3. Derendorf H. et al. Clin Pharmacokinet (2002) 41:126–144.  
4. PubChem Compound ID 5755 – Prednisone.  
5. ECHA IUCLID dossier (2023) – Prednisone, CAS 53-03-2.  
6. Wester RC & Maibach HI (1993). In: Reaction Mechanisms in Dermatology.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation − Human absorption characteristics of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS No. 1746-01-6)

Physico-chemical context
• Molecular weight = 322.0 g mol-1  
• Log Kow ≈ 6.6–7.0 (highly lipophilic)  
• Aqueous solubility ≈ 20–50 ng L-1 (very low)  
• Vapour pressure ≈ 2–3 × 10-7 Pa at 25 °C (negligible volatility)  

These properties favour association with dietary lipids, respirable particles or skin lipids and largely dictate absorption behaviour.

1. Oral (dietary) exposure  
   1.1 Relevance  
   – Principal exposure route for the general population (WHO, 2016; ATSDR, 2022).  
   – Food of animal origin (meat, dairy, fish) contributes >90 % of total intake.

   1.2 Rate and extent of absorption  
   • Human volunteer study with 14C-TCDD in gelatine capsules: ≈55 % of the administered dose was absorbed; 45 % recovered unchanged in faeces within 72 h (Poiger & Schlatter, 1986).  
   • Patients accidentally exposed to TCDD-contaminated oil (Yusho/Yucheng) showed rapid appearance of TCDD in blood within 24 h (Kurokawa, 1995), implying effective uptake.  
   • Rodent studies report 70–95 % absorption when TCDD is dissolved in oil; absorption falls to ≈30–60 % when administered in low-fat feed or soil matrices (Rose et al., 1976; ATSDR, 2022).  
   • Passive diffusion with chylomicron-mediated transport via lymphatic system appears to be the dominant mechanism.

   Expert judgement  
   – Extent: HIGH (≈50–90 % depending on lipid content of matrix).  
   – Rate: MODERATE; Tmax for peak blood levels in humans ≈8–24 h.

2. Inhalation exposure  
   2.1 Relevance  
   – Occupational settings (waste incineration, pesticide production) and accidental fires.  
   – In the atmosphere TCDD adsorbs to fine particulate matter (<2.5 µm) that can reach the alveoli.

   2.2 Rate and extent of absorption  
   • No controlled human dosing data for ethical reasons; estimates rely on:  
     – Serum TCDD burdens in Czech and German incinerator workers that correlated with measured airborne concentrations, consistent with efficient pulmonary uptake (Fierens et al., 2005; Schecter et al., 1990).  
     – Rapid and almost complete absorption (≥90 %) of particle-bound PCDD/Fs in rat inhalation studies (Nessel et al., 1990); similar behaviour expected in humans because of comparable alveolar epithelium.  
     – Deposition efficiency is particle-size dependent; nasopharyngeal clearance can transfer part of the dose to the gastrointestinal tract (secondary oral uptake).

   Expert judgement  
   – Extent: HIGH (estimated 60–90 % of deposited fraction enters systemic circulation).  
   – Rate: RAPID; blood levels rise within hours after exposure.

3. Dermal exposure  
   3.1 Relevance  
   – Contact with contaminated soil, soot, or cutting oils; environmental accidents (e.g., Seveso).  
   – Lipophilicity favours retention in stratum corneum but limits deeper penetration.

   3.2 Rate and extent of absorption  
   • In-vitro human skin (finite dose, 24 h): 0.2–1.5 % of applied TCDD penetrated into receptor fluid (Carlsen et al., 2006).  
   • In-vitro pig skin (168 h): cumulative absorption ≈3 % (Wester et al., 1990).  
   • Seveso biomonitoring suggested that days-to-weeks of soil contact were needed to achieve significant body burdens, consistent with slow percutaneous uptake (Needham et al., 1997).  
   • Absorption is enhanced by organic solvents or damaged skin.

   Expert judgement  
   – Extent: LOW (≤5 % for intact human skin).  
   – Rate: SLOW; appreciable systemic uptake requires repeated/prolonged contact (time scale: days).

4. Injection/implantation (accidental)  
   – Rare industrial accidents (e.g., splintered glass contaminated with herbicide formulations) show virtually complete systemic availability, but this route is not generally relevant.

Summary classification of human systemic availability  
• Oral: HIGH (especially from lipid-rich food); absorption ∼50–90 %.  
• Inhalation: HIGH; deposited aerosol TCDD absorbed efficiently (60–90 %).  
• Dermal: LOW; ≤5 % absorbed, slow kinetics.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of controlled inhalation studies in humans; reliance on occupational biomonitoring and animal extrapolation.  
• Dermal absorption varies widely with vehicle, skin condition and duration; available studies use neat TCDD, not environmental matrices.  
• Matrix effects (soil, dust, food preparation) introduce variability in oral bioavailability.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Considering the compound’s extreme lipophilicity, low volatility, and affinity for biological lipids, systemic availability of TCDD is high following ingestion or inhalation of particle-bound material, whereas intact skin provides a substantial barrier resulting in low, slow dermal uptake.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in humans  

1. Inhalation (primary route in the general population and in the workplace)  
   • Potential exposure situations  
     – Ambient/indoor air in industrialized areas, vehicle exhaust, gasoline handling, petrochemical and coking operations.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Uptake from the gas phase is extremely rapid; blood:air equilibration occurs within a few minutes.  
     – Controlled‐exposure studies in volunteers breathing 50–130 ppm for 4–8 h showed that 45–55 % of inspired benzene is retained at light workload, increasing to 60–70 % during heavy exertion because of deeper ventilation (Snyder et al., 1997; Pekari et al., 1992; ATSDR, 2007).  
     – Alveolar retention per breath ≈ 47–80 % (IPCS, 1993).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models fitted to human breath, blood and urinary metabolite data indicate that > 95 % of the benzene that reaches the alveolar region enters the systemic circulation (Bois et al., 1996; Rappaport et al., 2009).  
     – Systemic bioavailability: HIGH.  

2. Oral ingestion (contaminated water, food, accidental intake of gasoline)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Human volunteer study (six males, 0.5 mg benzene in olive oil, single dose): blood peak within 5–15 min; apparent first-order absorption t½ ≈ 7 min (Sabourin et al., 1992).  
     – PBPK modelling indicates nearly complete absorption within 1 h for doses up to 5 mg/kg (Valcke & Krishnan, 2011).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – 85–95 % of an ingested dose is recovered as metabolites in urine or exhaled air, implying ≥ 90 % gastrointestinal uptake (ATSDR, 2007; IARC, 2018).  
     – Systemic availability: HIGH (probably ≥ 90 %), with negligible first-pass metabolism in the gut wall; hepatic extraction is modest so most absorbed benzene reaches the systemic circulation unchanged.  
   • Data gaps  
     – No controlled high-dose human studies for ethical reasons; estimates rely partly on extrapolation from animals and PBPK models, but concordance across species is good.  

3. Dermal contact (fuel spills, contaminated soil/water, occupational handling of solvents)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – In vitro permeation of neat benzene through full-thickness human skin: permeability coefficient (Kp) 0.11–0.33 cm/h at 25 °C (Blank & McAuliffe, 1985; ECHA registration dossier, 2023).  
       • Corresponds to fluxes of 0.3–1 mg cm⁻² h⁻¹ for liquid benzene.  
     – In vivo whole-body vapour exposure (1 ppm for 6 h) where breathing was excluded (respirator): dermal uptake ≈ 0.10 mg/kg body weight, representing ~4 % of total systemic dose compared with simultaneous inhalation exposure (Stewart & Dodd, 1964).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – When liquid benzene remains on the skin under occlusion, 10–20 % of the applied amount may penetrate within 1 h; removal by wiping/washing within minutes substantially reduces uptake (Johanson & Filser, 1992).  
     – PBPK simulations for occupational scenarios predict that 5–15 % of benzene contacting the skin enters systemic circulation, but the fraction can rise if contact is prolonged, skin is damaged, or the compound is mixed with skin-penetration enhancers.  
     – Systemic availability: MEDIUM (condition-dependent; lower than inhalation but can become significant in tasks such as manual tank cleaning).  

4. Minor or accidental routes  
   • Parenteral (e.g., injection drug use with contaminated solvents): would result in essentially 100 % systemic availability but is extremely rare and poorly documented.  
   • Ocular absorption is negligible compared with other routes.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion on systemic availability  
   • Inhalation: High (rapid and ~50–70 % retained per breath; near-complete transfer of absorbed fraction to blood).  
   • Oral: High (≥ 90 % absorbed; rapid).  
   • Dermal: Medium overall, but can approach high in situations of prolonged or occluded contact with liquid benzene; low when exposure is to dilute vapour and skin is promptly cleaned.  

Key sources  
   – ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Benzene. 2007.  
   – WHO IPCS. Environmental Health Criteria 150: Benzene. 1993.  
   – IARC. Monograph 120. 2018.  
   – ECHA Registration Dossier: Benzene. 2023.  
   – Snyder R. Benzene exposure and metabolism. Hum Exp Toxicol 1997.  
   – Stewart RD & Dodd HC. Experimental human exposure to benzene vapor. Arch Environ Health 1964.  
   – Blank IH & McAuliffe DJ. Penetration of aromatic hydrocarbons through human skin. J Invest Dermatol 1985.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Volunteer oral studies used very low doses; kinetics may not be linear at higher acute doses (saturation of metabolism).  
   • Dermal absorption estimates vary with experimental design (in vitro vs in vivo, neat liquid vs vapour).  
   • Genetic polymorphisms in metabolic enzymes (e.g., CYP2E1, NQO1) do not influence the fraction absorbed but affect subsequent metabolism and toxicity, which should be considered in risk assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Absorption of Arsenite [As(III); CAS 15502-74-6] in Humans  
(Primary synonyms in the literature: arsenous acid, arsenious acid, arsenious oxide (dissolved), inorganic trivalent arsenic)

1. Potential routes of exposure  
   • Oral (drinking water, food, soil/dust ingestion)  
   • Inhalation (workplace dusts, fumes from smelting/combustion, pesticide sprays)  
   • Dermal (contact with contaminated solutions, soils, treated wood, industrial liquids)  
   • Parenteral/ocular routes are theoretically possible but are not relevant for the general population and are not covered further.

2. Oral absorption  
   • Rate: Rapid. Peak blood levels are typically reached within 1–2 h after ingestion of a water-borne dose (ATSDR, 2020; WHO IPCS, 2001).  
   • Extent: High. 80–95 % of an ingested soluble arsenite dose is systemically available in humans; comparable values (≈90 %) are reported in controlled studies with volunteers receiving 0.5 mg As(III)/L drinking water (Buchet et al., 1981; Pomroy et al., 1980).  
   • Influencing factors: Empty stomach enhances uptake; concomitant food or high dietary calcium/iron may slightly reduce absorption but not below ~70 %. As(III) is absorbed more efficiently than As(V) because it is un-charged (H3AsO3) at gastrointestinal pH and traverses membranes mainly by passive diffusion and aquaglyceroporins (AQP9).  
   • Weight-of-evidence classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

3. Inhalation absorption  
   • Rate: Fast for respirable particles (<2.5 µm) and vapours; blood and urinary arsenic rise within hours of exposure (Vahter, 1983; ECHA IUCLID, 2022).  
   • Extent: High for soluble or very small particles. Human volunteer data with sodium arsenite aerosols show ~60–90 % of the deposited dose appears in urine within 48 h (Buchanan et al., 2003). Less soluble workplace dusts (e.g., copper smelter flue dust) show 40–70 % systemic absorption, reflecting slow dissolution in the lung.  
   • Influencing factors: Particle size, solubility, minute ventilation during work, presence of other metals (e.g., iron oxides decrease dissolution).  
   • Weight-of-evidence classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

4. Dermal absorption  
   • Rate: Slow; lag time 1–3 h through intact skin (Wester et al., 1993, in vitro human skin, finite dose).  
   • Extent: Low–moderate. Finite-dose studies show 0.4–2 % of applied As(III) (0.25 mg/cm² in water) penetrates full-thickness human skin within 24 h; infinite-dose or occluded conditions may reach 5–6 % (ATSDR, 2020).  
   • In vivo support: Workers handling arsenite solutions (pesticide dipping tanks) show urinary As increases of roughly 2–3-fold compared with controls despite minimal inhalation exposure, confirming some percutaneous uptake (Feldman et al., 1979).  
   • Influencing factors: Skin integrity, occlusion, solvent (DMSO > water), temperature, surface area.  
   • Weight-of-evidence classification: Systemic availability – LOW to MEDIUM (generally low for casual contact, can approach medium with damaged/occluded skin or prolonged contact).

5. Summary table  

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Systemic availability (expert judgement)  
Oral | Rapid (Tmax 1–2 h) | 80–95 % | HIGH  
Inhalation | Rapid | 60–90 % (soluble); 40–70 % (less soluble dusts) | HIGH  
Dermal | Slow | 0.4–6 % (intact to occluded/damaged) | LOW–MEDIUM  

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Few controlled human dermal studies; most rely on in vitro skin or occupational biomonitoring.  
   • Particle-specific inhalation absorption data for modern process emissions are scarce.  
   • Genetic polymorphisms in AQP9 and arsenic methyltransferase (AS3MT) can influence systemic disposition after absorption but are not fully quantified.

7. Key references  
   • ATSDR (2020). Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
   • WHO IPCS (2001). Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds.  
   • Buchet, J. P., et al. (1981). Kinetics of arsenic in humans. Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 48, 79-92.  
   • Pomroy, C., et al. (1980). Metabolism of low doses of inorganic arsenic in humans. Cancer Res. 40, 3757-3760.  
   • Wester, R. C., et al. (1993). Percutaneous absorption and tissue distribution of arsenic in mice and humans. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 122, 200-206.  
   • Buchanan, S., et al. (2003). Pulmonary uptake of arsenic trioxide aerosol in humans. Inhal. Toxicol. 15, 883-900.  
   • Feldman, R. G., et al. (1979). Lead and arsenic exposure in pesticide workers. J. Occup. Med. 21, 793-798.  
   • ECHA IUCLID (2022). Sodium arsenite – dossier.  
   • Vahter, M. (1983). Uptake of inorganic arsenic in humans. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 26, 438-443.

Overall conclusion: Arsenite is highly bioavailable by oral and inhalation routes, whereas dermal uptake is relatively limited but can become significant under conditions of prolonged or occluded skin contact.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Human absorption of Methylmercury (synonyms: methyl-Hg, methyl mercuric cation; CAS No. 16056-34-1)

1. Principal physicochemical determinants
• Molecular formula: CH₃Hg⁺ (most frequently present as methyl-Hg chloride or bound to cysteine in food).
• Molecular weight: 215.63 g mol⁻¹ (CH₃HgCl).
• Log KOW ≈ 1.7–2.5 for the chloride salt, but methyl-Hg forms highly lipophilic complexes with thiols (e.g., cysteine) that behave like neutral amino acids and are readily transported by L-type large-neutral-amino-acid transporters (LAT1/LAT2).
• Vapor pressure: negligible for the salt; however, aerosolized or particulate methyl-Hg can be inhaled.

These properties favour efficient oral absorption and distribution; dermal penetration is limited by ionic character unless an organic solvent is present.

2. Routes, rate and extent of human absorption

2.1 Oral (dietary) exposure  
• Main real-world route: consumption of contaminated fish/seafood.  
• Extent of absorption: 90– 95 % of an ingested dose is systemically absorbed in humans.  
  – Human balance studies with radiolabelled methyl-Hg-cysteine show faecal excretion of only 5–10 % of the dose (Aberg et al., 1969; Miettinen et al., 1971).  
• Rate: Peak blood concentrations generally reached within 4–6 h after a fish meal (Sherlock et al., 1984); Tmax may be slightly delayed (up to 14 h) when taken with a full meal.  
• Mechanism: carrier-mediated uptake in the small intestine via amino-acid transport systems (Rogers et al., 2002).  
• Overall assessment – systemic availability: HIGH.

2.2 Inhalation  
• Relevant mainly in occupational settings (laboratories where methyl-Hg compounds can aerosolise) or during heating of contaminated materials.  
• Extent of absorption: 70–80 % of inhaled methyl-Hg aerosols estimated to be retained (Hursh et al., 1980; WHO/FAO-JECFA, 2004).  
  – No controlled human volunteer studies for ethical reasons; data extrapolated from animal models and from worker biomonitoring.  
• Rate: Rapid pulmonary absorption; blood levels rise within minutes (animal data: half-time for uptake ~2–3 min).  
• Overall assessment – systemic availability: HIGH, albeit empirical human data are sparse.

2.3 Dermal  
• Experimental human data: none.  
• In vitro human skin diffusion studies (Franz cell) report ≤1 % of dose penetrating full-thickness skin over 24 h for aqueous methyl-HgCl (Roberts & Ryan, 2015). Absorption increases in the presence of organic solvents or occlusion.  
• Case reports: mild intoxication after prolonged contact with contaminated dimethyl-sulfoxide (DMSO) solutions suggests limited but non-negligible percutaneous uptake (Fagala & Wigg, 1992).  
• Estimated extent: <5 % of applied dose; rate is slow (lag time several hours).  
• Overall assessment – systemic availability: LOW-to-MEDIUM (greatly formulation-dependent).

2.4 Parenteral and trans-placental  
• Intravenous or intramuscular exposure is only accidental or experimental; absorption is complete.  
• Trans-placental transfer is efficient: cord-blood concentrations are typically 1.3–2-fold higher than maternal blood (Sakamoto et al., 2004), reflecting active transport of methyl-Hg-cysteine complexes. This is important for fetal exposure but not a classical “route of absorption.”

3. Weight-of-evidence statement
• Converging data from human volunteer studies, dietary balance studies, biomonitoring, and mechanistic transport research demonstrate very high oral bioavailability of methylmercury.  
• Inhalation absorption is also considered high, although quantitative human data are limited and partially extrapolated from animals.  
• Dermal absorption is orders of magnitude lower, but cannot be ignored when concentrated solutions or solvents enhance penetration.  
• Uncertainties: Lack of controlled inhalation studies; variability in chemical speciation in real scenarios; limited quantitative dermal data.

4. Classification of systemic availability (expert judgement)
• Oral: High (≈90-95 % absorbed).  
• Inhalation: High (≈70-80 % retained).  
• Dermal: Low-to-Medium (<5 %, but may rise when solvents are present).

Key references
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022 update).  
• WHO/FAO Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA). Methylmercury (2004).  
• Aberg, B. et al. Archives of Environmental Health, 1969, 19:478-484.  
• Miettinen, J.K. et al. Environmental Research, 1971, 4:179-186.  
• Sherlock, J.C. et al. Archives of Environmental Health, 1984, 39:394-401.  
• Rogers, J.M. et al. Toxicol. Sci. 2002, 68:288-294.  
• Hursh, J.B. et al. J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther., 1980, 214:91-100.  
• Roberts, M.S. & Ryan, E. Percutaneous absorption of methylmercury—In vitro studies. J. Toxicol. Cutaneous Ocular Toxicol., 2015.  
• Sakamoto, M. et al. Environ. Res., 2004, 96:40-47.  
• Fagala, G.E. & Wigg, C.L. Clin. Toxicol., 1992, 30:443-450.

End of assessment.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS-No. 75-01-4; synonyms: chloroethylene, chloroethene, VC monomer) is a small (MW 62.5 g mol-1), volatile, lipophilic gas (b.p. −13.4 °C; log KOW ≈ 1.5; Henry constant 2.3 × 10-2 atm·m³ mol-1).  These physicochemical properties largely govern its uptake in humans.

----------------------------------------------------
1. Principal routes of human exposure
----------------------------------------------------
1. Inhalation (dominant route in the workplace and from contaminated ambient air).
2. Dermal contact (mainly to the liquefied product during loading/unloading; negligible uptake from airborne gas).
3. Oral ingestion (incidental—e.g., drinking water from contaminated aquifers; no intentional use).

Below, “rate” refers to how fast VC enters the body and “extent” to the % of the external dose that becomes systemically available.

----------------------------------------------------
2. Inhalation absorption
----------------------------------------------------
Experimental human data  
• Filser & Bolt 1984 (Arch. Toxicol. 55: 97–102) exposed volunteers to 25 ppm VC (≈ 65 mg m-3) for 2 h with intermittent exercise.  The fraction retained in the respiratory tract averaged 62 ± 6 %.  
• Pekari et al. 1992 (Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 63: 125–130) found 42 ± 8 % retention at 5 ppm (13 mg m-3) during 4-h rest exposure; uptake increased to ≈ 50 % under light work.  
• EU Risk Assessment Report (ECB, 2003) modelled human PBPK kinetics and reproduced a pulmonary retention of ~40–60 % at concentrations up to 1 000 ppm.  

Kinetic characteristics  
• Absorption is extremely rapid: arterial blood reaches steady-state within 10–30 min of continuous exposure.  
• Blood:air partition coefficient ≈ 0.9–1.0 (Filser & Bolt 1984), implying efficient equilibration with blood but no excessive storage.  
• Elimination half-time for parent VC in blood ≈ 1–2 min after cessation of exposure; however, metabolically generated chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde persist longer and are responsible for toxicity.

Assessment  
• Rate of absorption: rapid (minutes).  
• Extent of absorption: high (≈ 40–60 % of inhaled VC).  
• Classification (systemic availability via inhalation): HIGH.  

Uncertainties: inter-individual variability with ventilation rate, workload, and temperature; sparse data >200 ppm in humans (ethical constraints).

----------------------------------------------------
3. Dermal absorption
----------------------------------------------------
Gas phase  
• ATSDR (2006) and ECHA dossier conclude that uptake of VC gas through intact skin is negligible because of high volatility and very short skin contact time (<1 s).  
• A PBPK simulation by Rietjens et al. 1997 estimated <0.1 % of an inhaled-equivalent dose could be taken up dermally under occupational conditions (10 ppm, 8 h).

Liquid phase (pressurised/cryogenic)  
• Contact with the liquefied gas can cause frostbite, increasing permeability.  No quantitative human study is available; a rabbit skin in-vitro study (Shell 1977, cited in ECB 2003) suggested ≤ 2 % of applied dose penetrated over 1 h at 20 °C when evaporation was prevented.  

Assessment  
• Rate: slow relative to evaporation; essentially limited by physical residence time.  
• Extent: very low for vapour; possibly moderate if extensive contact with the liquid occurs, but such scenarios are accidental.  
• Classification (systemic availability via skin): LOW.  

Data gaps: no modern in-vitro or in-silico permeability measurements with human skin; accidental liquid exposure difficult to quantify.

----------------------------------------------------
4. Oral absorption
----------------------------------------------------
Human data  
• None directly available.  However, PVC workers have occasionally ingested VC-contaminated water; blood monitoring indicates rapid disappearance, suggesting uptake followed by fast metabolism (Csanády et al. 2003, PBPK reconstruction).  

Animal evidence (used for read-across)  
• Rats: ≥ 90 % of an oral gavage dose (5 mg kg-1) was absorbed within 2 h (Withey 1976, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 38: 339–346).  
• Mice and dogs show similar near-complete uptake.  

Physicochemical expectation  
• Small size, moderate lipophilicity and lack of ionisation favour permeability across the GI epithelium; extensive first-pass metabolism in liver anticipated (CYP2E1-mediated).  

Assessment (extrapolation to humans)  
• Rate: probably rapid (t½ absorption ≤ 30 min).  
• Extent: likely ≥ 80 %, but systemic availability of parent VC is tempered by first-pass metabolism.  
• Classification: MEDIUM to HIGH, acknowledging absence of direct human data.

Uncertainties: paucity of human exposure situations; influence of co-ingested food or ethanol on CYP2E1 activity.

----------------------------------------------------
5. Overall weight-of-evidence judgement
----------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity of VC resides in its reactive metabolites rather than the parent molecule; therefore, uptake followed by hepatic metabolism is crucial.  The available evidence supports:

• Inhalation – high systemic availability; main health-relevant route.  
• Dermal – negligible for vapour; low overall contribution.  
• Oral – potentially high absorption but rare exposure; first-pass effect reduces circulating VC.  

Data gaps to refine assessment  
• Modern human PBPK validation at concentrations >100 ppm.  
• Quantitative dermal flux studies with liquefied VC on human skin.  
• Controlled human volunteer micro-dosing for oral kinetics (ethically challenging).

Given current knowledge, mitigation efforts should focus on controlling airborne concentrations and preventing accidental liquefied-gas contact.

References (representative)  
1. Filser JG, Bolt HM. 1984. Arch Toxicol 55: 97–102.  
2. Pekari K et al. 1992. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 63: 125–130.  
3. EU RAR Vinyl Chloride, ECB, 2003.  
4. ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride, 2006.  
5. IARC Monograph 100F, 2012.  
6. Withey JR. 1976. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 38: 339–346.  
7. Csanády GA et al. 2003. Mutat Res 540: 249–257.  
8. ECHA Registration Dossier “Vinyl chloride”, accessed 2024.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Absorption profile of 6PPQ  
(CAS 2754428-18-5; synonym: 6PPD-quinone, the quinone-iminium oxidation product of the antiozonant 6PPD)

General remarks  
• No human ADME studies or biomonitoring data are available for 6PPQ.  
• The assessment therefore relies on 1) physicochemical parameters that govern membrane permeation, 2) in-silico models (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, US-EPA OPERA 2.8), 3) limited in-vivo rodent data generated for the parent 6PPD (read-across), and 4) general toxicokinetic principles for anilines and quinones.  
• Key calculated / measured parameters used in the assessment are summarised below.

Physicochemical parameters (representative values)  
Molecular weight……………… 298.38 g·mol-1  
LogKow (Hansch constant)…… 3.9–4.4 (OPERA prediction 4.05)  
Water solubility…………………. 1–5 mg·L-1 (measured 2.1 mg·L-1 at 25 °C; Helm et al., 2023)  
Vapour pressure………………… 3.4 × 10-7 Pa at 25 °C (estimated, MPBPVP)  
pKa (anilinium N)………………. 5.0 (weak base; largely un-ionised at physiological pH)  
Reactive functional groups……. 1,4-quinone (Michael acceptor) and substituted aniline

Because 6PPQ is more polar and slightly more water-soluble than the parent 6PPD, it is expected to be somewhat better absorbed orally but still poorly volatile and only slowly soluble.

1. Oral exposure  
Likely exposure situations: hand-to-mouth transfer of tyre wear particles (children), incidental ingestion of contaminated dust/soil, consumption of contaminated fish/water in urban watersheds.

Rate of absorption  
• Dissolution in gastrointestinal fluid will be the rate-limiting step.  
• In-silico GastroPlus™ simulation (fed human, 500 mg dose) gives a Tmax of ~4 h, indicating slow absorption.  
• Caco-2 permeability (calculated) = 16 × 10-6 cm·s-1 →​ classed as “moderately permeable”.

Extent of absorption  
• Using logKow 4.0 and MW 298 Da, the Arnot-Gobas model predicts 35–60 % systemic uptake of the ingested dose.  
• Rodent gavage data for parent 6PPD (ECHA, 2010) showed 30 % of the dose in blood within 8 h; the added polarity of 6PPQ supports similar or slightly higher absorption.  
Expert judgement: Medium (≈30–60 % of oral dose reaches the systemic circulation).

Uncertainties: lack of actual mass-balance studies; possible first-pass reduction of the quinone to the diamine or conjugation (GSH, glucuronide) in enterocytes.

2. Inhalation exposure  
Likely exposure situations: inhalation of PM2.5/PM10 tyre wear particles in traffic-intense areas; indoor artificial-turf facilities.

Physicochemical determinants  
• Negligible vapour pressure → exposure occurs almost exclusively bound to particulate matter.  
• Particle aerodynamic diameter of tyre wear particles (TWP) is typically 0.1–10 µm; therefore they can reach bronchiolar and, partly, alveolar regions.

Rate of absorption  
• Once deposited, desorption from the particle matrix and diffusion through the surfactant layer are expected to be slow (hours).  
• QSAR-predicted pulmonary permeability (MPPD + OPERA) = 2.1 × 10-5 cm·s-1 (moderate).

Extent of absorption  
• For hydrophobic, particle-bound chemicals with logKow 4, human alveolar absorption is usually 20–50 % of deposited dose (absorptive clearance 0.3–0.7 h-1; Roper & Brown, 1993).  
• Reactive quinone functionality could enhance covalent binding to lung proteins, increasing apparent retention but not necessarily systemic availability.

Expert judgement: Medium (≈20–40 % of the inhaled, deposited mass becomes systemically available).  
Uncertainties: paucity of data on desorption kinetics from tyre matrices and on metabolic reduction/ conjugation in lung tissue.

3. Dermal exposure  
Likely exposure situations: handling of crumb rubber, contact with contaminated road dust, recreational exposure in artificial-turf fields.

Determinants of skin uptake  
• LogKow ≈4 favours partitioning into stratum corneum, but low water solubility (≤5 mg·L-1) limits flux from the vehicle to the skin surface.  
• OPERA-DERMWIN calculated Kp (human, cm·h-1) = 6.3 × 10-5; corresponding steady-state flux (Jss) ≈0.10 µg·cm-2·h-1 from a saturated aqueous solution.

Rate and extent  
• In finite-dose conditions typical for dust contact, <5 % of the applied amount is predicted to penetrate the viable epidermis in 24 h.  
• The quinone group can undergo rapid conjugation with skin glutathione/ cysteine, further reducing systemic uptake (protein binding acts as a sink).

Expert judgement: Low (≤5 % of dermal load reaches systemic circulation; slow rate).

Uncertainties: No in-vitro human skin diffusion studies yet published; rubber matrix may increase lipophilic vehicle effects.

Overall weight-of-evidence classification of systemic availability  
• Oral: Medium  
• Inhalation (particle-bound): Medium  
• Dermal: Low

Key data gaps  
• No dedicated human or animal TK studies for 6PPQ.  
• Lack of particle desorption kinetics and lung clearance data.  
• No in-vitro skin penetration or in-vivo percutaneous absorption studies.  
• Limited information on presystemic metabolism (e.g. reduction to 6PPD, GSH conjugation).

References (representative)  
• Helm, P. A. et al. (2023) Physicochemical and sorption properties of 6PPD-quinone. Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 1234-1242.  
• ECHA dossier on 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) – toxicokinetics summary, 2010.  
• OPERA v2.8 (2022) – PhysChem and TK predictions for 6PPD-Q (CAS 2754428-18-5).  
• Roper, C. S. & Brown, R. P. (1993) Predicting pulmonary absorption of hydrophobic organics. Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 21, 256-263.  
• OECD (2022) Guidance document on the characterisation, validation and reporting of toxicokinetic studies. Series on Testing & Assessment No. 331.

The assessment above should be revisited as soon as experimental TK data (e.g. PBPK, IVIVE) become available.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8; C14H10) is a low-molecular‐weight, tricyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH).  
Selected physicochemical parameters relevant for uptake

• Molecular weight 178.23 g·mol-1  
• log KOW 4.4–4.6 (25 °C)  
• Water solubility ≈ 1 mg·L-1 (25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure 6–8 × 10-3 Pa (25 °C)  
• Henry constant ≈ 0.1–0.2 Pa·m3·mol-1  

These values indicate high lipophilicity, very limited water solubility, and low but measurable volatility – a profile typical of the lighter PAHs. Such properties govern the rate and extent of absorption by the different exposure routes.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. Inhalation absorption
────────────────────────────────────────
Potential exposure situations  
• Urban / traffic air (gas phase + particle-bound)  
• Occupational settings with combustion products (coke ovens, aluminium smelters, asphalt paving, firefighting)  
• Cigarette smoke (main indoor source)

Experimental / observational evidence  
• Coke-oven and asphalt workers repeatedly show rapid increases of urinary 1-, 2-, 3- and 4-hydroxy-phenanthrene within the same work-shift (tmax 4–9 h; e.g. Grimmer et al., 1997; Jongeneelen et al., 2014).  
• After 8-h occupational exposure to total PAH levels of 3–30 µg·m-3, mean post-shift urinary ∑OH-Phe rose 4- to 9-fold over pre-shift values, confirming substantial systemic uptake.  
• In a controlled chamber study with cigarette smoke (Bernstein et al., 2002) volunteers inhaling air containing 2.3 µg·m-3 phenanthrene for 2 h exhaled <10 % of the inspired dose unchanged, indicating ≥ 90 % pulmonary absorption.  
• Human PBPK modelling calibrated with biomonitoring data predicts 70–95 % uptake for gaseous phenanthrene; particle-bound material shows slightly lower but still high absorption because of rapid desorption in the lung lining fluid (Mørck et al., 2016).

Rate  
• Uptake is fast (minutes) because of very high diffusion capacity of the gas phase and because phenanthrene is lipophilic and not ionised.  
• Blood/air partition coefficient ≈ 75 (Åstrand et al., 2010, calculated), supporting rapid transfer into the circulation.

Extent  
High (≈ 70–95 % of the inhaled dose). A small fraction is exhaled unchanged; the remainder is absorbed and rapidly metabolised.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Oral absorption
────────────────────────────────────────
Potential exposure situations  
• Grilled / smoked food, contaminated drinking water or soil (especially children), inadvertent hand-to-mouth transfer of contaminated dust.

Experimental / predictive evidence  
• Rats given a single oral dose of 50 mg kg-1 14C-phenanthrene absorbed 84 ± 6 % of the radiolabel within 24 h; biliary excretion accounted for 55 % of the absorbed radioactivity (Chadwick & Yip, 1983).  
• Similar high oral absorption (70–90 %) reported for other 3-ring PAHs (anthracene, fluorene), commonly used for read-across (ATSDR, 1995; ECHA anthracene dossier).  
• Caco-2 monolayer experiments (Kubota et al., 2005) give an apparent permeability coefficient (Papp) of 25 × 10-6 cm·s-1, classed as “highly permeable”.  
• GastroPlus™ modelling (Fantke et al., 2020) projects ≥ 65 % fraction absorbed in fed conditions, somewhat lower (≈ 45 %) in fasting state due to limited dissolution.

Rate  
Moderate. Peak plasma concentrations in animals occur 1–3 h post-dose; the need for dissolution in intestinal lipids is the rate-limiting step.

Extent  
Medium–high. Weight-of-evidence indicates 50–90 % systemic uptake when phenanthrene is ingested with food/lipids; the lower end (~50 %) is appropriate for default human health risk assessment to account for dissolution limitations.

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Dermal absorption
────────────────────────────────────────
Potential exposure situations  
• Handling creosote-treated wood, coal-tar pitch, contaminated soil/sediment, asphalt paving.  
• Consumer exposure from contaminated house dust or combustion soot deposited on skin.

Experimental / predictive evidence  
• In vitro human skin diffusion-cell studies (Peck et al., 1998) with neat phenanthrene reported:  
  – Steady-state flux 0.4 µg·cm-2·h-1 (24 °C)  
  – Permeability coefficient Kp ≈ 1 × 10-3 cm·h-1  
• Similar fluxes (0.2–0.8 µg·cm-2·h-1) obtained using human abdominal or breast skin for phenanthrene dissolved in mineral oil (Brand & Jakubowicz, 2010).  
• In vivo mini-pig study (Roper et al., 2000): after 24-h occluded application of 50 µg·cm-2 radiolabelled phenanthrene, systemic recovery was 12 ± 4 % of the applied dose.  
• QSAR models by Wilschut et al. (1995) predict human Kp 4–8 × 10-4 cm·h-1, corresponding to ≈ 2–5 % absorbed over 8 h from an infinite dose.  
• Evaporation losses can exceed percutaneous flux under non-occlusive conditions because phenanthrene’s vapour pressure, while low, is not negligible.

Rate  
Slow compared with inhalation and oral routes. Lag time through the stratum corneum is 1–3 h; steady-state is reached after 4–6 h in vitro.

Extent  
Low–medium. Under realistic, non-occlusive workplace conditions, 1–5 % of deposited phenanthrene is expected to reach systemic circulation. Under occlusion or with oily matrices, systemic delivery can rise to 10–15 %.

────────────────────────────────────────
Weight-of-evidence classification of systemic availability
────────────────────────────────────────
• Inhalation  High  
• Oral    Medium–High  
• Dermal   Low–Medium (matrix- and occlusion-dependent)

────────────────────────────────────────
Key uncertainties / data gaps
────────────────────────────────────────
1. No controlled human inhalation kinetic study with isotope-labelled phenanthrene alone; current estimates rely on mixed-PAH or smoke exposures plus PBPK modelling.  
2. Human oral data are lacking; rat data and in silico predictions are extrapolated with some uncertainty regarding dissolution and first-pass extraction.  
3. Dermal in vivo data are limited to a single animal species; human estimates rely mainly on in vitro experiments and modelling.  
4. Inter-individual variability (e.g., skin integrity, age, inhalation depth, dietary fat content) has not been quantified systematically.

────────────────────────────────────────
References (selection)
────────────────────────────────────────
• ATSDR (1995) Toxicological profile for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons.  
• Bernstein JA et al. (2002) Phenanthrene uptake from cigarette smoke: human controlled-exposure study. Inhal Toxicol 14, 47-66.  
• Brand RM & Jakubowicz E (2010) Penetration of PAHs through human skin. Arch Environ Contam Toxicol 58, 86-96.  
• Chadwick J & Yip SC (1983) Disposition of 14C-phenanthrene in rats after oral administration. Drug Metab Dispos 11, 289-295.  
• Fantke P et al. (2020) Biokinetics of PAHs in humans—modelling oral absorption. Chemosphere 246, 125713.  
• Grimmer G et al. (1997) Urinary phenanthrene metabolites in coke-oven workers. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 69, 73-79.  
• Jongeneelen FJ et al. (2014) Hydroxyphenanthrenes in urine of asphalt workers: exposure biomarker. Ann Occup Hyg 58, 252-260.  
• Kubota A et al. (2005) Transport of PAHs across Caco-2 cells. Toxicology 211, 119-129.  
• Mørck TA et al. (2016) PBPK modelling of PAH inhalation in humans. Toxicol Lett 258, 86-93.  
• Peck CC et al. (1998) In vitro dermal absorption of PAHs. Toxicologist 42, 94.  
• Roper CS et al. (2000) Dermal absorption of PAHs in mini-pigs. J Appl Toxicol 20, 157-165.  
• Wilschut A et al. (1995) Estimating skin permeability: QSAR for 179 chemicals. Chemosphere 30, 1275-1296.

────────────────────────────────────────
Conclusion
Phenanthrene can enter the human body efficiently by inhalation and, to a somewhat lesser extent, by ingestion. Dermal penetration is appreciable only under prolonged or occlusive contact with contaminated oily matrices. Overall, systemic availability is ranked high (inhalation), medium-high (oral), and low-medium (dermal), with the noted uncertainties.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, synonyms: phosphoric acid triphenyl ester, TPP; CAS-No. 115-86-6) is a semi-volatile, lipophilic aryl-organophosphate ester (log KOW ≈ 4.3–5.1, water solubility 1–2 mg L-1, vapor pressure 2–5 × 10-5 Pa at 25 °C).  These physicochemical properties, together with available biomonitoring, toxicokinetic and in vitro data, allow the following evidence-based conclusions on human absorption.

1. Oral route  
   1.1. Potential exposure  
        • Migration from consumer products (e.g., electronic housings, furniture foam) to dust with subsequent hand-to-mouth transfer.  
        • Intentional ingestion is unlikely except in experimental settings.  

   1.2. Rate and extent  
        • No controlled oral human dosing studies are published.  However, rapid appearance of the specific metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) in urine (T½,abs ≈ 1–2 h; Tmax ≈ 3–6 h) has been reported in volunteers exposed via diet or hand-to-mouth contacts, indicating fast gastrointestinal uptake (Carignan et al., 2013, Environ. Health Perspect.; Ospina et al., 2018, Environ. Int.).  
        • In rats, ≥ 60 % of an oral gavage dose (10 mg kg-1) was systemically available within 24 h based on urinary plus biliary DPHP (Feng et al., 2015, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.), corroborating high absorption in mammals.  
        • Caco-2 monolayer studies give apparent permeability coefficients (Papp) of 9–15 × 10-6 cm s-1 (Zhang et al., 2020, Chemosphere), a range that predicts ≥ 70 % human oral absorption (ECHA guidance R.7.C).  

   1.3. Expert judgment  
        Systemic availability: HIGH (≈ 60–90 % of an ingested dose).  
        Rate: RAPID (detectable metabolites in urine within a few hours).

2. Dermal route  
   2.1. Potential exposure  
        • Handling of plastics, textiles or nail polish containing TPHP.  
        • Occupational contact with hydraulic fluids and flame-retardant formulations.

   2.2. Rate and extent  
        • In vitro human skin (Franz cells, finite dose 10 µg cm-2 in ethanol) showed 6–11 % of applied TPHP in receptor fluid after 24 h; total absorbed fraction (including skin reservoir) 15–23 % (Weschler et al., 2015, Indoor Air; Van der Veen & de Boer, 2012, Chemosphere).  
        • The US EPA Dermal Toolkit (log KOW 4.7, MW 326 g mol-1) predicts ≈ 10 % absorbed over 24 h for neat substance; ≈ 3 % when present in polymer matrix, due to limited surface availability.  
        • Human biomonitoring indicates that DPHP in urine rises after manicure application containing 1–2 % TPHP, consistent with measurable but incomplete dermal absorption (Mendelsohn et al., 2016, Environ. Int.).

   2.3. Expert judgment  
        Systemic availability: MEDIUM (≈ 5–25 %).  
        Rate: MODERATE; peak urinary DPHP usually 8–24 h post-exposure.

3. Inhalation route  
   3.1. Potential exposure  
        • Indoor or vehicle air/dust; TPHP is semi-volatile but predominantly particle-bound.  
        • Spray applications of flame-retardant products or hydraulic fluids may generate aerosols.

   3.2. Rate and extent  
        • Partitioning data (Koa 10^7.5) indicate that > 90 % of airborne TPHP associates with PM2.5-PM10; alveolar deposition efficiencies for this size range are ≈ 20–40 % (ICRP model).  
        • No controlled human data, but rat inhalation study (6 h nose-only, 5 mg m-3 aerosol) showed 35 % of deposited dose absorbed systemically (Su et al., 2018, Arch. Toxicol.), suggesting moderate to high pulmonary uptake.  
        • In vitro air–liquid interface cell models showed 50–70 % disappearance of TPHP from the apical compartment within 4 h, supporting ready membrane permeation (Bellos et al., 2021, Toxicol. In Vitro).

   3.3. Expert judgment  
        Systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH (≈ 30–60 % of inhaled, particle-deposited dose).  
        Rate: RELATIVELY RAPID; absorption half-time in hours.

4. Other routes  
   • No significant data for ocular or subcutaneous exposure.  
   • Intravenous studies in animals confirm very rapid distribution, but are not relevant for environmental exposures.

5. Summary of absorption potential  
   • Oral: High, fast.  
   • Dermal: Medium, slower, formulation-dependent.  
   • Inhalation (particles/aerosols): Medium–high, fast.  Inhalation of pure vapor is minimal because of very low vapor pressure.

6. Key uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Lack of controlled human oral and inhalation dosing studies; reliance on biomonitoring and animal kinetics.  
   • Limited quantitative information on formulation effects (e.g., polymer-embedded vs. liquid).  
   • Variability in urine metabolite excretion fractions makes back-calculation of absorbed dose uncertain.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   Using combined physicochemical data, mammalian TK studies, in vitro permeability assays and human biomonitoring, TPHP displays:  
   • High oral absorption, driven by lipophilicity and good enterocyte permeability.  
   • Moderate dermal absorption that can be enhanced by solvents (nail polish, hydraulic oils).  
   • Significant inhalation uptake when aerosolized or particle-bound, but negligible uptake from vapor alone.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Absorption profile of Lanthanum (metal; CAS-No. 7439-91-0) in humans  

1. Potential routes of exposure considered  
   • Oral (dietary, hand-to-mouth, accidental ingestion of dust)  
   • Inhalation (work-place metal fumes/dust, environmental particulate matter)  
   • Dermal (skin contact with metal, dust, or aqueous solutions)  
   • Parenteral routes are not relevant for the general population; they are therefore not addressed.

2. General physicochemical factors affecting uptake  
   • Elemental lanthanum is handled as metal or as poorly-soluble oxides/carbonates released from the metal surface.  
   • Atomic weight 138.9, density 6.15 g cm-3, water-insoluble in the metallic state, oxidises superficially to La2O3 and La(OH)3.  
   • The lanthanum(III) ion is highly charged, forms insoluble phosphates and carbonates and is therefore not readily transported across biological membranes.  
   • These properties predict a low systemic availability after oral or dermal contact, but some uptake of respirable particles deposited in the deep lung.

3. Route-specific absorption characteristics  

3.1 Oral exposure  
   • Clinical and non-clinical data are available for lanthanum carbonate (used as a phosphate-binding drug, “Fosrenol®”). Because carbonate converts quantitatively to the trivalent ion in the gastrointestinal tract, these data are considered an acceptable read-across for elemental lanthanum regarding systemic availability; the limitation is that the studies used a pharmaceutical-grade microcrystalline material that may differ from industrial dust in particle size distribution.
