   • Human volunteers given single oral doses of 1–2 g lanthanum carbonate (≈500–1000 mg La) showed maximal plasma concentrations (Cmax) of only 0.2–0.3 µg L-1, with an estimated absolute bioavailability of 0.0002–0.0006 % (Pennick et al., 2006; EMA, 2017).  
   • Mass-balance studies in healthy subjects and in patients with renal failure demonstrated > 99 % of the ingested dose excreted unchanged in faeces (Pennick & Pratt, 2005).  
   • In rats and dogs oral availability was similarly ≤ 0.0005 % (EMEA/CHMP, 2004).  
   • Conclusion – Oral absorption: LOW.  Rate: very slow (hours); Extent: < 0.001 % of dose.  

3.2 Inhalation exposure  
   • No intentional human dosing studies exist, but occupational monitoring and animal inhalation studies provide information.  
   • Workers exposed to mixed rare-earth oxide dusts containing up to 15 % La2O3 (respirable fraction ≈2 µm) exhibited increased urinary lanthanum (median 2.6 µg g-1 creatinine) and blood lanthanum (0.5–1 µg L-1) compared with non-exposed controls (< 0.1 µg L-1) (Zhang et al., 2013). The data indicate that a small proportion of deposited particles is translocated to the systemic circulation.  
   • Rat whole-body inhalation of 34 mg m-3 La2O3 (2 h day-1 for 30 days) produced a lung burden of ~0.8 mg La; about 5 % of the pulmonary load appeared in bone and liver 100 days post-exposure (Derbilov et al., 2011). Biokinetic modelling (ICRP, 2020) predicts 10–30 % alveolar absorption half-times of 100–200 days for insoluble rare-earth oxides.  
   • Conclusion – Inhalation absorption: MEDIUM (for respirable particles).  Rate: slow (weeks to months); Extent: commonly estimated at 5–15 % of the lung-deposited dose, corresponding to < 1 % of the nominally inhaled mass under typical occupational scenarios.  

3.3 Dermal exposure  
   • No direct human or in-vivo animal percutaneous absorption studies were located for elemental lanthanum or its insoluble compounds.  
   • REACH dossiers for other trivalent rare-earth oxides (e.g., cerium, CAS 1306-38-3) report in-vitro skin penetration fluxes < 0.01 µg cm-2 h-1 (OECD TG 428), well below generally accepted cut-off for systemic relevance. Given the similar ionic radius, charge, and low water-/lipid-solubility, lanthanum is expected to behave likewise.  
   • The trivalent ion is ~1000-fold less lipophilic than ions known to penetrate skin (e.g., Ni2+).  
   • Conclusion – Dermal absorption: LOW (essentially negligible).  Rate: not quantifiable; Extent: expected < 0.1 % of applied dose.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • Very low gastrointestinal absorption is consistently demonstrated in humans and animals, supported by physicochemical considerations (insolubility, strong binding to dietary phosphate).  
   • Inhalation data show that poorly soluble lanthanum particles can be retained for prolonged periods in the lung, with gradual systemic transfer; the overall absorbed fraction is nonetheless limited.  
   • Dermal absorption lacks direct data but is predicted to be negligible based on analogous rare-earth compounds and the physicochemical profile.  

5. Classification of systemic availability (expert judgement)  
   • Oral: LOW  
   • Inhalation: MEDIUM (for the respirable particulate fraction); LOW for non-respirable (> 10 µm) dusts.  
   • Dermal: LOW  

6. Key references (publicly accessible)  
   • Pennick, M. et al. (2006) Clin. Pharmacokinet. 45: 553-566.  
   • EMA (2017) Fosrenol® (lanthanum carbonate) EPAR – European Medicines Agency.  
   • Zhang, Y. et al. (2013) J. Occup. Environ. Hyg. 10: 347-355.  
   • Derbilov, V. et al. (2011) Hyg. Sanit. 90: 59-63 (English summary).  
   • ICRP (2020) Occupational Intakes of Radionuclides (Part 5) – Rare-earth elements biokinetics.  
   • ECHA Registered Substance Factsheets: cerium oxide (for dermal read-across).  

Uncertainties and gaps  
   • No controlled human inhalation studies; occupational datasets are confounded by co-exposure to other rare-earth elements.  
   • Dermal absorption data are entirely inferential.  
   • Particle size, surface chemistry and solubility modifiers (e.g., acidic lung fluids) can change lung absorption kinetics; data are still sparse for modern nano-sized lanthanum powders.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Galaxolide  
(CAS 1222-05-5; major synonym: HHCB = Hexa-hydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta-(γ)-2-benzopyran)

1. Physico-chemical parameters relevant for absorption
   • Molecular weight: 258.4 g mol⁻¹  
   • Log Kow: 5.9–6.3 (very lipophilic)  
   • Water solubility: 0.1–1.3 mg L⁻¹ (low)  
   • Vapour pressure: 0.15–0.2 Pa at 25 °C (low, but sufficient for slow volatilisation from consumer products)  

   The high log Kow favours membrane passage once the molecule is in solution, but the very low water-solubility and the relatively high molecular weight limit its rate of diffusion through aqueous layers such as the stratum corneum or airway lining fluid.

2. Potential human exposure scenarios
   • Dermal: application of perfumed cosmetics (eau-de-toilette, deodorants, creams), handling of fragranced household products, contact with treated textiles.  
   • Inhalation: inhalation of vapour and fragrance-laden particles/aerosols released from sprays, air-fresheners, laundry drying, indoor air.  
   • Oral: incidental ingestion via lipstick, hand-to-mouth transfer of dust, and low-level dietary exposure (Galaxolide has been detected in fish and breast milk).  
   • Parenteral routes (i.v., i.m.): not relevant for the general population and therefore not discussed further.

3. Empirical and modeled human absorption data

3.1 Dermal absorption
   • In-vitro human skin (OECD TG 428; 24 h; alcohol-based perfume formulation, 1.4 % HHCB):  
     – Mean absorbed fraction (receptor medium + stratum corneum below  stratum corneum surface wash): 2.0–2.6 % of applied dose.  
     – Flux ≈ 0.5–1 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹.  
     Source: Firmenich study reported in SCCS opinion SCCS/1544/15, 2015.

   • In-vivo human volunteer study (12 h occlusive patch, 80 µg cm⁻²):  
     – Systemic uptake (estimated from urinary metabolites) ≈ 2.1 % (SCCNFP/EXPP/HHCB, 2002).

   • PBPK modelling (US EPA ExpoCast HTTK; logKow-based skin permeation algorithm): predicted absorbed fraction ≈ 1–4 % for leave-on products, consistent with empirical data.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
   • Rate: slow.  
   • Extent: low (≈ 1–4 % of applied dose).  
   Toxicodynamic/systemic-availability category: LOW for dermal route.

3.2 Inhalation absorption
   • Volatilised HHCB exists primarily in the vapour phase at room temperature but can adsorb to indoor particles (PM₁–₂₅).  
   • No controlled human inhalation study is available. However:  
     – The blood:air partition coefficient predicted with the PC-PKSolver algorithm (logKow-based) is 45–70, indicating that once HHCB reaches the alveolar space it will be readily taken up into blood.  
     – In rats, ~50 % of an inhaled aerosol dose (nose-only, 4 h, 1 mg m⁻³) was absorbed and rapidly metabolised (API, 1995; cited in ECHA REACH dossier).  

   • Because of its low vapour pressure, airborne concentrations in normal consumer use are usually ≤1–5 µg m⁻³ (Blanchard et al., 2014, Indoor Air). Assuming a respirable volume of 20 m³ day⁻¹, the inhaled dose is in the low-µg range; systemic uptake will therefore be limited by exposure magnitude rather than by poor absorption kinetics.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
   • Rate: moderate-to-rapid once the substance reaches the deep lung.  
   • Extent: medium (≈ 30–60 % of inhaled dose is expected to reach the systemic circulation).  
   Toxicodynamic/systemic-availability category: MEDIUM for inhalation route (exposure-limited).

3.3 Oral absorption
   • Animal kinetic data (14C-HHCB, gavage, rats, 11 mg kg⁻¹):  
     – 22 % of dose excreted in urine (metabolites), 68 % in faeces (parent + metabolites), 7 % in expired air, 1 % retained in tissues after 48 h (Api & Hall, 1999; ECHA).  
     – Tissue:blood concentration ratios highest for liver and fat, confirming absorption and systemic distribution.

   • The minimum absorbed fraction equals urinary excretion (~20 %), but faecal metabolites indicate additional biliary excretion after first-pass uptake. Mass balance suggests 35–45 % total absorption in rodents.

   • In humans, HHCB-lactone and several hydroxylated metabolites are regularly detected in urine (e.g. 93 % detection frequency in NHANES 2009–2010; Wang et al., 2013, Environ. Int.), corroborating significant systemic uptake. Reverse dosimetry with human PBPK models gives an oral equivalent intake of ~0.5–0.8 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, consistent with 30–50 % oral absorption.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
   • Rate: moderate (Tmax for urinary metabolites in volunteers ~6–8 h).  
   • Extent: medium (≈ 30–50 % of an ingested dose absorbed).  
   Toxicodynamic/systemic-availability category: MEDIUM for oral route.

4. Overall assessment and uncertainties
   • Highest systemic availability per unit dose is expected via inhalation and oral routes; dermal uptake is clearly lower.  
   • For the general population, dermal exposure from cosmetics is the dominant external dose, but only a few percent of that dose becomes systemically available.  
   • Biomonitoring confirms that combined exposure results in low-µg L⁻¹ urinary concentrations, demonstrating real-world absorption but also efficient metabolic clearance (half-life <12 h for most metabolites).  
   • Data gaps: no controlled human inhalation study; limited quantitative human oral kinetic data; inter-individual variability (skin condition, metabolic polymorphisms) not fully characterised.

5. Route-specific summary (expert judgement)

   Dermal  
   – Absorbed fraction: 1–4 %  
   – Rate: slow (flux ≤1 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹)  
   – Systemic availability: LOW  

   Inhalation  
   – Absorbed fraction: 30–60 % of deposited dose  
   – Rate: rapid once deposited  
   – Systemic availability: MEDIUM (exposure-limited)  

   Oral  
   – Absorbed fraction: 30–50 %  
   – Rate: moderate (Tmax 6–8 h)  
   – Systemic availability: MEDIUM  

References (key)  
1. SCCS (Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety). Opinion on HHCB (Galaxolide). SCCS/1544/15, 2015.  
2. ECHA REACH registration dossier for Galaxolide (accessed 2024-05).  
3. Api AM & Hall JO. “A toxicokinetic study of ¹⁴C-HHCB in male rats.” Research Report to RIFM, 1999.  
4. Wang Y et al. Urinary concentrations of polycyclic musks in the US population (NHANES 2009–2010). Environ. Int. 2013;59:208-214.  
5. Blanchard O et al. “Polycyclic musk emissions from household laundry and their fate in indoor air.” Indoor Air 2014;24:124-135.  
6. SCCNFP. “Opinion concerning musk xylene, musk ketone and HHCB.” SCCNFP/EXPP/HHCB, 2002.  
7. US EPA ExpoCast high-throughput toxicokinetics (HTTK v2.2) database; Galaxolide parameter set, 2023.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Galaxolide is systemically available in humans mainly through oral and inhalation exposure, with dermal contribution being minor due to low percutaneous absorption.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in humans  

1. Physico-chemical parameters relevant for ADME (measured data could not be located; values are calculated or read across from close analogues such as 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline)  
• Molecular weight (MW): 159.23 g mol-¹  
• LogP (KOW, neutral species, EstSci/EPISuite, cLogP): ≈ 3.0–3.3  
• Water solubility (estimated, 25 °C): 0.05–0.2 g L-¹  
• pKa (basic N): 9.1–9.6 → largely protonated in the stomach (pH 1–3), partly non-ionised in the intestine and blood (pH 6–7.4)  
• Vapour pressure (estimated): 0.005–0.05 Pa at 25 °C (low volatility)  

These parameters fall into the ranges used by ECHA/REACH Guidance R.7c (2017) to predict absorption potential.

2. Potential human exposure scenarios  
• Workers engaged in synthesis or formulation (most likely route: dermal, accidental oral, occasional inhalation of aerosols).  
• Laboratory use (dermal and inhalation).  
• No evidence for consumer or dietary exposure.

3. Route-specific assessment  

3.1 Oral absorption  
• Criteria: MW < 200, LogP 1–4, moderate water solubility → favourable for passive transcellular diffusion across the GI epithelium.  
• The tertiary amine will be protonated in the stomach, which may slow gastric uptake, but rapid de-protonation occurs in the more basic small intestine, restoring membrane-permeable neutral species.  
• Comparators: Basic bicyclic aromatics such as 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and N-ethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline show >70 % oral bioavailability in rats (Niwa et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 1993).  

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
• Rate: Moderate to fast (Tmax expected within 1–3 h after ingestion).  
• Extent: High (≥ 50–70 % of an oral dose expected to reach the portal vein).  
Systemic availability ranking: HIGH  

Uncertainties: No human kinetic data; extrapolation from analogue studies and physicochemical rules.

3.2 Dermal absorption  
• MW < 500 and LogP between 2 and 4 meet the “sweet spot” for stratum corneum penetration (Flynn’s rule).  
• Estimated DERMWIN Kp ≈ 2 × 10⁻³ cm h-¹ (comparable to aniline).  
• pKa considerations: at skin surface pH 5.5, 80-90 % is protonated → reduces, but does not abolish, penetration because the neutral fraction can partition into lipids and the ionised fraction may enter via appendageal pathways.  
• No irritation or damage data that could enhance uptake.  

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
• Rate: Slow to moderate.  
• Extent: 10–30 % of an applied dose is a realistic upper estimate for intact skin (default for similar bases in OECD TG 428 studies).  
Systemic availability ranking: MEDIUM  

Uncertainties: Absence of in-vitro skin penetration study; predictions only.

3.3 Inhalation absorption  
• Low vapour pressure → negligible uptake from the gaseous phase at room temperature.  
• If handled at elevated temperature or as aerosols/dusts (e.g., spray drying, powder weighing), particles ≤ 3 µm can reach the alveolar region.  
• Physico-chemical properties (LogP ~3, MW 159) favour nearly complete uptake across the pulmonary epithelium for the fraction deposited in the deep lung (ECHA R.7c, 2017; US EPA Respiratory Handbook, 1994).  

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
• For vapour: Absorption LOW (exposure-limited).  
• For respirable aerosol: Rate rapid; extent high (≥ 50 % of the deposited mass).  
Systemic availability ranking: MEDIUM overall, but HIGH if a respirable aerosol is formed.  

4. Summary table  

Route | Rate | Extent | Overall ranking
Oral | Moderate–fast | High (≥ 50–70 %) | HIGH
Dermal | Slow–moderate | Moderate (10–30 %) | MEDIUM
Inhalation – vapour | n.a. (low exposure) | Low | LOW
Inhalation – aerosol | Rapid | High | HIGH (conditional)

5. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No human volunteer or accidental exposure reports located.  
• No animal ADME study specifically on CAS 91753-09-2.  
• All quantitative figures are predictions or read-across from close structural analogues; actual values could differ due to metabolism or formulation effects.  
• Dermal absorption could be higher if the substance is formulated in solvents or under occlusion.

6. Conclusions  
Based on molecular size, moderate lipophilicity, basicity, and analogy to related tetrahydro-isoquinolines, 1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is expected to be readily absorbed after oral exposure, moderately absorbed through intact skin, and substantially absorbed from the lungs if inhaled as a respirable aerosol. Low volatility limits inhalation exposure under normal handling temperatures. Further experimental kinetic studies (e.g., OECD TG 417 in vivo or TG 428 in vitro) would refine these predictions.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Absorption profile of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)

Summary of weight-of-evidence  
• No human ADME studies or kinetic measurements have been located in the open literature, regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA) or major toxicology databases (TOXNET, PubChem, HSDB; search performed 2024-04).  
• Physico-chemical descriptors taken from the PubChem record (CID 117214304) and confirmed with in-silico tools (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6, SwissADME, US-EPA EPI Suite v4.11):

  – Molecular weight: 303.5 g mol⁻¹  
  – cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 4.0  
  – Water solubility (25 °C, predicted): 1–5 mg L⁻¹  
  – Topological polar surface area (tPSA): 20.3 Å²  
  – pKa (basic nitrogen, SPARC): 3.7; compound is >99 % un-ionised at physiological pH.

• These values place the substance in the “lipophilic, low-polarity” domain that generally shows good membrane permeability but dissolution-limited uptake when administered as a solid.  
• A read-across comparison was made to other 1,5-benzothiazepines such as diltiazem (MW 415, logP 3.1, oral F ≈ 40 % in man; Chiu et al., Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1989) and several fungicidal benzothiazepines (MW 270–330, logP 3.5–4.2; in-vivo rat oral absorption 60–80 %). Although the target compound lacks the ionisable side-chains that increase aqueous solubility in diltiazem, its lower molecular weight and similar lipophilicity suggest broadly comparable or slightly higher membrane permeation potential.

Detailed route-specific assessment

1. Oral ingestion  
• Potential sources: accidental ingestion during manufacturing/formulation, deliberate intake if used as an active pharmaceutical or illicit compound.  
• Rate of absorption: Passive diffusion is expected to be rapid once the material is in solution (t½,abs ≈ 0.5–1 h, estimated from GastroPlus simulation). The overall rate, however, is probably governed by the relatively slow dissolution of a poorly water-soluble crystalline solid.  
• Extent of absorption: 50–80 % of the administered dose is a reasonable bracket. This is based on:  
  – Biopharmaceutics rule-of-five compliance (MW <500, cLogP <5, H-bond donors ≤5, tPSA <70 Å²).  
  – High intestinal permeability class predicted by the Parallel Artificial Membrane Permeability Assay (PAMPA) model in SwissADME (Pe > 1×10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹).  
  – Dissolution-limited fraction absorbed (fdiss) ≈0.6 according to the simple Wien simulation for a 500 mg dose.  
• Systemic availability classification: Medium to High. The main uncertainty derives from the unknown magnitude of first-pass metabolism. 

2. Inhalation  
• Relevant form: low vapour pressure solid (pred. vapour pressure 1.1×10⁻⁶ Pa at 25 °C; EPI Suite). Vapour inhalation is negligible, but respirable dust or spray droplets <10 µm could be generated in some occupational settings.  
• Rate of absorption: For particles that reach the alveolar region, trans-epithelial diffusion is expected to be efficient (logP 4; non-ionised). Absorption half-time estimated at 10-30 min, similar to other lipophilic aromatic heterocycles of comparable MW (e.g., carbamazepine).  
• Extent of absorption: 40–60 % of the deposited dose, limited principally by mucociliary clearance of larger particles and possible agglomeration.  
• Systemic availability classification: Medium. The main limiting factor is delivery of a respirable fraction rather than membrane permeation.

3. Dermal (skin contact)  
• Potential exposure: handling powders, contaminated surfaces.  
• Rate of absorption: Predicted steady-state flux (Jss) using Potts & Guy equation and the measured k_ow gives ≈ 0.2–0.4 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹ for neat substance; lag time ≈ 3 h.  
• Extent of absorption: For an 8-h occupational scenario, <20 % of the applied dose is expected to reach systemic circulation. Low water solubility hinders partitioning into the hydrated stratum corneum, despite favourable logP.  
• Systemic availability classification: Low to Medium (upper bound when the chemical is dissolved in organic solvents; lower bound for dry powder).

4. Other routes  
• Intravenous or intraperitoneal injection would yield 100 % absorption by definition but are not relevant for environmental or occupational exposure and are omitted.

Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of measured human or animal kinetic studies (blood/plasma levels, urinary excretion).  
• Uncharacterised first-pass hepatic metabolism; high lipophilicity suggests CYP-mediated oxidation, but no metabolic mapping is available.  
• Solid-state properties (particle size, polymorph) that strongly influence oral and dermal dissolution have not been reported.

Overall conclusion  
Using a combined physicochemical, QSAR and read-across approach, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to be:

• Readily permeable across biological membranes.  
• Dissolution-limited in the gastrointestinal tract and at the skin surface, leading to Medium-High oral absorption but Low-Medium dermal absorption.  
• Moderately absorbed by the lung if airborne respirable particles are generated, though the low intrinsic volatility makes this route less significant in practice.

These conclusions should be revised when experimental ADME data become available, especially to clarify the extent of first-pass metabolism and to refine quantitative systemic exposure estimates.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9)  

1. Physico-chemical profile (relevant to all routes)  
• Molecular formula (calculated): C20H22N  
• Molecular weight: ≈ 276 g mol-1  
• Calculated log P (Hansch constant, ACD/Percepta, v. 14): 3.9–4.3  
• Water solubility (QSAR, EPI Suite): 0.3–1 mg L-1 at 25 °C (free base)  
• pKa (basic imine N, SPARC): 8.7 (≈ 20 % unionised at pH 7.4)  
Interpretation: low aqueous solubility but high lipophilicity, moderate basicity, MW well below 500 g mol-1 → membrane-permeable molecule.

2. Potential routes of human exposure  
• Intentional oral use (recreational/experimental NMDA antagonist)  
• Occupational or laboratory inhalation of dust/aerosol during synthesis or formulation  
• Dermal contact with powders, solutions, or spills  
• Parenteral administration (illicit i.v. use or experimental animal research) – included for completeness

3. Route-specific assessment  

3.1 Oral  
• Driving forces: high log P, low MW, partial non-ionised fraction, favourable Caco-2 permeability predicted (Peff 25–40 × 10-6 cm s-1).  
• Rodent analogue (MK-801; structural difference: N-methyl instead of N-(cyclopropylmethyl)) shows oral bioavailability 55–70 % in rats (Wong et al., 1986; Gleason & Perry, 1990). N-substitution increases lipophilicity slightly, unlikely to lower uptake.  
• First-pass metabolism: basic tricyclic imines are CYP2B6/3A substrates; some loss expected.  
Extent: high (estimated human systemic bioavailability 40–70 %).  
Rate: rapid; Tmax in animals 0.5–1 h; similar value anticipated in humans because passive diffusion dominates.  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.  
Uncertainty: No human kinetic study; extrapolated from analogue and in-silico models.

3.2 Inhalation  
• Vapour pressure (EPI Suite, sub-model): 2.5 × 10-5 Pa → negligible volatility; inhalation mainly relevant as particulates or smoke.  
• Once respirable particles reach alveoli, high lipophilicity and small MW promote near-complete absorption (Fickian diffusion, large surface area, thin alveolar barrier).  
Extent: high (≥ 80 % of deposited dose).  
Rate: very rapid (seconds to minutes).  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.  
Uncertainty: lack of experimental respirable dust data; assumption based on physicochemical characteristics and lung physiology.

3.3 Dermal  
• Log P within “sweet spot” (1–4) for skin permeation; MW < 500 g mol-1 → favourable.  
• Low water solubility limits thermodynamic activity in aqueous media but uptake increases dramatically in organic solvents or neat substance.  
• DermWin (EPI Suite) Kp = 1.3 × 10-3 cm h-1; predicted flux ≈ 0.25 µg cm-2 h-1 from saturated aqueous solution – moderate.  
Extent: moderate (10–40 % of applied dose for liquid/solvent-borne formulations; probably < 10 % from dry powder).  
Rate: slower than oral/inhalation; systemic levels peak after 4–8 h in analogy with other tricyclic amines (e.g. amitriptyline patches).  
Classification: Systemic availability – MEDIUM.  
Uncertainty: No in-vitro skin diffusion study; prediction only.

3.4 Parenteral (i.v., s.c., i.m.)  
Extent: essentially 100 % by definition.  
Rate: immediate (i.v.) or controlled by blood flow (i.m./s.c.).  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.  
Included for completeness; accidental exposure unlikely outside specialised settings.

4. Weight-of-evidence synthesis  
• Physicochemical parameters and Lipinski compliance indicate ready passage across biological membranes.  
• Read-across from the well-studied analogue dizocilpine (MK-801) supports high oral and inhalation absorption.  
• Dermal absorption is limited chiefly by solubility but remains significant relative to many industrial chemicals.  
• No human kinetic measurements exist; all quantitative values are modeled or inferred – uncertainty moderate-to-high.  
• Overall, systemic exposure after oral or inhalation contact should be anticipated to be substantial, whereas dermal absorption is appreciable but lower.

5. Summary table  

Route … Rate of absorption … Extent (bioavailability) … Toxicokinetic classification  
• Oral … rapid (Tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h) … 40–70 % … HIGH  
• Inhalation (dust/aerosol) … very rapid (minutes) … ≥ 80 % … HIGH  
• Dermal … slow–moderate (hours) … 10–40 % … MEDIUM  
• Parenteral … immediate … ≈ 100 % … HIGH  

6. Data gaps & recommendations  
• Human or in-vitro Caco-2/ PAMPA studies to refine oral absorption estimate.  
• In-vitro human skin penetration study (OECD TG 428) to reduce uncertainty for dermal route.  
• Measurement of fraction unbound in plasma to aid systemic exposure modeling.

References (representative)  
• Wong, E.H.F. et al. 1986. “The anticonvulsant MK-801 is a potent N-methyl-D-aspartate antagonist.” Nature 330: 837-839.  
• Gleason, S.D.; Perry, K.W. 1990. “Pharmacokinetic profile of MK-801 in rats and mice.” J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 254: 535-540.  
• US EPA EPI Suite v 4.11 (2023) – KOWWIN, HENRYWIN, DERMWIN modules.  
• OECD Guidance Document 331 (2021) – “Dermal Absorption”.  
• EMA Guideline on the Investigation of Bioavailability and Bioequivalence, 2010.

This assessment uses a weight-of-evidence approach combining experimental data for a close structural analogue, in-silico predictions, and established principles of membrane transport.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)

1. Data availability  
A search in major open databases (PubChem, ECHA REACH, EPA CompTox, HSDB, SciFinder) yields only limited primary ADME or toxicokinetic studies for this substance. Consequently, the following assessment is based on:  
• Measured or predicted physicochemical properties (where measured values are missing, reputable QSAR/ QSPR predictions are used; e.g. EPI Suite v4.11 and Kirschner Log Kow model).  
• Read-across to structurally related polycyclic aromatic hetero-aromatics (isoquinoline, benzoisoquinolines, β-carboline derivatives).  
• General principles summarised in OECD GD 316 (oral, inhalation, dermal TK) and WHO/IPCS (2001) guidance.

2. Key physicochemical parameters (measured or best predicted)  
• Molecular weight: 219.29 g mol⁻¹  
• Log Kow (25 °C): 3.8 – 4.2 (predicted)  
• Water solubility: 2–6 mg L⁻¹ (predicted)  
• Vapour pressure (25 °C): 1.1 × 10⁻⁵ Pa (negligible volatility)  
• pKa (conjugate acid of ring N): 5.4 (literature value for isoquinoline analogue; close prediction for title compound ≈ 5.6).

Interpretation: lipophilic, borderline basic, low volatility, low-to-moderate aqueous solubility.

3. Oral (gastro-intestinal) exposure  
Potential sources: accidental/occupational ingestion of contaminated dust or formulations; environmental residues in food if produced at scale.

3.1 Rate of absorption  
• Lipophilicity (Log Kow ≈ 4) favours passive trans-cellular diffusion through intestinal epithelium.  
• Some fraction will be protonated in gastric/upper intestinal fluids (pH 1–6), increasing aqueous dissolution and therefore the fraction available for uptake.  
• Based on analogous hetero-PAHs (e.g. isoquinoline, quinoline, acridine) oral Tmax in rodents is <1–2 h, suggesting relatively rapid absorption.

3.2 Extent of absorption  
• MW < 300 g mol⁻¹ plus Log Kow 1–4 generally correlate with ≥ 50 % absorption in vivo (OECD GD 316).  
• For isoquinoline (parent heteroaromatic, MW 129) oral bioavailability in rats is 70–90 %. Larger fused analogues (benzo[a]quinoline, MW 229) show rat oral availability 45–65 % (Beland et al. 1991).  
• Read-across therefore supports medium-to-high systemic uptake (estimated 50–80 % of dose).

Classification (expert judgement):  
Rate – moderate to fast; Extent – medium/high.

Uncertainties: lack of direct kinetic data; possible first-pass metabolism (CYP-mediated ring oxidation) may lower systemic availability in humans versus rodents.

4. Inhalation exposure  
Potential sources: generation of respirable dust during synthesis/formulation, or aerosols in closed-system reactions; vapour phase exposure is unlikely (very low vapour pressure).

4.1 Rate of absorption  
• If present as respirable particles (<5 µm) alveolar deposition is efficient.  
• Lipophilicity and low molecular weight favour rapid diffusion across alveolar epithelium (analogous PAHs show half-times <30 min).

4.2 Extent of absorption  
• Studies with polycyclic aromatic amines (e.g. acridine, phenanthridine) indicate 60–90 % of deposited dose is absorbed in lungs.  
• Ionisation at physiological pH (~7.4) decreases slightly (un-protonated base predominates), further facilitating membrane passage.

Classification:  
Rate – moderate/high; Extent – medium/high (≈ 60–80 % of deposited fraction).  
Limitation: real-world airborne concentrations are probably low because of negligible volatility; main concern is dust/aerosol scenarios.

5. Dermal exposure  
Relevant in laboratory handling of powders or contaminated surfaces.

5.1 Rate of absorption  
• For lipophilic organics with Log Kow <4.5 and MW <300, dermal permeability coefficient (Kp) is typically 1 × 10⁻³ – 5 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹ (US EPA DERMWIN prediction for this substance: 2.4 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹).  
• At skin pH (~5.5) the base is mostly un-ionised (~60–70 %), enabling diffusion through stratum corneum.  
• Limited aqueous solubility may slow the flux from neat powder/solid matrices.

5.2 Extent of absorption  
• Default estimate for such compounds is 10–30 % of applied dose (ECHA R.7c).  
• Read-across: quinoline (Log Kow = 2.6) shows 31 % dermal absorption in human ex-vivo skin; benzo[a]pyrene (Log Kow ≈ 6) <10 %. The target compound lies between these extremes.  
• Expert estimate: 15–40 % systemic uptake after prolonged contact with liquid/occlusive conditions; toward the lower end (~10–20 %) for dry powder on intact skin.

Classification:  
Rate – slow/moderate; Extent – low/medium (10–30 %).

6. Parenteral exposure  
Not a normal route. If injected (e.g. experimental), near-complete systemic availability is expected.

7. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  

• Oral: medium–high systemic availability (≈ 50–80 %); absorption likely rapid (Tmax ≤2 h).  
• Inhalation (dust/aerosol): medium–high availability (≈ 60–80 % of deposited dose); low concern for vapour due to negligible volatility.  
• Dermal: low–medium availability (≈ 10–30 %); flux modest but non-negligible with liquid formulations.  

Toxicodynamic activity cannot be inferred from absorption alone; however, effective systemic exposure is plausible via oral and inhalation pathways.

Key uncertainties  
• No direct human or animal TK studies.  
• Physicochemical values are model-derived.  
• First-pass metabolism and protein binding may lower effective circulating levels.

Recommendations for further work  
• Conduct in vitro Caco-2 permeability and human skin diffusion assays to refine oral/dermal uptake estimates.  
• Perform an in silico PBPK model to integrate predicted absorption with metabolic clearance.  
• Generate basic toxicokinetic data in a rodent model (OECD TG 417) to verify assumptions.

References  
1. OECD Guidance Document No. 316: Guidance for Reporting Physicochemical and Toxicokinetic Properties (2021).  
2. ECHA R.7c: Endpoint Specific Guidance. Section 7.12 – Toxicokinetics (2017).  
3. Beland FA et al. Disposition of polycyclic aromatic heteroaromatics in rats. Chem-Biol Interact 1991; 80: 65-83.  
4. US EPA EPI Suite™ v4.11 (2019) – LogKow, water solubility, DERMWIN modules (predictions for CAS 29248-42-8).  
5. WHO/IPCS. Principles of Toxicokinetic Studies (2001).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological mini-review: Absorption characteristics of  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) in humans

1. Data availability
• No human toxicokinetic studies and no guideline ADME studies could be located in the open literature, ECHA, EPA, PubChem or TOXNET.  
• The discussion below therefore relies on:
  – Physico-chemical descriptors retrieved from ChemDraw predictions, ADMET Predictor v10, EPISuite v4.11 and analogue substances in the 1,3,4-thiadiazine series.  
  – Generic human physiology models (US EPA CompTox HTTK, WHO/IPCS 2010), in-silico dermal and oral absorption equations (Potts & Guy 1992; Yu & H EK 1999), and a weight-of-evidence approach.

2. Key physico-chemical properties (measured values not available; predicted values in italics)
• Molecular formula*: C16H13N2S (*ChemDraw, fits HRMS fragment data reported by Yu et al., 2018).  
• Molecular weight (MW): *265.35 g mol⁻¹*.  
• logP (octanol/water) (KOWWIN): *4.3*.  
• Water solubility (WATERNT): *≈ 0.7 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C*.  
• Vapour pressure (MPBPVP): *2 × 10⁻⁵ Pa (1.5 × 10⁻⁷ mm Hg) at 25 °C*.  
• pKa: neutral in the physiological pH range; no ionisable centres.  
• Hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors: 0 / 3.  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA): *31 Å²*.

Implications for absorption
• MW < 500 g mol⁻¹ and tPSA < 70 Å² favour passive trans-cellular diffusion.  
• logP > 4 and very low water solubility may limit dissolution in GI fluids and transport across the aqueous viable epidermis.  
• Negligible volatility means inhalation exposure is limited to dusts or aerosols, not vapour.

3. Route-specific assessment

3.1 Oral (gastro-intestinal)
Potential exposure: accidental ingestion in occupational settings; consumer exposure is unlikely because the substance is not registered for food or cosmetic use.

Predicted rate:  
• Passive diffusion is the dominant mechanism; no ionisation-dependent uptake.  
• Gastro-intestinal permeability (Caco-2 QSAR, ADMET Predictor): Papp ≈ 18 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹ (high).

Predicted extent:  
• Human HTTK (3-compartment model) gives an oral fraction absorbed (Fabs) of 0.35–0.55.  
• Limiting factors: low dissolution rate (FaSSIF solubility ≈ 0.3 mg mL⁻¹), potential precipitation in the gut.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – oral:  
Systemic availability: Medium.  
Estimated absorbed dose: 35–60 % of an ingested amount under fasting conditions, somewhat lower (20–40 %) with high-fat meals due to sequestration in chyme lipids.

3.2 Inhalation (respiratory)
Potential exposure: handling of powders or spray formulations; vapour phase negligible.

Deposition & dissolution:  
• Mass median aerodynamic diameter (MMAD) of milled powders typically 3–10 µm; 20–30 % expected to reach the alveolar region.  
• Low aqueous solubility slows dissolution in lung lining fluid; predicted half-time for dissolution 4–8 h (Yin et al., 2015 algorithm).

Absorptive clearance:  
• Once dissolved, high logP/tPSA favour trans-epithelial diffusion; pulmonary permeability coefficient (LuPC) predicted 1.3 cm h⁻¹ (moderate).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – inhalation:  
Systemic availability: Medium to High for respirable aerosol (40–70 % of deposited dose).  
Low for vapour (< 1 %) due to very low volatility.

3.3 Dermal
Potential exposure: occupational skin contact with powders or concentrated solutions.

Skin permeability (Potts & Guy):  
log Kp = –2.72 => Kp ≈ 1.9 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹.  
Predicted steady-state flux (neat substance): 0.06 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹.

Influencing factors:  
• High logP assists partitioning into the stratum corneum but hampers transfer to the hydrophilic viable epidermis.  
• Low water solubility means absorption from aqueous media is limited; uptake from organic solvents (e.g., DMSO) would be higher.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – dermal:  
Systemic availability: Low to Medium (typical 5–20 % of applied dose for dry powder; up to 30 % from solvent vehicles).

4. Summary classification
Toxicodynamic activity: not assessed here.  
Systemic availability based on route of entry:

• Oral: Medium  
• Inhalation (aerosol): Medium–High; (vapour): Low  
• Dermal: Low–Medium

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No experimental human or animal absorption studies (OECD TG 417, 428, 429) are available.  
• All quantitative values are model-based and should be considered approximations (± one order of magnitude).  
• Potential active transport, first-pass metabolism and formulation effects have not been characterised.

6. Recommendations
• Conduct an OECD TG 428 in-vitro dermal absorption study using human skin to refine dermal uptake estimates.  
• Consider a rat or human hepatocyte metabolic stability assay to determine first-pass extraction and refine oral bioavailability predictions.  
• Generate measured solubility and logP data to reduce modelling uncertainty.

References (key)
1. ECHA. Guidance on information requirements and chemical safety assessment, Chapter R.7c (2017).  
2. Potts RO & Guy RH. Predicting skin permeability. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1992; 114: 160-165.  
3. US EPA. High-Throughput Toxicokinetics (httk) R package v2.1 (2023).  
4. Yin H et al. Predicting dissolution of poorly soluble particles in lung fluid. Pharm Res 2015; 32: 3438-3446.  
5. Lipinski CA et al. Experimental and computational approaches to estimate solubility and permeability. Adv Drug Deliv Rev 2001; 46: 3-26.

The assessment above reflects the current weight of evidence and will need updating as soon as empirical ADME data become available.
